summary: The tag→build→GHCR-push→SSH-deploy pipeline is unusually mature for a single-VPS SaaS: blocking quality gates (lint/tsc/unit + real-Postgres e2e + contract/i18n drift) run before any image is built, secrets are pre-flighted, version tags are regex-validated against shell injection, env files land atomically (scp→.new→mv), images are pulled as immutable :vX.Y.Z and SHA-verified before the :current pointer moves, deploys are health-GATED (not blind restarts), and an ERR-trap auto-rolls back the image layer. The migration model is genuinely additive (verified across recent migrations) so the run-in-old-container-before-swap ordering is sound for the happy path. However, several real production hazards remain: staging and prod deploys race on a single shared /root/kds checkout with no host-level lock (different concurrency groups), a migration that fails mid-deploy leaves the DB partially migrated with no DB rollback, docker pull has no retry against the very GHCR-propagation race the team has already hit, the disk-prune window can delete the image rollback would need, and a failed deploy/rollback is completely silent (no alerting). These need attention before scaling onto real paying tenants.
strengths: Immutable image identity with a verified pointer swap: images are pulled as :vX.Y.Z, SHA-verified that the running container actually equals the pulled image (scripts/deploy.sh:397-412 verify_running_image), and :current is only retagged after the new container passes health checks (swap_backend / verify_and_promote, deploy.sh:444-539). This avoids the classic 'latest' mutable-tag drift.

Health-GATED container swap rather than restart+sleep: wait_until_healthy polls /api/health with a real budget (deploy.sh:414-442) and, on failure, dumps the last 200 backend log lines + container state for forensics before rolling back.

Quality gates are a hard blocking prerequisite to building any image — release-deploy.yml:160-162 and test-deploy.yml:70-72 both `uses: ./.github/workflows/quality-gates.yml`, which runs backend lint/tsc/unit AND a real-Postgres+Redis e2e job (quality-gates.yml:54-121), frontend, and contract/i18n drift before the build job ever starts.

Shell-injection-safe version handling: workflow_dispatch tag input is piped via env: (not inlined into ${{ }}) and regex-validated `^v[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$` before use (release-deploy.yml:183-204), with the rationale documented inline.

Strong staging/prod credential isolation: every access/decrypt secret has a STAGING_* twin (test-deploy.yml:43-48, 201-226; docs/DEPLOYMENT.md:34-59); staging forces PAYTR_TEST_MODE=1, mock email/SMS, and its own (or no) Google client, so a staging bug can never charge a real card, send real mail, or authenticate against prod.

Pre-flight gate refuses to deploy on a fire: probes prod /api/health via SSH loopback (avoiding Cloudflare WAF false-negatives), asserts all required secrets are present, and verifies SSH before burning build time (release-deploy.yml:41-156).
Verified-and-retained DB backup before every deploy: pg_dump → gzip with gzip -t integrity check, >1KB size floor, and a ≥5 CREATE-statement sanity check (deploy.sh:215-262), with N-day retention.

A conservative migration doctor that NEVER auto-marks migrations --applied and only auto-recovers a single failed migration whose SQL is provably idempotent, refusing P3005/drift/multi-failure for human judgement (db-migration-doctor.sh:1-310); auto-baseline is gated behind a `migrate diff --exit-code` proof that live schema == datamodel.

Production deploys queue in order (concurrency group production-deploy, cancel-in-progress:false) while staging is allowed to cancel in-flight (cancel-in-progress:true) — the correct asymmetry (release-deploy.yml:23-25, test-deploy.yml:20-22).

findings.title: Staging and production deploys race on a single shared /root/kds git checkout with no lock
findings.verify_reasoning:
The structural claim is TRUE on main. Both pipelines deploy from the same /root/kds checkout on the same box (test-deploy.yml:302-318 `cd /root/kds; git stash --include-untracked; git fetch origin test; git reset --hard origin/test; ./scripts/deploy.sh staging $SHA`; release-deploy.yml:470-487 `cd /root/kds; git stash; git fetch --all --tags --force; git reset --hard $VERSION; ./scripts/deploy.sh prod $VERSION`). They use DIFFERENT concurrency groups (staging-deploy cancel-in-progress:true at test-deploy.yml:20-21; production-deploy cancel-in-progress:false at release-deploy.yml:23-24), so GitHub serializes each environment independently — a `test` push CAN run concurrently with a `vX.Y.Z` tag. There is NO host-level mutex: `grep flock` over scripts/deploy.sh and scripts/db-migration-doctor.sh returns nothing (exit 1); the only /var/lib/kds-deploy usage is the STATE_DIR for image snapshots, not a lock. deploy.sh derives PROJECT_ROOT from its own location (:28-29) and reads COMPOSE_FILE/ENV_FILE (:104-105,:138-139) and passes $PROJECT_ROOT/backend to the doctor (:362-363) from the shared tree at runtime. So a mid-flight `git reset --hard` by the other env genuinely can rewrite files the in-progress deploy is actively reading.

BUT the finder's headline worst case — "the migration doctor could apply test-branch migrations to the PROD database" — is OVERSTATED. I opened db-migration-doctor.sh: the real prisma operations (migrate diff :100-102, migrate status :148, migrate deploy :288, migrate resolve) all run via `docker exec $BACKEND_CONTAINER ... cd /app` against the migrations BAKED INTO the env-specific container image (kds_backend_prod vs kds_backend_staging), not the host git tree. The host $MIGRATIONS_DIR (:38) is used only for a dir-existence check (:46) and to cat individual migration.sql files for operator display (:199). So the migrations actually applied to prod come from the prod image, not from a staging git-reset — the "test migrations land in prod DB" data-corruption path is largely refuted. Likewise COMPOSE_FILE/ENV_FILE are env-DISTINCT filenames (docker-compose.prod.yml/.env.production vs docker-compose.staging.yml/.env.test), so a staging reset never makes prod read the staging compose; it can only transiently change the CONTENTS of the prod files if they differ between the `test` branch and the release tag.

The residual REAL risk: (1) bash reads deploy.sh and db-migration-doctor.sh line-by-line during execution — a concurrent `git reset --hard` rewriting those files mid-run can make bash execute shifted/garbage lines (classic self-modifying-script footgun) → unpredictable mid-deploy abort; (2) compose/env file CONTENT can be momentarily inconsistent with the ref the deploy expects. Both require a narrow overlap window and overwhelmingly produce a FAILED/aborted deploy (which has rollback + health-gate verify_and_promote) rather than a silent, undetected prod data-corruption. That is a real correctness/availability hazard worth fixing (shared concurrency group or flock or per-env checkouts, exactly as recommended), but not a blocker-grade silent-data-loss path. Downgrade to high.
findings.evidence: Both pipelines deploy from the SAME path on the SAME box: test-deploy.yml:303-317 does `cd /root/kds; git stash --include-untracked; git fetch origin test; git reset --hard origin/test; ./scripts/deploy.sh staging` and release-deploy.yml:471-486 does `cd /root/kds; git stash; git fetch --all --tags --force; git reset --hard ${VERSION}; ./scripts/deploy.sh prod`. They use DIFFERENT GitHub concurrency groups (`staging-deploy` vs `production-deploy`, test-deploy.yml:21 / release-deploy.yml:24), so GitHub serializes them independently and a `test` push can run concurrently with a `vX.Y.Z` tag. deploy.sh then reads the compose file, prisma/migrations dir, and db-migration-doctor.sh FROM that shared working tree at runtime (deploy.sh:104/138 COMPOSE_FILE, :362-363 passes $PROJECT_ROOT/backend to the doctor). `grep flock|lockfile` over scripts/deploy.sh and all workflows returns nothing — there is no host-level mutex.
findings.why_it_matters: If a staging deploy runs `git reset --hard origin/test` while a prod deploy is mid-flight, the prod run can suddenly see staging's migration files, staging's docker-compose, or a staging-rewritten deploy.sh — the migration doctor could apply test-branch migrations to the PROD database, or prod could swap to images using a staging compose. This is a silent data-corruption / wrong-version-shipped path that is invisible until it bites, exactly when release cadence is highest.
findings.recommendation: Add a host-level mutex around the whole server-side deploy. Either give both workflows the SAME concurrency group (so GitHub serializes staging+prod), or wrap the SSH body in `flock -n /var/lib/kds-deploy/deploy.lock -c '...'` so a second deploy waits/aborts instead of stomping the shared tree. Better: give each environment its own checkout (/root/kds-prod, /root/kds-staging) so the working trees never collide.

findings.title: A migration that fails mid-deploy leaves the DB partially migrated with no DB rollback
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every technical claim is accurate on current main. The CI-invoked deployer is scripts/deploy.sh (release-deploy.yml:485 prod, :549 prod-rollback), NOT the root deploy.sh (which is dead legacy: it uses `git checkout HEAD~1` and looks for uncompressed `backup_${ENVIRONMENT}_*.sql`, incompatible with the new `.sql.gz` naming — its psql-restore path is never wired anywhere in CI). In scripts/deploy.sh: `set -euo pipefail` (line 26); `step()` runs the command unguarded (lines 42-47) so a non-zero exit propagates; run_deploy step 7 calls run_migrations_in_existing_backend → `docker exec ... prisma migrate deploy` (line 387) inside the OLD container BEFORE swap_backend (step 8). A non-zero migrate exit fires the ERR trap → on_failure (677-685) → restore_image_ids (580-626), which ONLY re-pins :current image tags + `dc up -d --force-recreate` and explicitly warns (623-625) "image state is at previous SHA, DB state is forward ... manual DB intervention is required." run_rollback (670-674) is also image-only. So there is NO automated DB restore on a failed migration; confirmed.

The next-deploy block is real: db-migration-doctor.sh auto-recovers a single failed migration ONLY if its SQL passes conservative idempotency checks (no bare CREATE TABLE/INDEX, no DROP/UPDATE/INSERT/DELETE/ALTER-type/CHECK unless an `-- @doctor:idempotent` marker is present) — lines 197-291; otherwise it exits 1 with a manual `prisma migrate resolve` runbook (270-283), and >1 failed migration always blocks (190-195). So a non-idempotent partial migration wedges subsequent deploys until a human reconciles via psql.

Mitigations the finder already accounts for, which cap the real-world severity below "critical": (1) a VERIFIED pg_dump backup is taken at step 2 BEFORE migration (215-262: gzip -t, >1KB, ≥5 CREATE stmts, known path) — so data IS recoverable, just manually and with loss of writes since deploy start; (2) the documented additive-only migration policy (header lines 19-22) means the rolled-back OLD image generally keeps serving against the partially-additive schema, so the common failure mode is a stalled pipeline + degraded next-deploy rather than an immediate tenant-down or data-loss event. The genuine gaps — no automated DB rollback, on_failure never prints the backup file or a one-command restore, and a non-idempotent partial apply forces manual psql reconciliation while tenants are live — are all real and worth fixing (the recommendation is sound), but the immediate blast radius is a recoverable, backup-protected operational outage, which is High, not Critical. No additional mitigation exists elsewhere (no transaction wrapping, --single-transaction, or restore helper anywhere in scripts/deploy.sh or the doctor).
findings.evidence: run_deploy applies `prisma migrate deploy` inside the OLD running backend BEFORE any image swap (deploy.sh:652 run_migrations_in_existing_backend → docker exec ... prisma migrate deploy, deploy.sh:387). If that exec returns non-zero (e.g. a multi-statement migration that creates a column then fails on an index, or a transient DB hiccup), `set -euo pipefail` fires the ERR trap → on_failure → restore_image_ids (deploy.sh:677-685). restore_image_ids ONLY re-pins image tags and recreates containers (deploy.sh:580-626); there is no pg_restore. The script itself documents this: deploy.sh:623-625 warns `image state is at previous SHA, DB state is forward ... manual DB intervention is required`. The prod-rollback action confirmed to contain no DB restore.
findings.why_it_matters: The old image is now running against a half-applied schema with a FAILED row in _prisma_migrations. The next deploy's doctor will see a failed migration (and only auto-recovers it if it is provably idempotent — db-migration-doctor.sh:197-291), otherwise it blocks. Meanwhile real tenants are live on a database that is neither the old nor the new schema. The verified backup exists (deploy.sh:215-262) but restoring it is a fully manual, undocumented-in-the-rollback-path operation that also discards any tenant writes since the deploy started.
findings.recommendation: Wrap each migration in a transaction where possible, and on migrate-deploy failure capture the exact failed migration name and surface a one-command restore (the backup path is already known). Document the DB-rollback runbook and make on_failure print the precise `pg_restore`/`gunzip|psql` command with the backup file it just created. Long-term, prefer expand/contract migrations validated in staging against a prod-shaped dataset so a failed apply is recoverable forward.

findings.title: docker pull has no retry — the known transient GHCR-propagation race aborts the whole deploy
findings.verify_reasoning:
Technically TRUE on main. The function is in scripts/deploy.sh (finder wrote "deploy.sh" — the repo root deploy.sh is an unrelated stale git-pull script, but the cited function name `pull_versioned_images`, the line range 283-294, and the exact code `docker pull "$img:$VERSION"` in a 5-image loop all match scripts/deploy.sh:283-294, so the substance is correct). The file runs `set -euo pipefail` (line 26) with `trap on_failure ERR` installed for deploys (line 696). pull_versioned_images is invoked via `step "4/10 Pull new images"` (line 649); step() (line 42) calls `"$@"` with no error suppression, so a single non-zero `docker pull` propagates to the ERR trap → on_failure → restore_image_ids (rollback). There is NO retry/backoff around docker pull anywhere; the only retry primitives are wait_until_healthy (line 414, health polling) and a pre-deploy liveness probe in release-deploy.yml:68-76 (probes the EXISTING backend, not the pull). I confirmed no mitigation exists in the workflow or the script. So the core claim — a transient GHCR pull blip aborts the whole deploy and fires the rollback path — is real.

WHY OVERSTATED to medium, not high: I traced the deploy ordering and the rollback body. Pull is step 4/10; migrations are steps 6-7 and the container swap is steps 8-9. So at the moment a pull fails, prod is STILL on the old, running, healthy containers — nothing has been swapped and the DB is untouched. restore_image_ids re-tags :current to the already-current prior SHAs and `dc up -d --force-recreate` the 5 app containers. Net real-user impact: (1) a healthy release fails to ship and prod stays on the prior good version (the documented manual remedy is to re-run the idempotent deploy job — annoying, not dangerous), and (2) the rollback unnecessarily force-recreates healthy prod containers, causing a brief restart blip even though prod was fine. There is no data loss and no sustained outage — the "DB forward / image back incompatibility" warning in restore_image_ids does NOT apply here because no migration has run at step 4. The lack of alerting the finder references is real but notify_failure() IS called in on_failure (line 683), so an operator is paged on the failure (mitigating the "operator may not notice" claim). Recommendation (bounded retry+backoff mirroring wait_until_healthy) is sound and low-risk. Net: a real reliability/flakiness gap with a documented manual workaround, no data risk, prod self-heals to prior version → medium.
findings.evidence: pull_versioned_images loops `docker pull $img:$VERSION` for all five images with no retry/backoff (deploy.sh:283-294); a single non-zero pull under `set -euo pipefail` fires the ERR trap and rolls back. `grep retry|attempt|until|propagat` over deploy.sh shows retry logic exists only in wait_until_healthy, not around docker pull. The project's own memory and release-deploy.yml comments acknowledge a 'transient GHCR-propagation race on swap-verify' where a freshly pushed image isn't immediately visible.
findings.why_it_matters: GHCR occasionally 404s/503s a layer for a few seconds right after push. A healthy, fully-built release then fails to deploy and triggers an unnecessary rollback — and because there is no alerting (see separate finding), the operator may not notice prod stayed on the old version. The documented mitigation today is 'manually re-run the failed deploy job', which is a manual footgun under load.
findings.recommendation: Add a bounded retry with backoff around `docker pull` (e.g. 5 attempts, 10s sleep) inside pull_versioned_images, mirroring the wait_until_healthy pattern already in the file. This turns a transient registry blip into a few extra seconds instead of a rollback.

findings.title: Disk-prune window can delete the very image the rollback needs
findings.verify_reasoning:
The core claim — that the disk prune can delete "the very image the rollback needs" — does not hold against the actual rollback design.

WHAT THE FINDER GOT RIGHT (mechanics): release-deploy.yml:525-533 runs `docker image prune -af --filter "until=168h"`, gated `if: success()` (line 526). deploy.sh:264-281 snapshots prior container SHAs into STATE_FILE; restore_image_ids (deploy.sh:580-626) rolls back by `docker tag <PREV_IMAGE_SHA> $IMG:current`. Those lines are accurate.

WHY THE RISK IS REFUTED:
1. The auto-rollback target is NOT "a version older than 7 days." STATE_FILE (`last-images-${ENV}.env`, deploy.sh:181) is truncated and rewritten at the START of every deploy (`: > "$STATE_FILE"`, line 265) with the SHA of the CURRENTLY-RUNNING container (`docker inspect <container> --format '{{.Image}}'`, line 271). So the rollback only ever restores the image that was live immediately before the current attempt — a single step back, never an arbitrarily old release.

2. That target image is prune-immune. `docker image prune -af` never removes an image that is referenced by an existing container. Between a successful deploy N and the next attempt N+1, image N is the running `:current` container — it cannot be pruned no matter how old (168h filter is moot for in-use images). The prune step is also gated `if: success()` and therefore never runs during a failed deploy that is rolling back; the failure path (deploy.sh on_failure → restore_image_ids, and release-deploy.yml:535-550 → prod-rollback) executes while image N is still present.

3. The only moment image N becomes prunable is AFTER a subsequent SUCCESSFUL deploy replaces it as the running container — at which point the auto-mechanism would never target N anyway (it would target the new running image). The finder's failure scenario requires a manual, multi-step rollback to a superseded old version, which this auto-rollback path does not attempt.

So the described degradation ("Snapshot is empty / retag failed" during an incident) cannot arise from age-based pruning of the auto-rollback target. The recommendation to re-pull the immutable `:vX.Y.Z` from GHCR if a local SHA is missing is a reasonable defense-in-depth for MANUAL/older rollbacks, but it does not correspond to a real auto-rollback defect. Residual nuance: a manual operator wanting to revert two+ releases back could find an old image reaped — that is an INFO-level operability note, not a high-severity rollback-safety hole.
findings.evidence: After a successful deploy the workflow runs `docker image prune -af --filter "until=168h"` (release-deploy.yml:525-533). restore_image_ids rolls back by `docker tag <PREV_IMAGE_SHA> $IMG:current` using SHAs snapshotted at deploy start (deploy.sh:264-281, 580-617). `prune -af` removes any image not used by a running container regardless of whether its SHA is recorded in the rollback state file; a previously-deployed :vX.Y.Z image older than 7 days that is not currently :current is eligible for reaping.
findings.why_it_matters: If a deploy succeeds, the prior version's image becomes 'not current' and, once it ages past 168h, is pruned. A later failed deploy that needs to roll back to that version will find the SHA gone, so `docker tag <missing-sha>` fails — the auto-rollback degrades to 'Snapshot is empty / retag failed', i.e. manual recovery during an incident. Rollback is only reliable to the immediately-prior release within a 7-day window, which is tighter than operators will assume.
findings.recommendation: Prune by 'keep last N release tags' rather than by age, OR exclude images referenced in /var/lib/kds-deploy/last-images-*.env from the prune. At minimum widen the window and make restore_image_ids re-pull the target :vX.Y.Z from GHCR if the local SHA is missing (the immutable tag still exists in the registry).

findings.title: Compose environment: block overrides the rendered .env, silently dropping the DB connection-pool tuning
findings.evidence: CI renders DATABASE_URL with explicit pool params: `...restaurant_pos_prod?connection_limit=25&pool_timeout=10` (release-deploy.yml:368). But docker-compose.prod.yml:90 sets `DATABASE_URL: postgresql://${POSTGRES_USER}:${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}@postgres:5432/${POSTGRES_DB}` with NO query string in the service `environment:` block. Docker Compose precedence is `environment:` > `env_file:`, so the compose value wins and the connection_limit/pool_timeout the pipeline carefully sets are discarded at runtime. (REDIS_URL is also doubly-defined — env_file db0 no-password vs compose db2 with-password at :91 — compose wins there too, which happens to be the intended value, but it shows the rendered REDIS_URL line is dead.)
findings.why_it_matters: Prisma then falls back to its default pool size (num_cpus*2+1), not the intended 25, and no pool_timeout — under real multi-tenant load this changes Postgres connection behavior from what was tuned and tested, and the team believes they shipped a 25-connection pool when they did not. It also means env edits made via the rendered file for these keys are no-ops, a confusing maintenance trap.
findings.recommendation: Pick ONE source of truth for DATABASE_URL/REDIS_URL. Either move the full tuned URL (with ?connection_limit=25&pool_timeout=10) into docker-compose.prod.yml's environment: block, or delete the DATABASE_URL/REDIS_URL lines from the compose environment: and let the rendered env_file provide them.

findings.title: No alerting on deploy or rollback failure — failures are silent
findings.evidence: notify_failure() is an explicit no-op stub (deploy.sh:628-638: `: # ...` with the real implementation commented out). The only failure signal is the GitHub Actions run going red. There is no Slack/PagerDuty/email on a failed prod deploy or, more importantly, on a failed auto-rollback (which leaves prod in an indeterminate state). The post-deploy SSL guard and perf smoke are warn-only (release-deploy.yml:496-523).
findings.why_it_matters: When onboarding real paying tenants, a failed deploy that rolled back (or worse, a rollback that itself failed leaving prod down) must page a human immediately. Relying on someone happening to watch the Actions tab is not an on-call posture; a failed rollback can mean an extended outage no one is alerted to.
findings.recommendation: Wire notify_failure to a real channel (the scaffold is already there) and fire it from on_failure AND when restore_image_ids reports 'Snapshot empty / manual recovery required'. Also alert on the workflow `deploy` job failure at the GitHub level (e.g. a job with `if: failure()` posting to Slack).

findings.title: Legacy root deploy.sh is an interactive, DB-destroying footgun still present in the repo
findings.evidence: The repo root /home/tarik/Projects/kds/deploy.sh is the OLD deployer: it targets /opt/kds (deploy.sh:18, a path that doesn't match the real /root/kds), rolls back via `git checkout HEAD~1` (deploy.sh:221), `docker-compose ... down` then restores a backup by piping into psql (deploy.sh:215-243), and prompts with interactive `read -p` confirmations (deploy.sh:139, 209, 227). It is distinct from the canonical scripts/deploy.sh. scripts/deploy-production.sh is a clearly-marked deprecated shim that correctly forwards to scripts/deploy.sh.
findings.why_it_matters: An operator with SSH muscle memory could run the wrong script during an incident — `./deploy.sh production rollback` from the root would `docker-compose down` (full outage) and offer to overwrite the prod DB from a backup, on a path that no longer exists. It is dead but dangerous code sitting next to the real entrypoint.
findings.recommendation: Delete the root deploy.sh (or convert it to a one-line shim that execs scripts/deploy.sh like deploy-production.sh does), so there is exactly one production deploy entrypoint.

findings.title: Public-URL health probes inside verify_and_promote can roll back an already-live deploy on a Cloudflare/DNS blip
findings.evidence: verify_and_promote runs FATAL public probes `wait_until_healthy $API_PUBLIC_URL 60` and `$FRONTEND_PUBLIC_URL 60` (deploy.sh:489-490) AFTER the container is already swapped and locally healthy and :current has been moved. A non-200 there returns 1 → ERR trap → restore_image_ids. Only the landing/developer/help probes are made non-fatal (deploy.sh:493-508). The SSL check inside the same function also returns 1 (rolls back) at <=3 days (deploy.sh:518-519).
findings.why_it_matters: The new image is verified healthy on loopback and :current is already promoted before these run; a transient Cloudflare 5xx/edge cache miss or a brief DNS/SSL anomaly on the public hostname would then roll the GOOD deploy back to the old image — an unnecessary, self-inflicted flip during exactly the moment the public edge is least stable. The team already learned Cloudflare WAF blocks runner IPs (hence the loopback gate); the public edge can fail the deploy-box probe too.
findings.recommendation: Make the public API/frontend probes retry-with-backoff and, since local health + :current promotion already passed, treat a persistent public-probe failure as a loud WARNING/alert rather than an automatic image rollback (a public-edge problem is not fixed by reverting the app image). Keep the local loopback probe as the authoritative rollback gate.

findings.title: Backend boot budget (240s) plus cold-path migrations widens the single-instance availability gap during swap
findings.evidence: There is exactly one backend container (no replicas — confirmed: grep replicas/scale in both compose files returns nothing; CMD is `node dist/main`). swap_backend does `dc up -d --force-recreate backend` (deploy.sh:446) which stops the old container and starts the new one; the health budget is HEALTH_BUDGET_SEC=300 and the compose start_period is 240s (docker-compose.prod.yml:144) because boot now runs hundreds of route registrations + onModuleInit DB reads (deploy.sh:168-176 comments). swap_backend then ALSO runs the doctor + `prisma migrate deploy` again post-swap (deploy.sh:456-461).
findings.why_it_matters: With a single replica, --force-recreate means the API is down from container stop until the new one passes health — potentially 1-4 minutes per deploy given the documented cold-boot time. For a POS/KDS system taking live orders this is a noticeable per-deploy outage window, and it grows as the app boots slower. It is not data-unsafe, but it caps how casually releases can ship once tenants are live.
findings.recommendation: Consider a second backend replica behind nginx with rolling swap (start new, health-check, then drain old) to make deploys zero-downtime, or at minimum trim cold-boot work (lazy onModuleInit reads) so the swap gap shrinks. Track the actual stop→healthy duration as a deploy metric.

summary: The container setup is functional and shows real operational maturity in places: multi-stage builds, non-root runtime for the backend and Next.js apps, meaningful compose-level healthchecks with carefully-tuned start_periods, restart:always, loopback-bound datastore ports, and a thoughtful :vX.Y.Z→:current immutable-tag + SHA-verify deploy/rollback flow. However, the production runtime is materially under-hardened versus its own staging: docker-compose.prod.yml sets ZERO CPU/memory limits while staging caps every service, and all services (backend, postgres, redis) are co-located on one VPS with no Node heap cap — so a single backend leak/spike can OOM the shared database for every tenant. The backend image is 2.96GB on full-Debian node:20 with a non-deterministic dependency prune (npm install in the build stage, deprecated --only=production leaving typescript/prisma behind). No container drops capabilities, uses read-only rootfs, or sets no-new-privileges, and the monitoring stack exposes Prometheus/Alertmanager to all interfaces. None of these are aspirational gaps — they are concrete and will bite under real multi-tenant load.
strengths: Backend runs as non-root: backend/Dockerfile:70-72 chowns storage/uploads/logs to node and sets USER node (confirmed at runtime as uid=1000 node in the built image).

Next.js apps (landing/developer/help) run as a dedicated non-root user: landing/Dockerfile:40-47 creates nextjs:nodejs (uid 1001) and switches USER nextjs; uses Next standalone output for a slim runtime.

Genuine multi-stage builds: backend separates a development/build stage from a slim-ish production stage that installs only runtime native libs (backend/Dockerfile:42-50) and COPYs only dist/prisma/templates; frontend ships only static assets into nginx:alpine (frontend/Dockerfile:57-63).

Meaningful, durable healthchecks at the compose layer with well-reasoned tuning: backend uses /api/health (the stable contract that survives rollback to older images) with a 240s start_period sized to the real cold-boot path (docker-compose.prod.yml:132-144); datastores use pg_isready / redis-cli ping with start_period.
Datastore ports are correctly bound to loopback only: postgres 127.0.0.1:5432 (docker-compose.prod.yml:27) and staging redis/postgres on 127.0.0.1 — not publicly reachable.

Base images are tag-pinned and the rationale is documented: postgres:15-alpine / redis:7-alpine are deliberately major-pinned to avoid volume-format breaks (docker-compose.prod.yml:10-15,42-44); Prometheus/Alertmanager pinned to exact versions (v2.53.0/v0.27.0).

Strong deploy/rollback discipline: scripts/deploy.sh pulls the immutable :vX.Y.Z tag, verifies the running container image SHA matches, then atomically swaps the :current pointer and proves :current==:version (deploy.sh:397-538), with auto-rollback that re-pins the previous image.

.dockerignore for backend and frontend correctly excludes node_modules, .git, dist, and all .env / .env.* (keeping .env.example), preventing host secrets and the git tree from entering the build context (backend/.dockerignore:1-27).
restart: always on all prod services and service_healthy depends_on gating ensures the backend won't start before DB/Redis are actually ready (docker-compose.prod.yml:75-79).

findings.title: Production compose sets NO memory/CPU limits while all services share one VPS — a backend leak can OOM the shared database for every tenant
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is TRUE on main. docker-compose.prod.yml (version '3.8') defines postgres (postgres:15-alpine), redis (redis:7-alpine) and the NestJS backend all co-located on a single host sharing one bridge network (kds_network), with restart: always, and NONE of them carry any deploy/resources/mem_limit/cpus — a grep for those keys returns nothing. By contrast docker-compose.staging.yml caps memory+cpu on all 7 services (postgres 2G, backend 1G, etc., lines 28-244). There is no NODE_OPTIONS/--max-old-space-size set anywhere (compose env blocks, backend/Dockerfile, env templates all swept — none). So a backend leak, large PDF/export, or load spike can grow unbounded; the Linux OOM-killer then targets the largest RSS (often postgres), taking the DB down for every tenant — the multi-tenant blast-radius the finder describes is real, and there is NO mitigation elsewhere (no cgroup limit, no heap cap, no separate DB host).

Two nuances justify downgrading critical -> high rather than confirming critical outright:
(1) The finder MISSED that prod is deployed via plain `docker-compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d` (deploy.sh:170), NOT docker swarm/stack. Under non-swarm Compose, the `deploy.resources.limits` syntax that staging uses is IGNORED — Compose only honors `deploy` in swarm mode; outside swarm it needs the top-level `mem_limit`/`cpus` keys (or `--compatibility`). So staging's caps are themselves largely no-ops, and the finder's recommendation to "mirror staging" would be partially ineffective. The correct fix is compose-native `mem_limit`/`cpus`.
(2) This is a latent reliability/availability risk (a leak/spike must occur to trigger it), not an actively-exploitable vuln or a guaranteed-failure-on-deploy bug. Severity for a real-user prod multi-tenant SaaS on a shared VPS is genuinely high — an unbounded backend can crater the shared DB for all tenants — but "critical" overstates it slightly since it requires a triggering condition and no exploit/active outage is demonstrated. high is the accurate rating.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.prod.yml has zero deploy/resources/mem_limit/cpus (grep count of 'deploy:' = 0; confirmed 'PROD: zero resource limits'), yet docker-compose.staging.yml:28-36,61-68,117-124 caps every service. deploy.sh:156 confirms all containers run 'side-by-side on the shared VPS'. No NODE_OPTIONS/--max-old-space-size is set for the backend anywhere (grep across prod compose + Dockerfile + env templates returned NONE). Backend image is 2.96GB and pulls heavy deps (sharp, pdfkit, @sentry/profiling).
findings.why_it_matters: On a single VPS with postgres, redis and the Node backend co-located and no cgroup memory limit, a backend memory leak, a large PDF/export job, or a multi-tenant load spike consumes host RAM unbounded. The Linux OOM-killer then targets the largest RSS process — frequently postgres or redis — taking the database down for ALL tenants, not just the offending request. Without limits, docker also reports OOMKilled=false, hiding the root cause during incident triage.
findings.recommendation: Add deploy.resources.limits (memory + cpus) to every prod service mirroring staging, sizing the backend below host RAM minus postgres+redis reservations, and set a Node heap cap (NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size) safely under the container memory limit so V8 GCs before the cgroup kills the process. Reserve memory for postgres/redis so they win OOM contention.

findings.title: Backend production image is non-deterministically built (npm install, not npm ci) and the dependency prune is the deprecated --only=production, leaving devDeps in the runtime image
findings.verify_reasoning:
The factual core is TRUE but the severity is overstated and one key mechanism is mischaracterized.

VERIFIED TRUE:
1. backend/Dockerfile:20 = `RUN npm install` in the `development`/build stage (where `npx prisma generate` (L25) + `npm run build` (L28) run). Not `npm ci`, so a stale lockfile could be silently mutated / `^` ranges bumped — a real but partial reproducibility concern. Note package*.json IS copied (L19) so the lockfile is present; within a current lockfile, npm install is largely deterministic, so the "two builds differ" risk is real only when the lockfile is out of sync.
2. backend/Dockerfile:55 = `npm ci --only=production`. I confirmed `--only=production` is deprecated: npm 11.8.0 emits `npm warn config only Use --omit=dev`. Still functional today.
3. docker-compose.prod.yml:72 builds `target: production`, and the finder's cited prisma calls DO exist — but in scripts/deploy.sh:387 and :461 (the 700-line real deploy script), NOT the root deploy.sh (330 lines, prisma at :178 without --no-install). Both 387/461 run `docker exec ... npx --no-install prisma migrate deploy`, so production migrations genuinely depend on the `prisma` CLI surviving the prune.

MISCHARACTERIZED (the nuance that drops severity): the finder calls the prune "partial and graph-dependent, not clean... happens to leave prisma in place." That is wrong. I inspected node_modules/@prisma/client/package.json: it declares `peerDependencies: {prisma:"*", typescript:">=5.1.0"}`. prisma and typescript survive `--omit=dev` precisely because they are PEER DEPENDENCIES of the production dependency @prisma/client — a DETERMINISTIC outcome, not luck. `npm ls` confirms prisma is deduped under @prisma/client. jest/@nestjs/cli are absent because they are pulled only by devDeps (ts-jest, jest-mock-extended). So the migration tool's presence is guaranteed as long as @prisma/client stays a runtime dep — it is not an "accident of pruning." The recommendation to move prisma to dependencies is still reasonable hygiene, but the "future npm drops --only=production → migrations silently break" scenario is doubly speculative (needs a future npm major to drop a deprecated-but-working flag AND prisma to stop being a transitive peer).

SEVERITY: This is a build-hygiene / maintainability finding. No exploit, no data exposure, no auth/tenant-isolation impact. typescript (~23MB) in the runtime is minor attack-surface/image-size bloat, not a vulnerability. "high" is overstated for a real-user production context; this is a low-severity hardening item. Recommendation (npm ci in both stages, --omit=dev, make prisma's runtime presence explicit) is sound and worth doing, just not high priority.
findings.evidence: backend/Dockerfile:20 uses 'RUN npm install' in the development/build stage (where prisma generate + npm run build actually run) — not npm ci, so the lockfile does not pin the build. backend/Dockerfile:55 uses 'npm ci --only=production' (deprecated in npm 10.8.2 shipped by node:20). Runtime check of the built image showed typescript PRESENT and prisma PRESENT (both devDependencies, package.json:112) while jest and @nestjs/cli were absent — i.e. the prune is partial and graph-dependent, not clean.
findings.why_it_matters: npm install resolves versions afresh, so two builds of the same tag can differ — undermining the immutable-tag guarantee the deploy flow relies on. The deprecated --only=production yields inconsistent pruning: it happens to leave the prisma CLI in place (which deploy.sh:387,461 'npx --no-install prisma migrate deploy' depends on), so a future npm bump that finally drops --only=production support would silently break production migrations during a deploy. It also leaks build-only deps (typescript, ~23MB) into the runtime attack surface.
findings.recommendation: Use 'npm ci' (lockfile-pinned) in the build stage and 'npm ci --omit=dev' in the production stage. Because deploy.sh needs the prisma CLI at runtime, either move 'prisma' to dependencies or bundle 'prisma migrate deploy' into a container entrypoint that runs before --omit=dev pruning is assumed — make the migration tool's presence explicit, not an accident of pruning.

findings.title: No container is hardened: no cap_drop, no security_opt no-new-privileges, no read-only rootfs anywhere in prod or staging
findings.verify_reasoning:
Technical claims are all factually accurate on current main. (1) docker-compose.prod.yml and docker-compose.staging.yml contain ZERO security_opt/cap_drop/cap_add/read_only/no-new-privileges directives — grep returns nothing in either file; staging hardening-directive count = 0. So every prod/staging service (postgres, redis, backend, frontend, landing, developer, help) runs with the full default Linux capability set and a writable root filesystem. (2) frontend/Dockerfile production stage (line 57 FROM nginx:alpine ... line 67 CMD nginx -g daemon off) has NO USER directive → nginx master runs as root, as claimed. (3) docker-compose.monitoring.yml line 73 alertmanager `user: root` confirmed — and note line 29 prometheus is ALSO `user: root` (finder under-stated slightly). No mitigation exists anywhere: a repo-wide grep for no-new-privileges/cap_drop across all yml/yaml returns nothing. There is no compose override, no daemon-level default config, no AppArmor/seccomp profile in the tree.

Two nuances that pull the severity DOWN from the finder's "high": (a) The backend image — the highest-value target handling payment data — DOES drop to non-root: backend/Dockerfile line 70 chown -R node:node, line 72 USER node, plus its writes are already isolated to the /app/storage and /app/uploads named volumes (compose lines 129-131). The finder correctly acknowledged this in the recommendation, so the worst-case "Node RCE writes anywhere as root" is partly already constrained for that one service. (b) This is a missing defense-in-depth / hardening layer, NOT an independently exploitable vulnerability. The stated impact is explicitly conditional ("IF an RCE/escape primitive exists in Node/nginx/Next, the blast radius is wider"). No such primitive is identified. That is the canonical profile of a MEDIUM missing-hardening finding, not high — there is no direct, current attacker-reachable exploit, only amplified consequences of a hypothetical second bug. The monitoring user:root is real but lives in a separate internally-networked ops stack (kds-monitoring), lowering its individual weight.

Net: the finding is real, correctly evidenced, and the recommendation is sound and standard. But "high" is over-stated for a production context given (a) the crown-jewel backend already runs non-root with isolated writable volumes and (b) the impact is purely conditional defense-in-depth. Medium is the accurate calibration; I keep verdict=confirmed because the underlying technical claim is fully true and the gap is genuinely worth fixing.
findings.evidence: grep of docker-compose.prod.yml and docker-compose.staging.yml for cap_drop/cap_add/security_opt/read_only/no-new-privileges returns nothing. Every container therefore runs with the full default Linux capability set and a writable root filesystem. The frontend nginx:alpine has no USER directive (frontend/Dockerfile) so its master process runs as root, and docker-compose.monitoring.yml:73 runs alertmanager as 'user: root'.
findings.why_it_matters: A remote-code-execution or container-escape primitive in the Node backend, nginx, or a Next app starts with far more privilege than needed (e.g. NET_RAW, SETUID, the ability to write anywhere in the rootfs and install tools). For a multi-tenant SaaS handling payment data this materially widens the blast radius of any app-level vuln and removes a cheap, standard defense-in-depth layer.
findings.recommendation: Add 'security_opt: [no-new-privileges:true]' and 'cap_drop: [ALL]' (re-adding only what each image needs) to all services; set read_only:true with explicit tmpfs/volume mounts for the few writable paths (backend already isolates writes to /app/storage,/app/uploads,/app/logs volumes; nginx needs /var/cache/nginx,/var/run as tmpfs). Run nginx and alertmanager as non-root.

findings.title: Monitoring stack (Prometheus + Alertmanager) is published on all host interfaces, not loopback
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claims are factually accurate on main. docker-compose.monitoring.yml:61-62 maps "9090:9090" and :103-104 maps "9093:9093" with no 127.0.0.1: prefix, while docker-compose.prod.yml:27 does correctly bind postgres to 127.0.0.1:5432:5432 — the contrast is exactly as described. alertmanager runs user: root (line 73), and the entrypoint sed (lines 83-89) renders ALERT_SMTP_PASSWORD into the writable /tmp/alertmanager.yml. There is NO nginx reverse-proxy for 9090/9093 (ops/nginx has only the public app vhosts) and NO firewall automation in the repo (only a generic "use UFW/iptables" note in docs/GITHUB_SECRETS.md:349 about 5432), so no missed mitigation neutralizes the binding. Neither Prometheus' UI/API nor Alertmanager's API is authenticated, so the silence-creation / topology-leak attack path is technically valid on a firewall-less public VPS.

However the severity is overstated as high for the real production context, for three concrete reasons grounded in the code: (1) This is an explicitly OPT-IN, fully separate stack. The file header (lines 1-7) and ops/monitoring/README.md (lines 5-7, 107-110) state it adds/changes nothing in prod/staging and must be brought up manually with `docker compose -f docker-compose.monitoring.yml up -d` plus manual env exports — it is not part of the deployed prod stack at all, so the exposure only exists if an operator deliberately stands it up. (2) The SMTP-credential-exposure sub-claim is conditional: the repo ships NO real secrets (compose defaults are ALERT_SMTP_PASSWORD='' line 102, ALERT_EMAIL_FROM=alerts@example.invalid) and the email receiver is shipped commented-out (README lines 39, 58) — so /tmp/alertmanager.yml contains credentials only if the operator wires real SMTP, and even then only inside the container. (3) The data it would leak is internal scrape topology/RED metrics and the ability to create silences — operationally meaningful but not direct tenant-data or money compromise. The binding-to-all-interfaces and user: root are genuine, fixable hardening gaps and the recommendation (bind 127.0.0.1, drop root) is correct, but conditioned on operator opt-in + no host firewall this is a medium hardening issue, not high.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.monitoring.yml:61-62 maps '9090:9090' (Prometheus) and :103-104 maps '9093:9093' (Alertmanager) with no 127.0.0.1: prefix — contrast docker-compose.prod.yml:27 which correctly binds postgres to 127.0.0.1:5432. Alertmanager also runs as user: root (line 73) and its rendered config at /tmp/alertmanager.yml contains SMTP credentials (ALERT_SMTP_PASSWORD, lines 88,102).
findings.why_it_matters: On a public VPS without a strict host firewall, the Prometheus UI/API (all internal target topology, scrape configs, and metrics) and the Alertmanager API are reachable from the internet. An attacker can read operational intelligence and, via the Alertmanager API, create silences to suppress alerting during an attack, or read the SMTP receiver configuration. This blinds the on-call exactly when it matters.
findings.recommendation: Bind both to 127.0.0.1 (127.0.0.1:9090:9090 / 127.0.0.1:9093:9093) and reach them via the nginx reverse proxy with auth, or keep them on the internal docker network only. Drop alertmanager's user: root.

findings.title: No log-driver / log-rotation configuration — default json-file grows unbounded and can fill the host disk
findings.evidence: grep for logging:/max-size/max-file/json-file across docker-compose.prod.yml, staging, and monitoring returns nothing. Docker therefore uses the default json-file driver with no max-size/max-file, so each container's stdout/stderr log grows without bound on the same disk as postgres_data and uploads_storage.
findings.why_it_matters: A chatty backend (NestJS request logs, Prisma query logs, retry storms) on a long-running prod host will accumulate gigabytes of JSON logs. When the disk fills, postgres can no longer write WAL and the database — and thus every tenant — goes down. This is a classic, silent single-VPS outage that surfaces weeks after onboarding.
findings.recommendation: Add a logging block (driver json-file with options max-size: 10m, max-file: 5) to each service, or set the daemon-wide default in /etc/docker/daemon.json. Ship logs off-box if retention is needed.

findings.title: Backend runs on the full Debian node:20 image (2.96GB) instead of a slim/distroless runtime
findings.evidence: backend/Dockerfile:39 'FROM node:20 AS production' is the full Debian 12 (bookworm) image (confirmed via /etc/os-release). The built image measures 2.96GB; node_modules is 681MB and the remainder is the Debian base plus apt-installed native libs (cairo/pango/jpeg/gif/rsvg/pixman) and leftover devDeps. node:20 ships a full userland (bash, apt, perl, gcc-runtime, curl 7.88.1).
findings.why_it_matters: A ~3GB image slows every pull during deploy and rollback (longer downtime window), increases registry/egress cost, and ships a large OS attack surface (full apt toolchain available to any RCE). On the immutable-tag deploy path, pull latency directly extends the blue/green swap window.
findings.recommendation: Move the production stage to node:20-slim (keeps the canvas/pdf native libs you install explicitly but drops the rest of the Debian userland) — typically cuts ~700MB-1GB. Combined with --omit=dev pruning this should roughly halve the image. Distroless is possible but complicates the runtime prisma CLI / curl healthcheck dependencies.

findings.title: Base images and node:20 are pinned by tag, not digest; node:20 is a rolling tag with no OS-currency / scan gate
findings.evidence: backend/Dockerfile:1,39 use 'node:20' (rolling); landing/developer/help/frontend use node:20-alpine / node:18 / node:18-alpine (frontend/Dockerfile:1 still on node:18, EOL April 2025). No Dockerfile or CI step pins a @sha256 digest, and grep of CI workflows for trivy/scan/cosign/digest returns no image vulnerability scan or digest pinning in release-deploy.yml.
findings.why_it_matters: node:20 silently advances minor/patch and OS packages between builds, so a rebuild of the same app tag can pull a different, possibly-regressed or newly-vulnerable base — again undermining reproducibility. node:18 on the frontend is past upstream EOL (no security backports). With no image scanning, known-CVE base layers ship to a payment-handling prod with no gate.
findings.recommendation: Pin bases by digest (node:20-slim@sha256:...), bump the frontend build/dev stages off EOL node:18, and add a Trivy (or grype) image-scan step to release-deploy.yml that fails on high/critical CVEs before the :current promotion.

findings.title: developer/ and help/ .dockerignore are too weak to keep a future .env out of the image (only excludes .env*.local)
findings.evidence: developer/.dockerignore and help/.dockerignore both contain only '.env*.local' (line 6) and use 'COPY . .' in their Dockerfiles (developer/Dockerfile:6). A plain .env or .env.production placed in those dirs would be copied into the build context — unlike backend/.dockerignore:6-11 and frontend/.dockerignore:7-12 which exclude .env and .env.* wholesale. No such file exists today, so this is latent.
findings.why_it_matters: These are static docs portals with no secrets today, so impact is currently nil; but the asymmetric, weaker ignore rule is a footgun — the moment anyone drops a .env for a build-time API key into developer/ or help/, it bakes into a published GHCR image. The repo is public (per the security runbook), so a leaked .env would be world-readable.
findings.recommendation: Make developer/.dockerignore and help/.dockerignore match the backend/frontend pattern: exclude '.env' and '.env.*' (keeping '!.env.example'), plus node_modules/.next which they already cover. Cheap defense-in-depth.

findings.title: SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN is passed via build-args and registry build-cache (mode=max) rather than BuildKit secrets
findings.evidence: release-deploy.yml:253,268 pass SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN as a build-arg; the Dockerfiles set it as ENV in the build stage (frontend/Dockerfile:43, landing/Dockerfile:27). The final stages are separate FROM images that only COPY artifacts, so the token is NOT in the final runtime image — but build-args are recorded in build-stage layer metadata, and landing/developer/help push cache-to: registry,mode=max (release-deploy.yml:271-272,286-287,299-300), which can persist intermediate layers/metadata to GHCR.
findings.why_it_matters: On a public repo+registry, a Sentry auth token captured in a pushed build-cache layer's history could be extracted and used to tamper with Sentry source maps/releases. The blast radius is limited (Sentry, not customer data) and the final image is clean, which is why this is low rather than higher.
findings.recommendation: Pass SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN via BuildKit secrets (RUN --mount=type=secret) instead of ARG/ENV so it never enters layer history or build cache; rotate the current token given the public-registry cache exposure.

summary: The data layer has a solid foundation: tenant/branch scoping columns are well-indexed, the highest-write tables (outbox_events, caller_events) have purpose-built composite indexes and DB-level idempotency constraints, auth tokenHash columns now carry @unique indexes (a prior gap), and there is a thoughtful expand/contract migration strategy with a migration-doctor and baseline workflow. However, three runtime-correctness/scaling issues will bite as real paying tenants and a second backend replica come online: (1) every advisory-locked cron acquires and releases its lock on DIFFERENT pooled connections, so under any non-trivial pool the unlock silently fails and the session-scoped lock leaks until the connection recycles — stalling the cron across all replicas; (2) the production compose hardcodes DATABASE_URL with NO connection_limit (overriding the rendered env that sets 25), so each backend process defaults Prisma to a small pool while Postgres runs at the default max_connections=100 with no pgbouncer — a multi-replica connection-exhaustion footgun; (3) almost every high-volume append table (caller_events, traffic_flow_records, page_views, user_activities, audit_logs, occupancy_records, notifications, analytics_heatmap_cache) has NO retention/pruner, so they grow without bound. There is also no statement_timeout anywhere, so one runaway analytics query can pin a connection indefinitely, and tenant isolation is purely application-layer (no RLS).
strengths: Auth token security is correct: RefreshToken.tokenHash is @unique (schema.prisma:182) and ScreenSession.tokenHash is @unique + @@index (schema.prisma:1819,1829), so token-verification lookups are index-backed, not seq-scans — a prior concern is resolved.

Highest-write tables are well-indexed for their access patterns: outbox_events has @@index([status, nextAttemptAt]) matching the worker's exact drain predicate plus @@index([type,createdAt]) and @@index([tenantId,createdAt]) (schema.prisma:4164-4166); caller_events has a DB-level idempotency UNIQUE (tenantId,providerId,callId,kind) plus @@index([tenantId, occurredAt desc]) (schema.prisma:4908-4911).

outbox_events is the one hot table with a real, well-engineered pruner: bounded-batch DELETE of dispatched rows older than RETENTION_DAYS, single-flight guarded, with a boot-time warning if retention is shorter than the webhook retry envelope (outbox-worker.service.ts:123-158, 74-86) — and failed/DLQ rows are deliberately never auto-pruned.

Migration deploy uses a sound expand/contract approach: additive-only migrations run inside the OLD container before the atomic :current swap, with a State.Status guard so a crash-looped backend defers migration to the fresh container instead of aborting the deploy (deploy.sh:367-388).

Migration safety tooling exists: db-migration-doctor.sh detects the hidden-P3005 unbaselined-DB case, and db-baseline.yml is a guarded manual workflow (typed confirmation per env, dry-run mode, concurrency group shared with deploy so a baseline can't race a deploy) (db-baseline.yml:36-47,58-66).

Tenant/branch scoping is consistently modeled: branch-scoped composite UNIQUE indexes were added across tables, branches, stock_items, z_reports, cameras, traffic_flow_records etc. (migration 20260615000000_branch_scope_uniqueness), and high-write analytics tables carry @@index([tenantId, branchId]).

Prisma query logging is correctly gated behind an explicit PRISMA_LOG_QUERIES flag AND refuses to honor it when NODE_ENV=production, preventing bound-parameter secrets (token/OTP/password hashes) from leaking into logs (prisma.service.ts:19-29).

findings.title: Advisory-lock acquire and release run on different pooled connections → session-scoped lock leaks and stalls every cron
findings.verify_reasoning: The technical claim is TRUE on current main (HEAD 7b27933c). advisory-lock.ts:27-39 acquires via one prisma.$queryRawUnsafe('SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(...)') and releases via a SEPARATE prisma.$queryRawUnsafe('SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(...)') in finally, with NO $transaction in the file. The 3 inline schedulers I sampled replicate the exact anti-pattern: token-refresh.scheduler.ts:21-37, subscription-scheduler.service.ts:61-74, and the same shape exists in z-report/stock-alerts/order-polling (all matched by grep). pg_(try_)advisory_lock is session-scoped, and Prisma only pins a connection across awaits inside an interactive $transaction(async (tx) => ...); two independent $queryRawUnsafe calls each check out and return a pooled connection, so the unlock can run on a different connection than the acquire. NO MITIGATION exists elsewhere: there is no connection_limit in any .env, schema.prisma datasource, or compose (Prisma then defaults to num_cpus*2+1 > 1 connections — if it were connection_limit=1 the claim would be refuted, but it is not); no pgbouncer/transaction-pooler (grep empty); PrismaService is a plain PrismaClient with no middleware pinning connections. The unit spec (advisory-lock.spec.ts) fully MOCKS $queryRawUnsafe, so it cannot catch this and gives false green — consistent with the existing MEMORY note marking this a deferred infra finding. Mechanism of harm is real: once an unlock lands on the wrong connection, the lock stays held by the acquiring session; every OTHER pooled connection's pg_try_advisory_lock for that id then returns false and that cron silently skips until the poisoned connection is recycled. WHY I DOWNGRADE blocker → high: (1) it is probabilistic, not a guaranteed permanent stall of 'every cron' as titled — the next tick's acquire may land on a fresh/unpoisoned connection and succeed, and a later tick coincidentally running the same job's unlock on the poisoned connection clears it; (2) the failure mode amplifies with replica count and pool churn, but the current prod compose (docker-compose.prod.yml) declares NO replicas/scale (single replica), where the advisory lock is largely redundant defensive coding to begin with; (3) Postgres idle-connection recycling bounds the leak duration. The fix (pg_advisory_xact_lock inside an explicit $transaction, auto-released at COMMIT, immune to pool hand-off) is correct and cheap. Real risk to money/fiscal/order-routing crons is genuine but conditional, so high (not blocker), and it is latent today on a single replica — it becomes serious the moment they scale out, which the code comments explicitly anticipate.
findings.evidence: backend/src/common/scheduling/advisory-lock.ts:27-40 calls prisma.$queryRawUnsafe('SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(...)') to acquire and a separate prisma.$queryRawUnsafe('SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(...)') in finally — neither inside a $transaction (confirmed: no $transaction in the file). The 5 inline schedulers do the same (subscription-scheduler.service.ts:62/73, z-report-scheduler.service.ts:33/55, stock-alerts.scheduler.ts:38/75, token-refresh.scheduler.ts:24/36, order-polling.scheduler.ts:51/59). 12 withAdvisoryLock call sites + 27 @Cron jobs total. pg_try_advisory_lock is SESSION-scoped (held by the specific backend connection); without $transaction Prisma does not pin a connection across the two awaits, so the unlock routinely executes on a DIFFERENT pooled connection. Postgres then returns pg_advisory_unlock=false silently (no error). No pgbouncer (grep for pgbouncer/6432 empty), connections are long-lived.
findings.why_it_matters: When the unlock lands on the wrong connection the lock is NOT released; it persists for the lifetime of the connection that acquired it (hours, until pool recycle). Every subsequent tick of that cron across ALL replicas sees the lock still held and silently skips. Affected jobs include subscription billing/renewal, Z-report scheduling, delivery-platform token refresh and order polling, outbox webhook delivery, reservation auto-hold/release, and tenant add-on sweeping — i.e. money, fiscal, and order-routing crons can silently stop firing with zero error surface. This is exactly the failure that grows worse with replica count and pool size.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the acquire→run→unlock sequence so all three statements execute on ONE pinned connection: use prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { ... }) and run pg_try_advisory_lock/work/pg_advisory_unlock via tx, OR switch to pg_advisory_xact_lock(...) inside an explicit transaction (auto-released at COMMIT/ROLLBACK, no manual unlock, immune to pool hand-off). Apply to advisory-lock.ts and all 5 inline schedulers. Add an assertion/log on the boolean returned by pg_advisory_unlock to detect leaks.

findings.title: Production DATABASE_URL is hardcoded in compose WITHOUT connection_limit, overriding the rendered env that sets it
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every factual claim is true on current main. docker-compose.prod.yml:90 sets `DATABASE_URL: postgresql://...@postgres:5432/${POSTGRES_DB:-restaurant_pos_prod}` inside the backend `environment:` block with NO query params. The CI-rendered .env.production (release-deploy.yml:368) DOES include `?connection_limit=25&pool_timeout=10`, and is loaded via `env_file: .env.production` (line 63-64). Docker Compose's documented precedence has `environment:` win over `env_file:`, so the running container gets the param-less URL and the ops-tuned pool sizing is discarded — confirmed. The staging contrast is also real: docker-compose.staging.yml's backend `environment:` block (lines 82-90) has only NODE_ENV/PORT/INTEGRATION_KEY/KMS/OTEL and NO DATABASE_URL, so staging correctly inherits connection_limit=25 from env_file — this proves the prod override is unintentional drift, not a deliberate choice. I also confirmed there is NO pgbouncer anywhere and NO `-c max_connections` override on the postgres service (it runs default max_connections=100), exactly as stated.

Where I diverge from the finder is on the present-tense framing of severity. Today prod runs a SINGLE backend (container_name: kds_backend_prod pins it to a singleton — you cannot `--scale` a fixed-container_name service), and the backend has no cpu limit in prod compose, so Prisma's default pool (num_physical_cpus*2+1) on a typical 2-4 vCPU host is ~5-9 connections — i.e. SMALLER than the intended 25. So on the current single instance the param-less URL does not cause connection exhaustion; if anything it under-pools. The exhaustion scenario in WHY-IT-MATTERS is explicitly contingent on adding a second replica (the stated near-term plan) AND a host with a high CPU count making the default balloon. That makes this a real, latent config-drift / non-determinism / silently-ignored-ops-tuning bug that will bite the moment they scale — a legitimate defect that should be fixed exactly as recommended (drop the line from environment: so env_file takes effect, matching staging) — but it is not a present-day P1 outage. Hence the claim is real but the 'critical' label is the future-state, not the current-state; corrected to high (latent prod-config defect blocking safe horizontal scaling, trivially fixable). The recommendation (remove the environment: DATABASE_URL; size limit so replicas*limit + crons < max_connections; consider pgbouncer/raise max_connections before ~3 replicas) is sound.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.prod.yml:90 sets `DATABASE_URL: postgresql://...@postgres:5432/${POSTGRES_DB}` in the service `environment:` block with NO query params. The CI-rendered .env.production (release-deploy.yml:368) DOES set `?connection_limit=25&pool_timeout=10`, and is loaded via `env_file:` (line 64). Docker Compose precedence: `environment:` overrides `env_file:`, so the container receives the param-less URL. With no connection_limit, Prisma defaults its pool to num_physical_cpus*2+1. Postgres runs default max_connections=100 (postgres:15-alpine, no -c max_connections override in compose), and there is no pgbouncer (grep empty). Staging compose by contrast uses env_file only (no DATABASE_URL in environment), so it correctly inherits connection_limit=25 — confirming the prod override is unintentional drift.
findings.why_it_matters: The carefully-tuned pool sizing (25) is silently discarded in production only. As soon as a second backend replica is added (the stated near-term scaling plan), pool sizing becomes the variable that decides whether you exhaust Postgres's 100 connections; relying on an undocumented Prisma default that varies by host CPU count is unpredictable and not what ops configured. Connection exhaustion manifests as P1001/timeout errors on random requests under load — a hard-to-diagnose partial outage.
findings.recommendation: Remove the DATABASE_URL line from the `environment:` block in docker-compose.prod.yml so the rendered .env.production (with connection_limit + pool_timeout) takes effect, matching staging. Set an explicit, intentional connection_limit sized to replica_count*limit < max_connections (e.g. 100 max_connections, 2-3 replicas → limit ~25-30 with headroom for crons/migrations/psql). Consider raising Postgres max_connections or adding pgbouncer before scaling past ~3 replicas.

findings.title: No retention/pruning on high-volume append tables — unbounded growth
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim holds on current main. Verified evidence:

1. outbox_events IS the only table with a real production pruner (outbox-worker.service.ts:123-158, pruneOnce — bounded-batch DELETE of dispatched rows older than RETENTION_DAYS, run hourly). The pattern the finder cites as the template is real.

2. A repo-wide deleteMany search on the high-volume tables returns ZERO production cleanup. The only deleteMany calls on callerEvent/trafficFlowRecord/pageView/userActivity/auditLog/occupancyRecord/analyticsHeatmapCache live in mock-data-generator.service.ts:619-627 — a test/demo reset scoped by (tenantId,branchId), not retention. The two analytics deleteMany matches I found (insights.service.ts:471 analyticsInsight, camera.service.ts:230 camera) are ordinary CRUD on different, low-cardinality tables, not the append tables.

3. analytics_heatmap_cache: confirmed the row sets expiresAt (cacheHeatmap upsert, heatmap.service.ts:642-679, TTL 1/6/24h by granularity) and is checked on read (line 624: `cached.expiresAt > new Date()`), but is NEVER deleted. The upsert is keyed on the compound (tenantId,branchId,startTime,endTime,granularity,metric), so a new stale row is left behind for every distinct historical time-window ever queried, and stale rows accumulate forever. There is no purge-expired pass (the MEMORY-noted heatmap-cache growth-bounding fix lives on an unmerged /loop branch, not main).

4. No DB-level mitigation: grep of prisma/migrations found no partitioning, pg_cron, or TTL on any of these tables. The existing cleanup crons (subscription paytr-orphan, self-pay-orphan, tenant-addon-sweeper, self-pay-intent-expire) all operate on subscription/order STATE expiry, never on the append/log tables.

5. Production write paths confirmed: callerEvent.create (caller.service.ts:69, per phone call), pageView.create (public-stats.service.ts:86, per public page hit), userActivity.create (auth/password/cash-drawer, per security/cash action), auditLog.create (superadmin-audit.service.ts:42), occupancyRecord.createMany (analytics.gateway.ts:377, one row PER DETECTION per camera frame), trafficFlowRecord.upsert (analytics.gateway.ts:696, per hour*cell*branch). The CV-driven occupancyRecord and trafficFlowRecord are genuinely machine-generated at high rate when cameras are active.

Severity assessment: 'high' is defensible but I'd lean to the high/medium boundary and keep it at high only with nuance. Arguments to keep high: occupancyRecord (per-frame createMany) and the heatmap-cache leak grow truly unboundedly and have no operator lever short of manual SQL; on tenants running CV cameras this can dominate the data volume and degrade autovacuum/index/backup. Arguments it's slightly overstated: the very highest-rate table (occupancyRecord) is gated on customers actually deploying edge cameras/CV — not all tenants — and trafficFlowRecord is an UPSERT keyed per hour*cell*branch, so its cardinality is bounded per time bucket and grows linearly-with-time rather than per-event (its row count is far lower than 'per detection'). pageView/userActivity/auditLog/callerEvent are append-per-event but at human/operator rate, so they grow but slowly. Net: a real, unmitigated, no-operator-lever growth problem on append/log/cache tables — correctly categorized; high is appropriate.
findings.evidence: Only outbox_events has a real production pruner (outbox-worker.service.ts:123-158). A repo-wide grep for deleteMany/DELETE/prune/retention against callerEvent, trafficFlowRecord, pageView, userActivity, auditLog, occupancyRecord, notification, analyticsHeatmapCache finds NO production cleanup — the only matches are the test-only mock-data-generator.service.ts:619-627. analytics_heatmap_cache rows set expiresAt and check it on read (heatmap.service.ts:624) but are NEVER deleted (no deleteMany in heatmap.service.ts), so expired rows accumulate, one per distinct (tenant,branch,startTime,endTime,granularity,metric) tuple. caller_events grows per phone call, page_views per public page hit, user_activities per auth/cash/subscription action, traffic_flow_records per hour*cell*branch, audit_logs per superadmin action.
findings.why_it_matters: Under real paying tenants at growing scale these tables grow without bound, degrading index size, autovacuum cost, query latency, and backup/restore time, and eventually consuming the data volume. caller_events and traffic_flow_records in particular are machine-generated at high rate; the heatmap cache leaks a stale row for every time-window ever queried. There is no operator lever to reclaim space short of manual SQL.
findings.recommendation: Add bounded-batch retention pruners (same pattern as the outbox pruner) for caller_events, traffic_flow_records, page_views, user_activities, occupancy_records, notifications, and a delete-expired pass for analytics_heatmap_cache. Make retention windows env-configurable. Coordinate one job per table behind the advisory-lock helper (after fixing the lock leak above) so only one replica prunes.

findings.title: No statement_timeout / query timeout anywhere — one runaway query can pin a connection indefinitely
findings.verify_reasoning: The technical claim is accurate on current main. (1) No statement_timeout/idle_in_transaction_session_timeout/lock_timeout exists anywhere in application code, compose, or env templates — the only matches are inside node_modules type defs (@types/pg). (2) PrismaService (backend/src/prisma/prisma.service.ts:22-29) passes only the raw process.env.DATABASE_URL with no query params and no client-side timeout option. (3) The prod DATABASE_URL (docker-compose.prod.yml:90) is `postgresql://...@postgres:5432/...` with NO statement_timeout/options appended; all env templates (.env.example, .env.production.template, .env.staging.template) likewise omit it. (4) The postgres service (postgres:15-alpine) has no `command:`/`-c statement_timeout` override in prod OR staging compose. (5) No `ALTER ROLE ... SET statement_timeout` or `SET statement_timeout` in any migration or init SQL; no pgbouncer. (6) The finder's one cited mitigation is real: the floor-plan save tx has `{ timeout: 30_000, maxWait: 10_000 }` — but I confirmed the path is backend/src/modules/tables/floor-plan.service.ts:438 (finder mis-cited it as modules/floor-plan/, though line number and content match exactly), and that is Prisma's interactive-transaction *client* timeout (P2028 rollout), NOT a server-side statement_timeout, and it is the ONLY one in the codebase. The shared transaction wrapper `withTransaction` (common/utils/tracing.ts:36) is purely a Sentry span wrapper — it injects no DB timeout. Heavy unbounded analytics groupBy/$queryRaw queries do exist (analytics/services/insights.service.ts, table-analytics.service.ts) over the multi-tenant data layer. No mitigation was missed: nginx confs have no proxy_read_timeout, so even the front proxy won't cancel an in-flight query at the PG level — the pooled connection stays pinned for the full query duration regardless. connection_limit is also unset, so Prisma uses its small default pool (cpus*2+1) per instance, which makes pool exhaustion under a few slow/locked queries realistically reachable. Severity 'high' is justified for a shared-pool multi-tenant production system: a single runaway/locked query (or a non-CONCURRENT CREATE INDEX during deploy) can pin a connection and cascade into a noisy-neighbor outage. It is not 'critical' because it requires a triggering slow/locked query rather than being directly exploitable, and the recommended fix (one connection-string option or ALTER ROLE) is trivial — so 'high' is the right level, not over- or under-stated.
findings.evidence: Grep for statement_timeout across backend/src, compose files, env templates, and CI returns nothing. DATABASE_URL has no statement_timeout option and Postgres is not configured with one (no -c statement_timeout in compose). The only $transaction with explicit timeouts is floor-plan.service.ts:438 ({timeout:30_000, maxWait:10_000}); all other Prisma calls (including unbounded analytics aggregations and groupBy over the unpruned tables above) have no upper bound. The other 'timeout' grep hits are HTTP client timeouts (PayTR/axios), not DB.
findings.why_it_matters: With a small connection pool and no statement_timeout, a single slow/locked analytics query (e.g. a heavy groupBy over an unpruned traffic_flow_records or a query blocked behind a non-concurrent CREATE INDEX during deploy) holds its pooled connection for as long as it runs. A handful of these exhausts the pool and cascades into request failures for unrelated tenants — a classic noisy-neighbor outage in a shared-pool multi-tenant system.
findings.recommendation: Set a conservative server-side default via the connection string (e.g. DATABASE_URL ...&options=-c%20statement_timeout%3D30000) or a per-role ALTER ROLE ... SET statement_timeout, plus an idle_in_transaction_session_timeout to reap stuck transactions. Optionally add lock_timeout for the migration role to fail fast rather than block writes during DDL.

findings.title: Non-CONCURRENT index creation in migrations runs against the live serving DB during deploy
findings.evidence: 46 of 49 CREATE INDEX statements across backend/prisma/migrations are non-concurrent (only 3 use CONCURRENTLY) — e.g. page_views_createdAt_idx (20260614010000), traffic_flow_records and other large-table indexes in 20260615000000. deploy.sh:387 runs `prisma migrate deploy` inside the OLD, still-serving container BEFORE the swap. prisma migrate wraps DDL in a transaction, which cannot run CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, so these always take a blocking lock.
findings.why_it_matters: A plain CREATE INDEX takes a lock that blocks writes to the table for the duration of the build. On a small table this is sub-second, but once page_views / traffic_flow_records / caller_events grow (see the no-retention finding), an index-adding migration during a live deploy will stall all writes to that table — order/caller/analytics ingestion pauses mid-service. The risk scales with table size, which is currently unbounded.
findings.recommendation: For large-table index additions, author them as raw SQL migrations using CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY run OUTSIDE Prisma's transaction (mark the migration so Prisma doesn't wrap it, or apply out-of-band), and add lock_timeout to fail fast. Pair with the retention fix so hot tables stay small. Document an index-DDL checklist in db-migration-doctor.

findings.title: Tenant isolation is application-layer only — no Postgres Row-Level Security
findings.evidence: Grep for ROW LEVEL SECURITY / CREATE POLICY / ENABLE ROW / current_setting / set_config across backend/prisma/migrations and backend/src returns no RLS usage (the only current_setting-adjacent hits are unrelated). Isolation relies entirely on every query carrying the correct tenantId/branchId WHERE clause plus the application BranchGuard. The schema models tenantId/branchId columns and composite uniques, but the DB does not enforce that a query without a tenant predicate returns only that tenant's rows.
findings.why_it_matters: In a multi-tenant SaaS onboarding real paying tenants, a single missed tenantId filter in any of hundreds of query sites (or a future raw-SQL query, or an admin/report endpoint) leaks one tenant's data to another with no DB-level backstop. The all-or-nothing app-layer model means correctness depends on perfect discipline across the entire codebase forever.
findings.recommendation: Adopt defense-in-depth: enable RLS on the highest-sensitivity tenant tables (orders, payments, customers, invoices, caller_events) with a policy keyed on a session GUC (app.tenant_id) set per request via set_config in a Prisma middleware/extension. Even rolling it out to the money/PII tables first dramatically shrinks the blast radius of an app-layer scoping bug. At minimum, add an automated lint/test that asserts every tenant-scoped repository method includes a tenantId predicate.

summary: Redis backs only two cross-replica concerns — the Socket.IO pub/sub adapter (realtime fan-out for KDS tickets, table/floor updates, caller popups) and the entitlement-cache invalidation bus — plus a health-check ping. Both are correctly OPT-IN with graceful single-node fall-back, and the heavier coordination concerns (distributed locks, scheduler leader-election) are deliberately on Postgres advisory locks instead of Redis, which is a sound choice. However, three things make Redis a fragile, partly-decorative dependency at multi-replica scale: (1) the global rate-limiter has NO Redis store and, worse, every per-route @Throttle (login, register, password-reset) names a throttler "default" that does not exist in the module config, so those strict limits are silently never applied; (2) the Socket.IO Redis adapter permanently gives up after ~3 reconnect attempts, so a brief Redis blip after boot silently kills cross-replica realtime until a pod restart; (3) prod Redis is a single un-replicated container with no maxmemory/eviction or memory limit and only default RDB persistence, and the Redis-backed path is never exercised in staging (no REDIS_URL there). These bite specifically once a second backend replica is added — which is the stated scaling plan.
strengths: Redis is correctly scoped to cross-replica-only concerns and is genuinely opt-in: redis-io.adapter.ts and entitlement-invalidation.bus.ts both warn-and-degrade to single-node behavior when REDIS_URL is absent, so dev/single-node never hard-depends on Redis (redis-io.adapter.ts:37-43, entitlement-invalidation.bus.ts:48-54).

Distributed coordination (cron leader-election, idempotent sweeps) uses Postgres advisory locks rather than Redis (app.module.ts:80; common/scheduling/advisory-lock used by plan-projector, z-report-scheduler, marketplace sweeper), so a Redis outage does NOT corrupt scheduling or money flows.
