The entitlement in-process cache is a well-engineered bounded LRU-ish cache (MAX_CACHE_SIZE=10_000, 60s sweep, unref'd timer) with millisecond cross-replica invalidation via Redis pub/sub and a senderId self-echo filter, and it correctly stays eventually-consistent via the 30s TTL when Redis is down (entitlement.service.ts:50-114, entitlement-invalidation.bus.ts:29-33,115-128).

The health probe wraps redis.ping() and prisma in a 2s withTimeout race so a half-dead Redis socket cannot hang the K8s readiness tick and cascade pod restarts (app.service.ts:16-25,69-80), and the health client uses maxRetriesPerRequest:1 + retryStrategy:()=>null so a probe never queues forever.

Prod Redis enforces AUTH (redis-server --requirepass) and the backend connects with the password and a dedicated logical DB (REDIS_URL=redis://:PASS@redis:6379/2), keeping it off db0 (docker-compose.prod.yml:47,91).
Socket.IO clean shutdown is wired: enableShutdownHooks + disconnectRedis()/quit() drains pub/sub clients on SIGTERM during rolling restarts (main.ts:230-232, redis-io.adapter.ts:96-103).

findings.title: Every per-route @Throttle names a non-existent "default" throttler → auth/login/register/password-reset rate limits are silently NOT enforced
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is TRUE on current main. Verified end-to-end:

1) Global throttlers are named only short/medium/long (app.module.ts:82-98) — there is NO throttler named "default".
2) Every sensitive auth override is keyed under "default": LOGIN_THROTTLE/REGISTER_THROTTLE/FORGOT/VERIFY/SOCIAL/REFRESH/CHANGE_PASSWORD = { default: {...} } (auth.controller.ts:40-58) applied at :114,131,151,etc.
3) @nestjs/throttler v6.4.0 (confirmed version in node_modules): the @Throttle decorator's setThrottlerMetadata (throttler.decorator.js:7-14) stores each override under the reflector key THROTTLER_LIMIT/TTL + name, where name = "default". But ThrottlerGuard.canActivate (throttler.guard.js:66-82) iterates ONLY this.throttlers (the global array: short/medium/long) and reads route overrides via THROTTLER_LIMIT + namedThrottler.name. Since none of those names is "default", the "default"-keyed route metadata is NEVER consulted. The decorators are dead.
4) The single global throttler guard is MachineThrottlerGuard (registered as the lone throttler APP_GUARD at app.module.ts:179-184); it extends ThrottlerGuard and overrides only getTracker, so it inherits the unmodified short/medium/long config. There is no second guard, no per-controller @UseGuards(ThrottlerGuard) with custom options, and no Redis throttler storage override.

Mitigation search came up empty: no custom guard binds "default"; no nginx limit_req/limit_conn anywhere (ops/nginx/*.conf, frontend/nginx.conf) — so there is NO infra-level brute-force cap either. The existing test (auth.controller.throttle.spec.ts) only asserts the decorator metadata is ATTACHED, never that a 429 fires at the threshold — and its own comment wrongly assumes the routes "inherit the global default throttler," which is exactly the bug.

So the finding is real and the recommendation (add a global throttler named "default", or retarget every @Throttle to short/medium/long, plus a firing integration test) is correct.

SEVERITY NUANCE (why I land on high, not critical): the routes are NOT wide-open/unthrottled. The global throttlers still apply per IP: short = 10/s and long = 100/min. So login is effectively capped at ~100/min/IP instead of the intended 5/min — a real ~20x weakening (and register 3/hr intended → ~100/min effective, ~2000x looser for that surface, which is the worst case for SMS/email-cost abuse). But because IP throttling at 100/min still exists, a single-IP attacker is not unbounded; meaningful credential-stuffing requires distributed IPs, which would also defeat the intended per-IP 5/min anyway. This is a genuine production security regression on auth/register/forgot/reset, but the residual 100/min cap and the fact that it's per-IP (not per-account) pull it down from "critical/unbounded" to a solid high. Easy, low-risk fix; should be remediated before broad customer exposure.
findings.evidence: ThrottlerModule.forRoot defines only throttlers named short/medium/long (app.module.ts:82-98). But all 49 per-route limits use @Throttle({ default: {...} }) — e.g. LOGIN_THROTTLE = { default: { limit: 5, ttl: 60_000 } } (auth.controller.ts:40-58, applied at :114,131,151,251,263,273,287; superadmin-auth.controller.ts:36-38; public-stats/desktop-app/caller/local-bridge controllers). @nestjs/throttler v6.4.0's guard loops over the GLOBAL throttler array and reads route overrides by THROTTLER_LIMIT + namedThrottler.name (node_modules/@nestjs/throttler/dist/throttler.guard.js:66,76). Since no global throttler is named "default", the "default"-keyed route metadata is never read; those routes fall back to the global limits (short 10/s, medium 50/10s, long 100/min). So login effectively allows ~100/min, register ~100/min instead of the intended 5/min and 3/hr.
findings.why_it_matters: Credential-stuffing and brute-force protection on login, the registration-abuse cap, and the password-reset/forgot rate limits are all ineffective. An attacker gets ~100 login attempts/min per IP instead of 5, and account/SMS/email-cost abuse on register/forgot is wide open — directly endangering real tenant accounts the moment the product is exposed to paying customers.
findings.recommendation: Rename the global throttlers to include a "default" entry (or change every @Throttle to target short/medium/long). Simplest: add { name: 'default', ttl, limit } to ThrottlerModule.forRoot so the existing { default: ... } decorators bind. Add an integration test that fires N+1 requests at /auth/login and asserts a 429 at the intended threshold (the current auth.controller.throttle.spec.ts only asserts the decorator is ATTACHED, not that it fires).

findings.title: Rate-limiter uses in-memory per-process storage → limits multiply by replica count and reset on every deploy
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is FACTUALLY CORRECT. ThrottlerModule.forRoot at app.module.ts:82-98 is called with only an options array (named throttlers short/medium/long) and no `storage` key. The packaged provider factory at node_modules/@nestjs/throttler/dist/throttler.providers.js (ThrottlerStorageProvider.useFactory) falls back to `new ThrottlerStorageService()` whenever options is an array OR has no `.storage` — and ThrottlerStorageService constructor (throttler.service.js:13) is `this._storage = new Map()`, a plain in-process Map. No Redis-backed throttler storage is installed or wired: grep for `throttler-storage`, `ThrottlerStorageRedis`, `nest-lab` returns nothing in package.json or src; only `@nestjs/throttler ^6.4.0` is present. So under horizontal scale each replica keeps independent counters (limit × replica count) and every deploy wipes counters. The finder also correctly identified that the throttler IS actually active (not dead): MachineThrottlerGuard extends ThrottlerGuard and is registered as a global APP_GUARD (app.module.ts:180-183), there are 82 @Throttle usages, and the named throttlers sidestep the separate ThrottlerModule "default"-name inertness bug noted in project memory.

WHY SEVERITY IS OVERSTATED to medium, not high: the "limits multiply by replica count" harm is conditional on multi-replica deployment, which is the finder's own words a "stated scaling plan" — NOT the current reality. There is NO `replicas:` directive in either docker-compose.prod.yml or docker-compose.staging.yml (grep returns nothing), and no nginx upstream block load-balancing multiple backend pods exists (no nginx/ or deploy/ upstream config found). Today there is a single backend container per environment, so the multiplication harm is latent, not active. The remaining real-today impact is the deploy-reset (counters wiped on every rolling deploy, briefly resetting limits) which is a minor, transient abuse window. The fix is correct and cheap (reuse the existing REDIS_URL with a ThrottlerStorageRedis adapter), and it becomes genuinely high the moment a second replica is added — but on the current single-node deployment the real-user production impact is moderate, so medium is the honest rating.
findings.evidence: ThrottlerModule.forRoot is called with only an options array and no `storage` (app.module.ts:82-98). The throttler provider therefore instantiates the default ThrottlerStorageService (node_modules/@nestjs/throttler/dist/throttler.providers.js:17-21), which is a plain in-process Map (throttler.service.js:13 `this._storage = new Map()`). No @nest-lab/throttler-storage-redis (or equivalent) is installed or wired anywhere in src or package.json.
findings.why_it_matters: With 2+ backend replicas (the stated scaling plan), each replica keeps its own counters, so the effective limit is multiplied by the replica count and depends on which replica the load balancer routes to — brute-force/abuse protection is per-pod, not global. Every rolling deploy also wipes all counters, briefly resetting all limits to zero. Even after fixing the "default"-name bug, the limits remain unreliable under horizontal scale.
findings.recommendation: Back the throttler with Redis (the same REDIS_URL already used by the socket adapter) via a ThrottlerStorageRedis adapter, so counters are shared across replicas and survive deploys. Keep an in-memory fallback only for single-node dev.

findings.title: Socket.IO Redis adapter permanently STOPS reconnecting after ~3 retries → a brief Redis blip silently kills cross-replica realtime until pod restart
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical mechanism is fully CONFIRMED on main. redis-io.adapter.ts:52-55 returns `new Error("redis unreachable")` once retries > 3, and this same clientOpts object is attached to the long-lived pub/sub clients passed to createAdapter (lines 49-81). In node-redis 4.7.1 / @redis/client 1.6.1 (the installed versions), _RedisSocket_shouldReconnect (socket.js:130-142) sets isOpen=false and returns a ReconnectStrategyError when the strategy returns an Error. For a mid-life Redis blip the path is onSocketError (socket.js:215-224) → _connect() loop (143-170); each failed attempt calls shouldReconnect(retries++), so after ~4 failed attempts (a few seconds) the strategy returns Error, isOpen flips false, the do-while at line 170 (`isOpen && !isReady`) exits, and the catch at line 222 swallows it — reconnection is permanently dead with no self-recovery. I confirmed there is NO external recovery: connectToRedis() is called exactly once at boot (main.ts:227), there is no error/'end' handler that re-creates the clients, and no metric/alert on adapter death. I also checked the claimed-plausible mitigation (the /healthz/ready probe that the controller comment says covers "DB + Redis + adapters"): it does NOT mitigate this, because the readiness Redis check uses a SEPARATE ioredis client (app.service.ts:29-39,69-80), not the Socket.IO adapter's node-redis pub/sub clients — so a dead Socket.IO adapter is invisible to the probe; and the prod compose healthcheck only hits /api/health anyway (docker-compose.prod.yml:138), with liveness being a static 200 (app.controller.ts:65-70), so nothing restarts the pod on adapter death. So both "permanent stop" and "no self-recovery short of restart" are TRUE.

Severity is OVERSTATED for the current production reality, hence partly. The whole impact ("cross-replica realtime silently dies for half the clients") is conditional on running 2+ backend replicas behind a load balancer. The actual deployment is SINGLE-REPLICA: docker-compose.prod.yml runs one backend container (container_name kds_backend_prod, no replicas:/deploy.replicas), nginx proxies to a single 127.0.0.1:3000 (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:45-53), and staging's only `deploy:` blocks are resource limits, not replicas. With one process, every socket is local and in-process server.to(room).emit works regardless of the Redis adapter's state, so a permanently-dead adapter has effectively zero user-facing impact today. This is a genuine latent landmine that becomes a real high-severity outage the instant anyone scales to 2+ replicas (the exact reason the adapter exists), and the fix is cheap (uncapped capped-backoff reconnect for the live clients, bounded give-up only for the boot attempt). But it is not a live production realtime outage on the current fleet, so high → medium.
findings.evidence: redis-io.adapter.ts:52-55 sets reconnectStrategy to return `new Error('redis unreachable')` once retries > 3. This same options object is attached to the long-lived pub/sub clients used by createAdapter (redis-io.adapter.ts:49-80). In node-redis, returning an Error from reconnectStrategy makes #shouldReconnect set isOpen=false and return a ReconnectStrategyError, which stops reconnection permanently (node_modules/@redis/client/dist/lib/client/socket.js:137-141). The comment says this is to avoid log spam on boot, but it equally applies to runtime: there is no separate error-driven re-create of the adapter clients.
findings.why_it_matters: On a multi-replica deploy, any Redis restart / failover / network blip lasting more than a few seconds permanently severs the Socket.IO Redis adapter for the lifetime of the pod. After that, server.to(room).emit only reaches sockets on the SAME replica — KDS new-ticket events, table/floor-plan live updates, and caller popups silently drop for the half of clients connected to other replicas, with no error surfaced to users and no self-recovery short of restarting every backend pod.
findings.recommendation: Use an unbounded (capped-backoff) reconnect strategy for the live clients — e.g. return Math.min(retries * 200, 5000) with no give-up — so the adapter recovers automatically when Redis returns. Keep the bounded give-up ONLY for the initial connectToRedis() boot attempt (a separate short-lived attempt) if you still want fast single-node fall-back. Add a metric/alert on adapter client 'end'/'error' so a dead adapter is observable.

findings.title: Prod Redis is a single un-replicated SPOF with no maxmemory/eviction policy and no container memory limit
findings.evidence: Prod runs one redis:7-alpine container with a fixed container_name (kds_redis_prod) and no replicas/Sentinel/Cluster (docker-compose.prod.yml:39-59). The command is just `redis-server --requirepass ...` — no --maxmemory, no --maxmemory-policy. grep for mem_limit/deploy/resources across the prod compose returns 0 matches, so neither Redis nor the backend has a memory cap. Default maxmemory is 0 (unbounded).
findings.why_it_matters: Redis is a single point of failure for cross-replica realtime + entitlement invalidation: when it is down at boot the system degrades to single-node behavior, but with growing tenants the pub/sub channel and any future Redis-backed keys (e.g. a shared throttle store) grow unbounded with no eviction; an unexpected memory spike OOM-kills the container on the host with no maxmemory backstop. Acceptable for the first few tenants, but it must be bounded before scaling.
findings.recommendation: Set `--maxmemory <N>mb --maxmemory-policy noeviction` (or allkeys-lru if only used as a cache) and a container mem_limit, so Redis fails predictably instead of OOM-killing the host. Plan a managed/replicated Redis (Sentinel or a managed service) before going multi-replica, since the realtime layer's correctness depends on it.

findings.title: The Redis-backed multi-replica path is never exercised in staging (staging backend has no REDIS_URL)
findings.evidence: The staging backend service sets NODE_ENV/PORT/INTEGRATION_KEY/KMS/OTEL but NO REDIS_URL (docker-compose.staging.yml:82-90; grep for REDIS in that file returns nothing for the backend env). A redis container exists for staging (docker-compose.staging.yml:40-60) but the backend is never pointed at it. With REDIS_URL unset, redis-io.adapter.ts:37-43 and entitlement-invalidation.bus.ts:48-54 both take the in-memory / local-only branch.
findings.why_it_matters: Staging runs the in-memory Socket.IO adapter and disables the entitlement invalidation bus, so the exact Redis-dependent behavior that only matters in multi-replica prod (cross-replica broadcast, the reconnect-give-up bug above, db-index/auth correctness of the Redis URL) is never validated before a prod tag. Bugs in the Redis path ship straight to prod unverified.
findings.recommendation: Set REDIS_URL on the staging backend pointing at the staging redis (mirroring the prod redis://:PASS@redis:6379/<db> form) so the Redis adapter and invalidation bus are actually exercised pre-prod; ideally run 2 backend replicas in staging to prove cross-replica fan-out.

findings.title: Health-check Redis client defaults to localhost when REDIS_URL is unset → can misreport Redis health
findings.evidence: app.service.ts:34 builds the health client from `process.env.REDIS_URL || 'redis://localhost:6379'`. If REDIS_URL is ever unset/misconfigured in an environment, the /api/health redis check pings localhost:6379 (where there is no Redis in the containerized prod topology), so it reports redis unhealthy even when the real Redis is fine — or, conversely, if a sidecar localhost Redis exists it would report a DIFFERENT instance than the app actually uses. The socket adapter and bus, by contrast, treat unset REDIS_URL as 'no Redis' rather than localhost, so the three clients can disagree about whether Redis is even configured.
findings.why_it_matters: The health endpoint is the deploy gate (docker-compose.prod.yml:138 curls /api/health) and feeds operator dashboards; a localhost fallback makes the redis health line unreliable as a signal during an incident, and the inconsistency between the three Redis-client URL-resolution paths (URL||localhost vs URL-or-disabled) is a latent misconfiguration trap.
findings.recommendation: Make app.service.ts share the same REDIS_URL resolution as the adapter/bus: if REDIS_URL is unset, report redis as 'not-configured' rather than pinging localhost. Centralize Redis URL/options resolution (including db index and password) in one helper used by all three consumers.

summary: No default throttler tier so ~60 default-keyed @Throttle limits are inert (endpoints run at 100/min/IP not intended); in-memory storage multiplies per replica; tenant login has no lockout.
strengths: Trust-proxy + nginx XFF correct; MachineThrottlerGuard principal-under-IP; body/upload limits set.
Named-tier override correct in catalog/payments; SuperAdmin login has DB lockout.

findings.title: No default throttler tier: ~60 default-keyed @Throttle limits are INERT
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is TRUE on main. app.module.ts:82-98 ThrottlerModule.forRoot registers only short/medium/long — no "default" tier. The decorator (@nestjs/throttler 6.4.0, throttler.decorator.js setThrottlerMetadata) writes per-tier metadata keyed THROTTLER_LIMIT/TTL + name, so @Throttle({default:{...}}) writes a "default"-keyed key. The guard (throttler.guard.js canActivate) iterates ONLY over this.throttlers (the configured short/medium/long) and reads reflector.getAllAndOverride(THROTTLER_LIMIT + namedThrottler.name) for each. It NEVER reads "default"-keyed metadata because no throttler is named "default". onModuleInit only maps a missing opt.name to 'default', but that does not create a default throttler when names are explicitly set. So every @Throttle({default}) override is genuinely inert and the route falls back to the three global tiers.

The custom global guard MachineThrottlerGuard (registered as the sole APP_GUARD at app.module.ts:180-183) extends ThrottlerGuard and only overrides getTracker — it inherits canActivate verbatim, so the dead-default behavior is unchanged. No mitigation in config, the guard, or env. I confirmed no class-level @SkipThrottle on AuthController, so the global tiers do still fire.

I counted 49 @Throttle({default:...}) decorator sites (the finder's "~60" counts the auth THROTTLE constants + endpoints, so the order of magnitude is right). Only ~10 sites correctly use short/medium/long (catalog, partner/display, payments) — catalog.controller.ts:82-87 even carries a comment proving a dev knew tiers must be named, but the knowledge never propagated. Affected: login (wants 5/min, gets global), register/forgot/verify/social/refresh/change-password, public reservation lookup, contact form, delivery webhooks, qr-menu, customer-public, customer-orders, customer-self-pay, device-mesh, desktop-app, caller, local-bridge, public-stats.

CORRECTION to the finding's framing: the routes do NOT become unlimited or "100/min/IP" only — all three configured global tiers apply (short 10/1s, medium 50/10s, long 100/60s, per-IP). So login is capped at ~100/min/IP (and 10/sec, 50/10s) instead of the intended 5/min — roughly 20x looser, with no account lockout, but NOT wide open. Because a coarse IP backstop exists and the worst-case is a 20x-loose brute-force window on credential endpoints (not a directly exploitable crash/data-loss), I downgrade from blocker to HIGH: a real, broad, security-relevant abuse-surface regression worth fixing, but the global tiers prevent the unbounded scenario "blocker" implies. This matches the existing MEMORY note flagging this as a deferred infra finding; the one-line fix is to rename a global tier to "default" (or add a default tier) and audit the sites.
findings.evidence: app.module.ts:82-98 forRoot only short/medium/long; @nestjs/throttler 6.4.0 guard never reads default-keyed metadata so routes fall back to 100/min/IP. Affects login (auth.controller.ts:40 want 5/min), register/forgot/OTP/refresh (41-58), reservation lookup (public-reservations.controller.ts:96), contact (contact.controller.ts:34), webhooks (delivery-webhook.controller.ts:33) and more.
findings.why_it_matters: Brute-force/abuse limits 20-200x looser than claimed; login 100/min/IP not 5/min with no lockout.
findings.recommendation: Add a default tier or rename a global tier to default; audit the 60 sites.

findings.title: In-memory per-process throttle storage (no Redis)
findings.verify_reasoning:
Technical claim is fully accurate. app.module.ts:82-98 calls ThrottlerModule.forRoot([{short},{medium},{long}]) with a bare array and no `storage` key, so @nestjs/throttler falls back to its default in-process ThrottlerStorageService (an in-memory Map per Node process). I confirmed no Redis throttler adapter exists: package.json has ioredis ^5.8.1 and redis ^4.6.12, but those are used only by app.service.ts (health ping), entitlement-invalidation.bus.ts, and the socket.io redis-adapter — never by throttling. The custom MachineThrottlerGuard (app.module.ts:183, common/guards/machine-throttler.guard.ts) only overrides getTracker for per-principal tracking; it supplies no custom storage. So counts are genuinely per-process and reset on restart.

However, severity is overstated as "critical." The Nx-limit bypass requires a second replica, which does NOT exist today: docker-compose.prod.yml:62-73 defines backend as a single container (container_name kds_backend_prod, restart: always) with no deploy.replicas/scale; staging's `deploy:` blocks are CPU/memory resource limits only, not replica counts. On a single replica the throttle works exactly as intended; the "resets on restart" effect is real but low-impact (infrequent restarts, brief bypass window). This is a latent scalability/correctness hazard gated entirely behind a horizontal-scale config change the team hasn't made — a known footgun for the future, not a live production abuse vector. Note also: a separate already-documented infra finding (in MEMORY) is that ThrottlerModule has no `default` named tier, which would make the ~60 @Throttle({default:...}) overrides app-wide inert regardless of storage — that is a more impactful issue than the storage backend. The Redis service is already present in prod (docker-compose.prod.yml:39-55), so the recommended fix is feasible. Verdict: real but overstated → medium (raise to high only once a second replica is actually planned/configured).
findings.evidence: forRoot bare array no storage (app.module.ts:82-98) so it uses in-process ThrottlerStorageService; no redis dep; one container today (docker-compose.prod.yml:62-73) but scale-out planned.
findings.why_it_matters: Second replica means per-process counts: N replicas yield Nx the limit; resets on restart.
findings.recommendation: Add Redis-backed storage before a second replica.

findings.title: Tenant /auth/login has no account-lockout
findings.verify_reasoning:
The core technical claim is TRUE: the tenant /auth/login path has no account-lockout. Verified the User model in schema.prisma has no failedLogins/lockedUntil fields (those exist only on SuperAdmin lines 2390-2391 and MarketingUser lines 3443-3444), and password.service.ts validateUser (lines 98-153) only increments a Prometheus counter on a bad password and returns null — no per-account attempt tracking, no lock. auth.service.ts login (448-521) likewise only logs activity. Superadmin lockout is real (superadmin-auth.service.ts:191-220: 5 fails -> 30-min lock), so the asymmetry the finder describes is genuine.

The finder is also correct that the @Throttle(LOGIN_THROTTLE) on auth.controller.ts:40,131 is INERT: it uses { default: {...} } but ThrottlerModule (app.module.ts:82-98, @nestjs/throttler ^6.4.0) only registers throttlers named short/medium/long — there is no 'default' throttler, so the 5/min override silently no-ops. This is the same MEMORY-deferred infra bug.

Where the finding is OVERSTATED: the claim "the only brake is the inert LOGIN_THROTTLE" is not quite right. Because the named global throttlers (short=10/1s, medium=50/10s, long=100/60s) are applied app-wide via MachineThrottlerGuard as APP_GUARD and are NOT inert, /auth/login still gets a per-IP cap of ~100/min. So a single-IP brute force is bounded; the inert override only means the limit is 100/min instead of the intended 5/min. The real, unmitigated gap is precisely the one the finder names in "why it matters": NO per-account throttle, and the IP keying (MachineThrottlerGuard.getTracker returns the IP for credential traffic) means a distributed/botnet attacker can spread guesses across many IPs against one tenant email with no account-level brake. No nginx limit_req/limit_conn and no fail2ban exist (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf has nothing for /auth), so there is no compensating network-layer control.

Severity: the asymmetry vs superadmin and the absence of any per-account lock is real and exploitable for credential stuffing / targeted password spraying. But it is not unbounded from a single source (100/min/IP global cap applies), passwords are bcrypt-hashed, and exploitation requires either a weak target password or a distributed IP pool. That places it solidly at medium for a real-user prod context rather than high — high would be warranted if there were truly zero per-IP brake or if the global throttler were also inert (it is not). I'd raise the recommendation to ALSO fix the inert {default} throttler config, since that is the cheaper near-term win and currently leaves login at 100/min instead of 5/min.
findings.evidence: SuperAdmin has lockout (superadmin-auth.service.ts:191-322); tenant path has none; only brake is the inert LOGIN_THROTTLE.
findings.why_it_matters: No lockout plus per-IP keying lets attackers spread guesses across IPs against one tenant email.
findings.recommendation: Add per-account lockout mirroring superadmin.

findings.title: No per-tenant quotas; reports/analytics unthrottled
findings.evidence: IP-keyed throttling only; reports/analytics/z-reports controllers have zero @Throttle, only the global 100/min/IP on heavy aggregations.
findings.why_it_matters: A leaked token doing 100 aggregations/min can degrade shared DB/CPU for all tenants.
findings.recommendation: Add per-tenant quota and a tighter throttle on report/analytics endpoints.

summary: Configuration management is genuinely mature for a project of this scale: there is fail-fast boot validation that aborts on missing/short/non-distinct secrets and PayTR test-mode, a CI pre-flight gate that verifies every required GitHub Actions Secret before burning build time, per-deploy rendering of .env.production from those secrets shipped atomically over SSH, distinct STAGING_* secrets and PAYTR_TEST_MODE=1 for staging, and boot-time prod refusals in the KMS and SMS modules. There are essentially no weak hardcoded secret fallbacks in code paths that reach production. The dominant, unresolved problem is operational, not architectural: real, current production secrets (JWT_SECRET, JWT_REFRESH_SECRET, POSTGRES_PASSWORD, EMAIL_PASSWORD, DESKTOP_RELEASE_API_KEY) remain readable in the public git history and are confirmed un-rotated — a standing compromise that onboarding paying tenants makes materially worse. Secondary issues are a couple of config-as-shipped gaps (customer OTP SMS is silently mocked in prod) and minor template/compose drift.
strengths: Fail-fast boot validation: backend/src/main.ts:21 runs validateEnv() before Nest/Sentry init; backend/src/common/helpers/env-validation.ts enforces presence of DATABASE_URL + 5 CORE_SECRETS in every env, and in prod additionally enforces minLength>=32, cross-realm distinctness (distinctFrom), .env.example placeholder detection, and a hard-fail if PAYTR_TEST_MODE is unset/empty/"1" (env-validation.ts:165-175) so test-mode payments can never silently run against prod merchant creds.

CI secret-presence gate: .github/workflows/release-deploy.yml:82-149 validates every required production secret is non-empty BEFORE building images and refuses to deploy with a clean ::error:: list, separating hard-required from recommended (degraded) secrets — catches the same class env-validation.ts would, but earlier.

Secrets are sourced from GitHub Actions Secrets and rendered into .env.production fresh on every deploy (release-deploy.yml:327-437), shipped via an atomic scp-to-.new + mv rename (release-deploy.yml:441-457) so a truncated transfer can't leave a malformed env, and the rendered file is rm -f'd from the runner afterward (release-deploy.yml:552-554).

Real staging/prod secret separation: .github/workflows/test-deploy.yml uses distinct STAGING_JWT_SECRET / STAGING_SUPERADMIN_JWT_* / STAGING_ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY / STAGING_POSTGRES_PASSWORD and forces PAYTR_TEST_MODE=1 on staging, so a staging compromise does not yield prod tokens.

Defense-in-depth secret distinctness at module load: superadmin.module.ts:51-68 independently re-checks SUPERADMIN_JWT_SECRET is present, >=32 chars, and differs from the tenant JWT_SECRET; KmsModule (kms.module.ts:48-57) refuses to boot in prod without INTEGRATION_KEY/KMS_MASTER_KEY; SmsService (sms.service.ts:30-39) refuses silent mock-mode in prod unless ALLOW_MOCK_SMS_IN_PROD is explicitly set.

Access tokens are server-side revocable despite a long TTL: jwt.strategy.ts:78-84 checks payload.ver against User.tokenVersion on every request, so password-reset / lockout can invalidate all live sessions immediately.

gitignore root cause is fixed: /home/tarik/Projects/kds/.gitignore:18-28 ignores all real .env* and only un-ignores *.example/*.template/*.sample, with an explicit comment that the removed !.env.development/!.env.staging negations were how secrets originally leaked — preventing recurrence. backend/.env (real dev secrets) is confirmed untracked.
A written, prioritized remediation runbook exists at docs/SECURITY_LEAKED_SECRETS_RUNBOOK.md documenting exactly which commits/files/secrets leaked and the rotate-then-purge sequence.

findings.title: Current production secrets remain readable in public git history and are un-rotated
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every load-bearing claim is TRUE on current main, and the blocker severity is justified for a real-user production context.

1. Historical leak is real and still in public history. `git show 5d75ef44:.env.default.backup` returns the exact secrets quoted: JWT_SECRET=o9wiRcQp...XoZ6, JWT_REFRESH_SECRET=YDLmpU84...0UWU, POSTGRES_PASSWORD=wLCtzGnukqqnBvQqBFpo217EOVpvVjgT, EMAIL_PASSWORD=Merhabalar06., DESKTOP_RELEASE_API_KEY=35e6700c...a6cc. bc5d8667 (.env.test) likewise exists. Both commits are reachable on main.

2. Repo is genuinely exposed: `gh repo view mtarikucar/kds` returns isPrivate:false, visibility:PUBLIC. So anyone on the internet can clone and read history.

3. The auth bypass is technically valid, not theoretical. Tokens are signed/verified with HS256 (symmetric) using JWT_SECRET — token.service.ts lines 84-101/162-165 sign with algorithm "HS256"; jwt.strategy.ts lines 41-46 verify with algorithms:["HS256"], secretOrKey=JWT_SECRET. With the leaked symmetric secret an attacker forges a fully valid access token with arbitrary sub/role/tenantId/branch claims for any tenant. There is NO asymmetric/JWKS/kid/key-rotation mechanism anywhere in backend/src/modules/auth (grep for jwks|kid|RS256|publicKey|rotateKey = empty), so the leaked secret is the sole trust anchor.

4. The finder's pre-empted counter-argument is correct: tokenVersion does NOT mitigate forgery. jwt.strategy.ts lines 81-84 compare payload.ver to user.tokenVersion, and schema.prisma defaults tokenVersion to 0 — so a forged token with ver:0 (or omitted, treated as 0 per the code comment) passes the revocation check for any never-revoked user. The attacker controls ver and just matches it.

5. Runbook confirms status OPEN; project memory records user confirmed 2026-06-23 these are the current prod values with rotation deferred. The DB password Merhabalar06 also appears in the local backend/.env DATABASE_URL, corroborating it is live (JWT_SECRET locally is a dev placeholder, but production uses the leaked one per memory).

I looked for mitigations and found none: no IP allowlist on the JWT path, no second-factor on token validation, no rotation. The only partial nuance is that Postgres/email exploitation requires network reachability, but JWT forgery requires only an HTTP endpoint — which is internet-facing — so the full multi-tenant auth-bypass / account-takeover path is live and unauthenticated. Blocker is the right call: rotation (JWT_SECRET + JWT_REFRESH_SECRET especially) neutralizes the highest-impact vector independently of the history purge and should happen immediately.
findings.evidence: git history of the public repo mtarikucar/kds still contains commit 5d75ef44 (.env.default.backup) and bc5d8667 (.env.test). Verified by `git show`: both expose JWT_SECRET=o9wiRcQpuIP3SfClS7sfgde2r5T4K2lq7I1YZ00RaO7ybuaZQlrGO9uvXSUZXoZ6, JWT_REFRESH_SECRET=YDLmpU84K0kHncpjFKuOfL09vl0TKVKdLlGPcZzaOoQQz5P+2AlynQs+Hr+J0UWU, POSTGRES_PASSWORD=wLCtzGnukqqnBvQqBFpo217EOVpvVjgT, EMAIL_PASSWORD=Merhabalar06., DESKTOP_RELEASE_API_KEY=35e6700cc5d60cd9a3656e9a17c65f6b99360f87efd0cbdf1ffdc5e74ef7a6cc. The same DB password (Merhabalar06) appears in the local backend/.env DATABASE_URL. docs/SECURITY_LEAKED_SECRETS_RUNBOOK.md status is OPEN and project memory records the user confirmed (2026-06-23) these are STILL the current prod values, with rotation explicitly deferred.
findings.why_it_matters: Anyone on the internet can forge valid tenant AND refresh JWTs (HS256 with the leaked symmetric secret) for any user/role of any tenant — a full multi-tenant auth bypass and account/data takeover — and can read/decrypt outbound mail, and connect to Postgres if it is ever reachable. Onboarding real paying tenants turns a latent exposure into live customer-money/PII compromise the moment the repo is scraped. JWT forgery is not mitigated by tokenVersion (an attacker simply sets ver to the current value).
findings.recommendation: Rotate JWT_SECRET, JWT_REFRESH_SECRET, POSTGRES_PASSWORD (role + secret + DATABASE_URL), EMAIL_PASSWORD, and DESKTOP_RELEASE_API_KEY now via GitHub Actions Secrets and redeploy (rotating JWT logs everyone out — acceptable). Rotation alone neutralizes the exposure and can happen independently of history purge. Then purge history with git-filter-repo and re-private the repo per the runbook. Treat ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY/INTEGRATION_KEY/SUPERADMIN_* as also-rotate IF they were ever in any historical .env file.

findings.title: Customer OTP SMS is silently mocked in production (ALLOW_MOCK_SMS_IN_PROD=true)
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical mechanism is TRUE on main: .github/workflows/release-deploy.yml:409-421 renders ALLOW_MOCK_SMS_IN_PROD=true + SMS_PROVIDER=netgsm with empty NETGSM_USERCODE/PASSWORD/MSGHEADER (those are only in the 'recommended' list, lines 124-128, not 'required' 105-117). backend/src/modules/customers/sms.service.ts:30-39 is an explicit escape hatch that lets prod boot in mockMode; send() (lines 100-107) then no-ops and returns a fake success messageId. The silent-degradation is real and slightly WORSE than the boot guard intended: phone-verification.service.ts:126-138 returns {message:'Verification code sent to your phone'} success, and because isServiceEnabled() is false the failure warn is also suppressed.

HOWEVER the finder's IMPACT framing is materially overstated. It claims 'a functional outage for an entire login channel (customer phone-OTP login / QR-menu / self-pay)'. That is false: (1) The QR-menu customer session is anonymous — ordering and self-pay are NOT gated by OTP at all (customer-session.service / customer-public.controller @Public). There is no phone-OTP LOGIN channel; OTP only sets customer.phoneVerified. (2) Server-side, phoneVerified gates only referral-code application (referral.service.ts:144 throws ForbiddenException) and loyalty enrollment (per LoyaltyContent.tsx:58 comment) — not auth, not ordering, not payment. (3) Crucially the only frontend caller, frontend/src/components/qr-menu/PhoneVerification.tsx, is DEAD code — it is not imported or rendered anywhere (grep across frontend/src found zero mounts). So no current production UI flow even hits send-otp. (4) It is deliberate and documented: the release-deploy.yml comment says creds aren't ready, SMS is intentionally bypassed for launch, and the provider auto-activates with no code change once NETGSM_* secrets are set.

So: real config-driven silent failure, but the blast radius today is a non-rendered referral/loyalty nicety, not an authentication outage. The risk becomes meaningful only if/when the PhoneVerification UI is wired up before NETGSM_* secrets exist. The recommendation (set NETGSM_* and flip ALLOW_MOCK off / promote to required when the feature launches) is sound. Severity 'high' is over-stated for current real-user impact; medium is fair (latent footgun: a misleading success on a feature that is one UI-import away from being live).
findings.evidence: release-deploy.yml:412-421 renders ALLOW_MOCK_SMS_IN_PROD=true into the prod .env with empty NETGSM_USERCODE/PASSWORD/MSGHEADER (the pre-check lists NETGSM_* only as 'recommended', release-deploy.yml:124-128). sms.service.ts:19 sets mockMode=!provider; sms.service.ts:30-39 normally throws in prod to prevent silent OTP loss, but the escape hatch ALLOW_MOCK_SMS_IN_PROD=true bypasses that, so SmsService.send (sms.service.ts:100) no-ops/logs instead of delivering.
findings.why_it_matters: Any production flow that depends on a real SMS one-time code (customer phone-OTP login / verification on the QR-menu / self-pay path) silently never receives a code, so those customers cannot authenticate. This is a functional outage for an entire login channel that looks 'configured' (SMS_PROVIDER=netgsm) but delivers nothing — exactly the silent-degradation class the boot guard was written to prevent, re-enabled by config.
findings.recommendation: Before scaling, set the NETGSM_* GitHub Secrets so the provider auto-activates, and flip ALLOW_MOCK_SMS_IN_PROD off (or remove it) so the boot guard once again hard-fails if creds go missing. If OTP SMS truly isn't launched yet, ensure no production user-facing flow routes through SmsService, and promote NETGSM_* to 'required' in the pre-check once enabled.

findings.title: Prod compose defaults SUPERADMIN_JWT_SECRET to the tenant JWT_SECRET
findings.evidence: docker-compose.prod.yml:96 — `SUPERADMIN_JWT_SECRET: ${SUPERADMIN_JWT_SECRET:-${JWT_SECRET}}`. If the rendered .env.production ever lacks SUPERADMIN_JWT_SECRET, compose silently substitutes the tenant secret. The compose `environment:` block also does NOT pass SUPERADMIN_JWT_REFRESH_SECRET or ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY at all — those rely entirely on the env_file (.env.production) reaching the process.
findings.why_it_matters: Collapsing the superadmin realm onto the tenant secret would let a forged tenant token (already possible given the leak) also satisfy superadmin verification, defeating the realm isolation the codebase deliberately builds. Today it is caught — env-validation.ts:53 (distinctFrom) and superadmin.module.ts:68 both hard-fail in prod when the two are equal — so this is a latent footgun, not a live break, but the `:-${JWT_SECRET}` default is a dangerous pattern that papers over a missing secret with the worst possible value instead of failing.
findings.recommendation: Drop the `:-${JWT_SECRET}` fallback and use `${SUPERADMIN_JWT_SECRET:?SUPERADMIN_JWT_SECRET must be set}` (matching the CORS_ORIGIN/INTEGRATION_KEY :? style already used at docker-compose.prod.yml:97,119). Add SUPERADMIN_JWT_REFRESH_SECRET and ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY to the explicit environment: block too, so the container's required-secret set is visible in compose rather than only implied via env_file.

findings.title: Shipped env templates are drifted/incomplete and would fail boot
findings.evidence: backend/.env.production.template and the root .env.production.example/.env.production.template omit several secrets the boot validator requires: ENCRYPTION_MASTER_KEY, SUPERADMIN_JWT_SECRET, SUPERADMIN_JWT_REFRESH_SECRET, INTEGRATION_KEY (CORE_SECRETS in env-validation.ts:22-41), plus PayTR live vars and REDIS_PASSWORD. The root .env.production.template still advertises Stripe (STRIPE_*) which the system no longer uses, and .env.production.example shows PAYTR_TEST_MODE=false while the real rendered prod env uses PAYTR_TEST_MODE=0 (env-validation only accepts "0").
findings.why_it_matters: An operator standing up a new environment from any committed template would hit a boot abort (and an opaque PayTR_TEST_MODE error from `false`). The CI render path is the only correct source of truth; the templates can mislead disaster-recovery or a second-region bring-up. Low severity because the live deploy path does not consume these files.
findings.recommendation: Regenerate the committed templates from the authoritative release-deploy.yml render block: include every CORE_SECRET + PayTR + REDIS_PASSWORD, set PAYTR_TEST_MODE=0, and delete the dead Stripe section. Add a one-line note pointing operators to the CI render as the canonical list.

findings.title: Rendered .env.production REDIS_URL contradicts the compose-injected REDIS_URL
findings.evidence: release-deploy.yml:369 renders `REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379/0` (no password, DB index 0) into .env.production, but docker-compose.prod.yml:91 sets `REDIS_URL: redis://:${REDIS_PASSWORD}@redis:6379/2` in the service `environment:` block. Compose `environment:` overrides `env_file:`, so the backend actually runs with password + DB index 2; the env_file value is dead. Redis itself requires a password (docker-compose.prod.yml:47 `--requirepass`).
findings.why_it_matters: No live break today (the correct value wins), but the contradictory dead value is a maintenance trap: a future change that makes the backend read the env_file REDIS_URL (or a tool/script that reads .env.production directly, e.g. a maintenance one-liner) would connect unauthenticated to the wrong logical DB and silently fail or split state across DB 0/2. It also undermines the env file as a trustworthy record of runtime config.
findings.recommendation: Make the rendered REDIS_URL match the compose form (`redis://:${REDIS_PASSWORD}@redis:6379/2`) or remove REDIS_URL from the rendered file entirely and let compose be the single source. Pick one owner for REDIS_URL.

findings.title: DESKTOP_RELEASE_API_KEY is shared between staging and production
findings.evidence: test-deploy.yml:226 renders staging's key from the SAME secret as prod: `DESKTOP_RELEASE_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.DESKTOP_RELEASE_API_KEY }}` (all other staging core secrets use distinct STAGING_* secrets). This key is also one of the values leaked in public git history (see blocker).
findings.why_it_matters: A compromise of the lower-trust staging environment (or anyone who read the leaked history) yields the production desktop-release publishing key, which gates pushing POS desktop auto-update artifacts to real tenants — a supply-chain path to every desktop client. Sharing it removes the staging/prod blast-radius isolation the rest of the secret set carefully maintains.
findings.recommendation: Mint a distinct STAGING_DESKTOP_RELEASE_API_KEY for staging and rotate the prod key as part of the leaked-secret rotation. Ensure the desktop-release endpoint validates against the prod key only in prod.

findings.title: OTP hash secret falls back to empty string if JWT_SECRET/APP_SECRET are unset
findings.evidence: customers.helpers.ts:55 — `const secret = process.env.JWT_SECRET ?? process.env.APP_SECRET ?? "";` then sha256(`${secret}:${code}`). If both env vars were ever absent the OTP hash degrades to an unsalted sha256 of a 6-digit code.
findings.why_it_matters: An unsalted sha256 of a 6-digit OTP is trivially reversible from a DB dump (10^6 precomputable hashes), defeating the at-rest protection the function documents. In practice JWT_SECRET is a required, boot-validated secret so the empty branch is unreachable in a correctly-validated prod boot — hence low — but the silent empty-string fallback is the wrong failure mode for a security primitive.
findings.recommendation: Throw if neither secret is present (mirroring public-stats.service.ts:29-37 which deliberately refuses to fall back to JWT_SECRET and demands IP_HASH_SALT), or read the validated secret via ConfigService so it shares the boot guarantee. At minimum, remove the `?? ""`.

summary: The observability foundation is genuinely more mature than most early-stage SaaS: there is a real prom-client /metrics endpoint with constant-time-token auth, meaningful business metrics (payment_intents_total, webhook_delivery_total, auth_login_failures_total, outbox/delivery DLQ depth) that are actually wired into business code, an AsyncLocalStorage request-correlation context that flows into Sentry + Winston, a Sentry beforeSend that scrubs auth headers and PII, a fail-fast env validator, and a complete, well-documented Prometheus/Alertmanager rule set that pages on 5xx rate, app-down, DLQ depth, and money-path failures. However, several wiring gaps mean a lot of this does not actually function in production as deployed: the Winston structured JSON logger is NOT registered as the Nest app logger (227 services use the default plain-text Logger, so prod logs are unstructured), Sentry v10 is initialized AFTER AppModule import (breaking its auto-instrumentation/tracing), OpenTelemetry tracing is dead code (never bootstrapped), the entire Prometheus/Alertmanager stack is opt-in and referenced nowhere in deploy.sh or the prod compose (so almost certainly not running), METRICS_TOKEN is absent from prod templates while nginx proxies /api/metrics straight through (business metrics likely public), and SENTRY_DSN is optional with no boot warning. The alerting design is excellent on paper but is not realistically operating in prod, and there is no log shipping/retention beyond an ephemeral container filesystem.
strengths: Real application metrics, not just node defaults: MetricsService (backend/src/common/metrics/metrics.service.ts) exposes http_request_duration_seconds with bounded route-pattern cardinality plus business counters/gauges, and these are GENUINELY emitted from business code — verified payment_intents_total in payments.service.ts:380-388, webhook_delivery_total in webhook-delivery-worker.service.ts:46, auth_login_failures_total in password.service.ts:121/token.service.ts:273, and outbox/delivery DLQ gauges set/inc in outbox-worker.service.ts:153,348 and delivery-log.service.ts:176,288.

/metrics endpoint is auth-gated with a constant-time comparison (metrics.controller.ts:42-55 uses crypto.timingSafeEqual + length check) and Cache-Control: no-store, and the route-label cardinality is deliberately bounded to req.route patterns with an 'unmatched' bucket (request-logger.middleware.ts:83-90).

End-to-end request correlation: RequestContext (AsyncLocalStorage, common/context/request-context.ts) seeds a requestId (honoring inbound X-Request-Id), echoes it as the X-Request-Id response header, enriches Winston meta and is stamped onto Sentry events in beforeSend (sentry.config.ts:60-63) and carries tenantId/branchId/userId for multi-tenant triage.

Sentry beforeSend does real PII/secret hygiene: deletes authorization/cookie/x-api-key/x-webhook-signature/x-csrf-token headers and user email/ip/username, and redacts password/token/api_key query params (sentry.config.ts:65-109); the exception filter also deliberately omits user email from local logs (http-exception.filter.ts:297-324).

A complete, accurate, well-documented Prometheus + Alertmanager opt-in stack (ops/monitoring/*): alert.rules.yml pages on 5xx ratio >5%, up==0 (BackendDown), outbox/delivery DLQ depth, and money paths (PaytrPaymentFailures, WebhookDeliveryFailures); every PromQL expr references a metric that actually exists, sum() is used correctly for multi-target counter aggregation, and the README documents the status_code label gotcha and operator setup.

Fail-fast env validation (common/helpers/env-validation.ts) aborts boot on missing/short/placeholder secrets, cross-realm secret reuse, and PAYTR_TEST_MODE=1 in prod — pulling misconfig forward to deploy time.

Superadmin actions are audited to a structured AuditLog model (prisma schema model AuditLog with previousData/newData/metadata{ip,userAgent,requestId}), written by superadmin-audit.service.ts — the highest-risk admin surface is covered.

The exception filter wraps Sentry capture in try/catch so a Sentry outage can never mask the original 500 (http-exception.filter.ts:124-162), and global unhandledRejection/uncaughtException handlers forward to Sentry (main.ts:26-47).

findings.title: Structured Winston logger is NOT the app logger — production logs are unstructured plain text from 227 call sites
findings.verify_reasoning:
The core technical claim is TRUE on main. backend/src/main.ts:49-52 bootstraps with `NestFactory.create(AppModule, { bodyParser: false })` and never calls `app.useLogger(...)` nor sets `bufferLogs`. Grep confirms ZERO `useLogger`, `bufferLogs`, `ConsoleLogger`, or `setLogger` occurrences anywhere in src/. The Winston LoggerService (common/services/logger.service.ts) emits JSON when NODE_ENV=production (line 53 jsonFormat) and enriches every line via RequestContext (lines 125-181), but it is only instantiated/injected in production at 3 sites: the common.module provider (`useValue: new LoggerService("App")`), the HttpExceptionFilter, and the RequestLoggerMiddleware (HTTP access + finish lines). Every other service uses `private readonly logger = new Logger(<Name>)` from @nestjs/common — confirmed across payments, payment-finalizer, checkout-intent/settlement/notifications, z-reports, stock-alerts, caller, entitlements, local-bridge, etc. Those resolve to Nest's default ConsoleLogger, which (a) writes colorized plain text to stdout, NOT JSON, even in prod, and (b) does not read RequestContext, so it cannot carry requestId/tenantId even though the middleware seeds the AsyncLocalStorage context for the request. So the substance — the vast majority of service-layer logs are unstructured and uncorrelated in prod, defeating per-tenant/per-request incident tracing — is correct, and the fix (bufferLogs + app.useLogger(app.get(LoggerService)), or nestjs-pino reading RequestContext) is exactly right.

Two nuances that keep this at high rather than higher: (1) The "227 call sites" figure is overstated — actual `new Logger(` count is 144 (the 227 may have counted the consuming `this.logger.*` invocations). This is a magnitude error, not a substance error. (2) Not ALL trace capability is inert: the HTTP access log and the global exception filter DO route through Winston with requestId enrichment, so there is a partial correlation backbone at the request boundary and on errors. But the per-step service-layer breadcrumbs (the money/fiscal/order/payment flow logs operators actually need to reconstruct a multi-tenant incident) are plain text without correlation ids. No mitigation found in config/ConfigModule, guards, nginx, or compose. Severity high is justified for a real multi-tenant prod SaaS: structured-prod-logging is largely inert and cross-service per-tenant tracing is not possible from the service logs — though it is an observability/operability gap, not a data-integrity or security defect, so high (not critical) is correct.
findings.evidence: backend/src/main.ts never calls app.useLogger(); grep for useLogger across src returns nothing. The Winston LoggerService (common/services/logger.service.ts) emits JSON in prod (line 53), but it is injected in only 3 places (common.module.ts, http-exception.filter.ts, request-logger.middleware.ts). All 227 other loggers are `new Logger(...)` from @nestjs/common (verified: 227 matches), which write Nest's default colorized plain-text to stdout and bypass Winston entirely — they carry no requestId/tenantId enrichment and are not JSON.
findings.why_it_matters: The vast majority of service-layer logs (orders, payments, checkout, fiscal, kds, subscriptions) are unstructured text with no correlation id, so they cannot be filtered/joined by requestId/tenantId in any log backend. During a multi-tenant incident an operator cannot pull one tenant's or one request's trace across services — exactly when it matters most. The JSON-prod-logging investment is largely inert.
findings.recommendation: Bootstrap the app with the Winston logger: const app = await NestFactory.create(AppModule, { bufferLogs: true }); then app.useLogger(app.get(LoggerService)) (or use nestjs-pino). Ensure the chosen logger reads RequestContext so every default Logger line carries requestId/tenantId.

findings.title: Sentry v10 initialized AFTER AppModule is imported — auto-instrumentation/tracing is effectively broken
findings.verify_reasoning:
The core technical claim is TRUE on main. backend/src/main.ts imports AppModule at line 4 (and @nestjs/core, express, body-parser above it), and only calls initSentry() at line 23. ESM import hoisting + webpack single-bundle evaluation (nest-cli.json "webpack": true; webpack.config.js) means the entire AppModule tree — PrismaService, the NestJS Express HTTP adapter, express — is fully loaded before initSentry() runs. Sentry v10 (@sentry/node ^10.32.1) auto-instrumentation is OpenTelemetry-based and must monkey-patch modules at require-time, so the prismaIntegration() DB-query spans and any auto HTTP/express request spans are not reliably installed. I confirmed there is NO mitigation: no instrument.js, no `node --import` (Dockerfile prod CMD is plain `node dist/main`), no @sentry/nestjs dependency, and no Sentry.setupExpressErrorHandler/setupNestErrorHandler anywhere (only @sentry/node is installed). beforeSendTransaction's slow_query tag depends on those auto-spans, so it is largely dead. So far the finder is correct.

However the severity/impact is OVERSTATED. Two things still work and the finding underplays them: (1) error capture via the manual captureException in the exception filter + the process unhandledRejection/uncaughtException handlers works fine. (2) More importantly, the manual tracing helpers withTransaction/withSpan in common/utils/tracing.ts call Sentry.startSpan, which works WITHOUT auto-instrumentation because Sentry.init() still runs before bootstrap()/app.listen(). These manual spans are wired onto the actual money paths (payments.service.ts lines 351/1161/1625, orders.service.ts line 230). So the codebase's hand-instrumented performance traces on payment/order flows DO produce data — only the AUTO HTTP-request spans and AUTO Prisma-query spans are broken. The finder's "performance traces ... largely won't be produced" is therefore too strong.

Net: this is real observability degradation (you lose request-scoped + DB-query auto context, and the slow_query tagging is dead), but it is not user-facing, not a data-integrity or security issue, and partial telemetry remains. For a real-user production context this is a medium, not a high. The recommendation (separate instrument file imported first / node --import, plus @sentry/nestjs SentryModule + setupExpressErrorHandler) is correct.
findings.evidence: backend/src/main.ts imports AppModule at line 4 (and @nestjs/core, express, body-parser, etc. above it) and only calls initSentry() at line 23. sentry.config.ts uses @sentry/node ^10.32.1 (package.json:46) with nodeProfilingIntegration + Sentry.prismaIntegration() (sentry.config.ts:32-36). Sentry v8+/v10 requires Sentry.init() to run BEFORE any instrumented module is required (docs mandate an --import ./instrument or top-of-entry init). There is also no Sentry.setupExpressErrorHandler(app) / @sentry/nestjs SentryModule anywhere (grep returns nothing).
findings.why_it_matters: Because Prisma/HTTP/Express are imported before init, the v10 auto-instrumentation cannot patch them, so prismaIntegration slow-query tags, performance traces, and request-scoped spans largely won't be produced. Error capture via the manual captureException in the filter still works, but the 'performance monitoring + DB query monitoring' the config advertises is mostly dead — operators get errors but not the latency/trace context they think they have.
findings.recommendation: Move Sentry.init into a separate file imported as the very first line of main.ts (or via node --import ./dist/instrument.js), per @sentry/node v10 setup; add @sentry/nestjs SentryModule + Sentry.setupNestErrorHandler/setupExpressErrorHandler so tracing and the request handler are wired.

findings.title: Prometheus/Alertmanager stack is opt-in and not wired into deploy — alerting almost certainly NOT running in prod
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claims are all TRUE on main. (1) docker-compose.monitoring.yml exists at repo root and is referenced NOWHERE in deploy.sh, docker-compose.prod.yml, or docker-compose.staging.yml — my grep for monitoring|prometheus|alertmanager across those three returned zero hits, and a broader grep including CI workflows also found nothing wiring the Prometheus/Alertmanager stack. (2) ops/monitoring/README.md:1-8 and the "Run it"/"Scope/non-goals" sections (lines 60-114) plus prometheus.yml:1-22 explicitly describe it as an OPT-IN, operator-bootstrapped stack ("Adds nothing to and edits nothing in docker-compose.prod.yml"). (3) alertmanager.yml:48-56 ships a placeholder webhook receiver (url: "__ALERT_WEBHOOK_URL__") with no committed real target; the default in compose is http://localhost:5001/ (a no-op). So the metric-based alert rules (BackendDown, HighHttp5xxRate, OutboxDLQNonEmpty, PaytrPaymentFailures, WebhookDeliveryFailures) genuinely only fire if a human ran the separate stack on the prod host AND set ALERT_WEBHOOK_URL. That part of the finding is correct and unmitigated.

NUANCE the finder missed / overstated: there IS a parallel observability path that IS wired into the deploy pipeline — Sentry. release-deploy.yml (lines 562-608) creates Sentry releases/deploys and uploads sourcemaps for backend/frontend/landing, and backend/src/main.ts:12,23 calls initSentry() at boot and captures unhandledRejection/uncaughtException (lines 26-47). sentry.config.ts gates on SENTRY_DSN, which is supplied via env_file: .env.production (docker-compose.prod.yml:63), i.e. operator-set, not in repo. So crashes/uncaught exceptions are plausibly captured and Sentry can alert on error spikes/new issues. The finding's framing that essentially nothing is watched is therefore overstated — app-crash visibility likely exists.

BUT Sentry does NOT cover the specific signals the finding names: a growing outbox/delivery DLQ, payment_intents_total{result="paytr_failed"} rate, webhook_delivery_total{result="failure"} rate, 5xx-ratio-over-threshold, or up==0 (BackendDown). Those are metric/counter conditions that typically do NOT throw exceptions, so Sentry is blind to them. Those money-path/reliability alerts are genuinely unmonitored unless the Prometheus stack was manually started. Also, both Sentry and the metrics stack are opt-in-by-operator-env, so neither is guaranteed running.

Net: the core defect is real (metric-based alerting not shipped with deploy, no committed scrape target or receiver) but the severity is overstated. This is an operational wiring/observability gap one `docker compose -f docker-compose.monitoring.yml up` away, with crash-level visibility already plausibly covered by Sentry — not a code defect, data-loss, or money-math bug. For a real-paying-tenant context the unmonitored DLQ/payment-failure/backend-down conditions are a legitimate reliability gap, which keeps it at medium rather than low.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.monitoring.yml exists at repo root but is referenced nowhere in deploy.sh or docker-compose.prod.yml (grep for 'monitoring|prometheus|alertmanager' in deploy.sh + prod/staging compose returns nothing). ops/monitoring/README.md:1-8 and prometheus.yml:2-4 explicitly call it an opt-in stack the operator must bootstrap manually with docker compose -f docker-compose.monitoring.yml up. alertmanager.yml ships a placeholder receiver (__ALERT_WEBHOOK_URL__) with no committed target.
findings.why_it_matters: All the excellent alert rules (5xx>5%, BackendDown, DLQ non-empty, PaytrPaymentFailures, WebhookDeliveryFailures) only fire if a human remembered to run the separate stack on the prod host and set ALERT_WEBHOOK_URL. As the system onboards real paying tenants, a payment-failure storm, an app-down event, or a growing outbox DLQ will produce metrics that nobody is scraping and no alert that anybody receives — silent failure of the money paths. There is no evidence this stack is actually deployed.
findings.recommendation: Either fold the prometheus+alertmanager services into the prod compose / deploy.sh so they ship with every deploy, or document+verify a standing managed alternative (Grafana Cloud agent, hosted Prometheus). Confirm on the prod host that a scrape target is UP and ALERT_WEBHOOK_URL points at a real on-call channel; add a synthetic 'always-firing' watchdog alert to prove the pipe works.

findings.title: /api/metrics likely PUBLIC in prod: METRICS_TOKEN absent from prod templates and nginx proxies it straight through
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every technical claim holds on main (only the cited path was slightly off: the controller lives at backend/src/common/metrics/metrics.controller.ts, not backend/src/metrics/).

1) Open-by-default auth: metrics.controller.ts:42-44 — assertAuthorized() reads METRICS_TOKEN and `if (!token) return;` (no auth). The @Public()+@ApiExcludeEndpoint() decorators (lines 33-34) confirm the global JWT guard is bypassed. The controller's own header comment (lines 22-24) admits that when the token is unset, deployments MUST keep /api/metrics off the public ingress.

2) Token not required/seeded in prod: env.validation.ts:65-67 declares METRICS_TOKEN as @IsOptional @IsString — never enforced in prod. grep across backend/.env.production.template, backend/.env.staging.template, .env.production.template, and .env.production.example returns NOTHING for METRICS_TOKEN — so an operator following the templates would never set it (the only METRICS_TOKEN references are in docker-compose.monitoring.yml and ops/monitoring/*, i.e. the Prometheus side, not the backend). docker-compose.prod.yml's backend `environment:` block (lines 80-126) does not set METRICS_TOKEN either; it could in theory live in the off-repo .env.production, but nothing prompts that.

3) Ingress proxies it through: ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:44-46 is `location /api/ { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000; }` with no allow/deny; there is no `location = /api/metrics` block anywhere (grep for "metrics" in ops/nginx returns nothing). Staging conf (staging.hummytummy.com.conf:51) is identical.

4) The exposed series are exactly the sensitive ones claimed — metrics.service.ts registers outbox_dlq_depth + delivery_dlq_depth gauges and an incCounter() registry, and the codebase emits payment_intents_total, payment_intents_outcome_total, subscription_billing_total, auth_login_failures_total, cash_drawer_ops_total, fiscal_receipts_issued_total + default http_request_duration histograms.

Aggravating factor the finder missed: docker-compose.prod.yml:127-128 binds the backend as `'3000:3000'` (no 127.0.0.1: prefix), so the backend — and thus /api/metrics — is reachable directly on the host's public IP:3000 in addition to nginx:443. So even an nginx deny rule alone would not fully close it without a host firewall.

No mitigation exists elsewhere (no guard, no nginx deny, no required env, no localhost-only bind).

Severity: this is a genuine unauthenticated business-intelligence/recon disclosure (order/payment/fiscal/cash volumes, login-attack visibility, DLQ depth) plus a registry-serialization availability nibble. But it is information-disclosure only — no PII, no auth bypass, no integrity/financial-loss vector — and the live exposure is contingent on the deployed (off-repo) .env.production not setting the token, which is likely-but-unproven. That places it solidly at HIGH rather than CRITICAL. The fix is correct: set METRICS_TOKEN in the prod/staging templates + require it in env-validation for prod, AND add `location = /api/metrics { allow <monitoring-net>; deny all; }` to the nginx confs, AND bind the backend port to 127.0.0.1 (or firewall 3000).
findings.evidence: metrics.controller.ts:42-44 returns early (no auth) when METRICS_TOKEN is unset. METRICS_TOKEN does not appear in backend/.env.production.template or .env.production.example (grep returns nothing) and is not validated in env-validation.ts. ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:44-46 proxies all `location /api/` (including /api/metrics) to the backend with no allow/deny — the controller's own comment (metrics.controller.ts:22-24) warns that when the token is unset 'deployments must then keep /api/metrics off the public ingress', which nginx does not do.
findings.why_it_matters: If METRICS_TOKEN is unset in prod (the templates give no reason to set it), anyone can GET https://hummytummy.com/api/metrics and read the full Prometheus exposition: per-route request rates/latencies, payment_intents_total, subscription_billing_total, auth_login_failures_total, cash_drawer_ops_total, fiscal_receipts_issued_total, outbox/delivery DLQ depth. That is a business-intelligence and reconnaissance leak (order/payment volumes, login-attack visibility, fiscal counts) to any unauthenticated visitor, and an availability risk (the endpoint serializes the whole registry on each call).
findings.recommendation: Add METRICS_TOKEN (32+ chars) to the prod/staging env templates and require it in prod via env-validation, OR add `location = /api/metrics { deny all; }` (allow only the monitoring network) to the nginx confs. Prefer both. Verify with an unauthenticated curl to the prod URL.

findings.title: SENTRY_DSN is optional with no boot warning — error tracking can be silently disabled in prod
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every technical claim checks out on current main (file paths differ slightly from the finder's report — real paths are backend/src/common/helpers/env-validation.ts and backend/src/sentry.config.ts — but content matches).

1) env-validation.ts:57 declares `{ key: "SENTRY_DSN", required: false, prodOnly: true }`. The `prodOnly` flag is inert here: the validation loop (lines 100-108) only pushes a missing-var error when `rule.required` is true. With `required:false`, prod boots cleanly without SENTRY_DSN and never even emits a warning (the warnings array at 136-141 is for CORE_SECRETS length, unrelated). Confirmed.

2) sentry.config.ts:13-16 — when DSN is absent it `console.log("⚠️ Sentry DSN not configured - error tracking disabled")` and returns. It is console.LOG (info level), not warn/error, so it blends into normal boot noise. captureException/captureMessage then become no-ops (Sentry.init never ran). Confirmed.

3) http-exception.filter.ts:124-147 funnels every 5xx (statusCode>=500 && Error) through captureException. With no DSN this is a silent no-op — the catch block there only fires if captureException THROWS, which it won't when Sentry is uninitialized. So all server-error reporting goes dark. Confirmed.

4) SENTRY_DSN is present only in backend/.env.example (empty, lines 117-122) and absent from backend/.env.production.template and .env.staging.template (grep confirmed). So an operator copying the prod template has no prompt to set it. Confirmed.

No mitigation exists elsewhere: no startup banner, no readiness/health gate on Sentry, no nginx/compose default. The EMAIL_* precedent the finder cites is real (lines 89-91 make EMAIL_HOST/USER/PASSWORD `required:true, prodOnly:true`), proving the team already has the exact pattern to make a prod-critical integration fail-fast and simply did not apply it to Sentry. The recommendation (make required in prod OR loud warn + add to prod template) is sound and cheap.

Severity nuance for a real-user prod context: this is a genuine observability-visibility gap, not a security/data-loss/money defect. The app keeps working; impact is contingent — it only bites during an incident, and access logs + structured service logs (the requestId stamping at sentry.config.ts:60 and filter logError) provide a partial fallback signal, so operators are not 100% blind. That contingent, partially-compensable nature plus the fact that it's a single observability control makes "high" slightly overstated. "medium" is the right calibration: a real degradation-of-incident-response risk worth fixing, but lower blast radius than a hard crash/exposure. The compounding with the other two logging/metrics findings is a fair point but each should be rated on its own.
findings.evidence: env-validation.ts:57 declares SENTRY_DSN as { required: false, prodOnly: true } so prod boots cleanly without it. sentry.config.ts:13-16 just console.log('Sentry DSN not configured - error tracking disabled') and returns. SENTRY_DSN appears only in backend/.env.example (empty value) and is absent from backend/.env.production.template (grep). The exception filter funnels ALL 5xx capture through Sentry (http-exception.filter.ts:124-147) — if the DSN is missing, captureException is a no-op.
findings.why_it_matters: If a deploy ships without SENTRY_DSN set, the entire crash-reporting pipeline goes dark while everything looks healthy at boot. Combined with finding #3 (no Prometheus running) and finding #1 (unstructured logs), a prod incident could leave operators with essentially no actionable error signal — they'd learn of outages from customers.
findings.recommendation: Either make SENTRY_DSN required in prod (mirror the EMAIL_* prod-required rationale already in env-validation.ts:83-91), or at minimum emit a loud console.warn / startup banner when prod boots without it. Add it to the prod env template with a comment.

findings.title: OpenTelemetry distributed tracing is dead code — never bootstrapped
findings.evidence: common/observability/tracing.ts exports bootstrapTracing() and withSpan(), and docker-compose.prod.yml:122-126 even passes OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT/OTEL_SERVICE_NAME. But bootstrapTracing() is never imported or called (grep for 'initTracing|observability/tracing|bootstrapTracing' across src returns only a comment reference in metrics.service.ts:16); main.ts never invokes it. The SDK packages are also lazy-required and listed as not-installed (tracing.ts:87-94).
findings.why_it_matters: The repo advertises an OTel tracing path (and prod compose plumbs the env vars) that physically cannot activate — setting OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT in prod does nothing because the bootstrap is never run. An operator who provisions a collector expecting traces will get silence, and there is no real distributed tracing across the device-mesh/delivery integrations.
findings.recommendation: If tracing is desired, call await bootstrapTracing() as the first statement in main.ts (before Nest create) and add the OTel packages; otherwise delete tracing.ts and the OTEL_* compose vars to avoid implying a capability that isn't wired.

findings.title: File logs write to an ephemeral container path with no volume or shipping — logs lost on every deploy/restart
findings.evidence: logger.service.ts:22 writes DailyRotateFile to join(process.cwd(),'logs') (i.e. /app/logs) when prod or LOG_TO_FILE=true; backend/.env.production.template:15 sets LOG_TO_FILE=true. docker-compose.prod.yml mounts only invoice_storage:/app/storage and uploads_storage:/app/uploads (lines 129-131) — there is NO /app/logs volume (grep 'logs' in prod compose returns nothing). No log shipper is present anywhere (grep for loki/promtail/fluent/vector/filebeat returns nothing).
findings.why_it_matters: Even the structured file logs (error/combined/access, retained 14d per the rotate config) live inside the container's writable layer and are destroyed on every redeploy (which is per-change/frequent here) and every restart. There is no central log store, so post-incident forensics for a tenant dispute, a payment discrepancy, or a security event is impossible after the next deploy — and these are unstructured anyway given finding #1.
findings.recommendation: Add a named volume for /app/logs in the prod compose (so logs survive container recreation) and/or ship stdout to a managed log backend (Loki/Grafana Cloud/CloudWatch). Given finding #1, prefer shipping structured JSON stdout rather than relying on the in-container files.

findings.title: Exception-filter requestId is a fresh random value, not the request's correlation id — breaks trace stitching
findings.evidence: http-exception.filter.ts:31 calls this.generateRequestId() which is `${Date.now()}-${Math.random()...}` (lines 345-347), and this value is returned to the client in errorResponse.requestId (line 102) AND used in the Sentry context (lines 126-147) and the error log (line 309). The real correlation id minted by the request-logger and set as the X-Request-Id response header comes from RequestContext.getRequestId() (request-logger.middleware.ts:47-51), which the filter ignores entirely.
findings.why_it_matters: A 5xx response shows the customer/support one requestId, the X-Request-Id header shows a different one, the access log line shows the header's id, and Sentry's tags.requestId (stamped from RequestContext in sentry.config.ts:60) differs again from the error's own logged id. When a tenant reports 'error id 1718...-abc' an operator cannot find the matching access log or the matching Sentry event — defeating the whole correlation system on exactly the error path where it's needed.
findings.recommendation: In http-exception.filter.ts use RequestContext.getRequestId() (falling back to (req as any).requestId) instead of generateRequestId(), so the response body, the X-Request-Id header, the error log, and Sentry's tag all share one id.

findings.title: Access logs persist client IP + user-agent on every request with no IP anonymization
findings.evidence: request-logger.middleware.ts:54 logs ip + userAgent for every inbound request via the http level, and 65-73 logs the response line; with LOG_TO_FILE=true these land in access-%DATE%.log retained 7d and combined-%DATE%.log retained 14d (logger.service.ts:88-98, 76-85). The filter already deliberately strips email to reduce KVKK surface (http-exception.filter.ts:297-307), but raw client IP (PII under GDPR/KVKK) is retained unmasked in access logs.
findings.why_it_matters: For a Turkish/EU-facing B2B SaaS, full client IP in retained logs is regulated PII. The team clearly cares (email is scrubbed from logs and Sentry), but the access-log IP retention is an inconsistent gap that could matter for a KVKK/GDPR data-subject or audit request.
findings.recommendation: Either truncate/anonymize the logged IP (e.g. drop the last octet / hash with a rotating salt) in the request logger, or document the IP retention + 7/14d expiry as an accepted, time-bounded lawful-basis decision. Low urgency but worth a deliberate call.

findings.title: Per-process DLQ gauges + per-replica registries make some metrics inconsistent under multiple backend replicas
findings.evidence: MetricsService holds a private per-process Registry (metrics.service.ts:24) and the outbox DLQ gauge is bumped with incOutboxDlqDepth() inline on a single replica (outbox-worker.service.ts:348) while the authoritative setOutboxDlqDepth(COUNT) only runs on whichever replica's hourly prune cron fires (outbox-worker.service.ts:153). prometheus.yml:59-66 defines a single static target. The 5xx/latency counters are fine (Prometheus sum()s per-target series), but gauge semantics and a single static target are single-instance assumptions.
findings.why_it_matters: If the backend is scaled to >1 replica (the codebase already adds a Redis Socket.IO adapter for exactly this, main.ts:220-228), Prometheus scraping one static target will see only one replica's view, and the DLQ gauge will reflect only the replica whose cron last ran — under-reporting the true DLQ depth and potentially suppressing the OutboxDLQNonEmpty alert. The DLQ is a money/data-loss signal.
findings.recommendation: When running multiple replicas, scrape every replica (service-discovery or per-pod targets) and aggregate DLQ gauges with max()/sum() by series in the rule, or compute DLQ depth from a single source of truth (DB COUNT exposed by one designated instance / a pushgateway). Document the single-replica assumption until then.

summary: The codebase shows genuine, above-average multi-replica engineering intent: Socket.IO has a real Redis pub/sub adapter wired in main.ts, the entitlement in-process cache has a Redis-backed cross-replica invalidation bus, and 14 of 15 scheduled jobs acquire a Postgres advisory lock to prevent double execution. However, the system is CURRENTLY deployed single-instance (nginx proxies /api, /uploads and /socket.io to one 127.0.0.1:3000 backend; prod compose runs one backend container) and several real blockers would bite the moment a 2nd replica is added — exactly the scale-out this infrastructure is built to enable. The two material blockers are: (1) the advisory-lock helper acquires and releases pg_try_advisory_lock on SEPARATE Prisma $queryRawUnsafe calls that the multi-connection pool routes to different connections, so the session-scoped lock is leaked on the wrong connection and locked crons (including subscription billing) can wedge permanently; and (2) user uploads (product images, tenant logos) are written to a host-local Docker volume with no shared object storage, so behind a load balancer half the asset requests 404. A subtler, already-active correctness bug independent of scale: every @Throttle({ default: ... }) decorator (~49 sites, including login/register/forgot-password brute-force limits) is INERT because no registered throttler is named "default" — they silently fall back to the lax global 100/min limit. Plus the throttler uses in-memory storage, so even the working global limits multiply by replica count.
