strengths: Socket.IO Redis adapter is genuinely wired for prod, not aspirational: main.ts:240-243 constructs RedisIoAdapter and calls app.useWebSocketAdapter; common/adapters/redis-io.adapter.ts connects pub/sub clients and installs createAdapter so server.to('<room>').emit() fans out across replicas. REDIS_URL is set in docker-compose.prod.yml:91. All gateway emits use room-scoped .to() (kds.gateway.ts, notifications.gateway.ts, analytics.gateway.ts), which the adapter handles correctly cross-replica.

All three WebSocket gateways broadcast via Redis-replicated rooms (pos-/kitchen-/user:/tenant:/customer-session- rooms) rather than iterating an in-memory socket registry for fan-out — so realtime order/payment/notification events reach clients on any replica once the adapter is active.

Entitlement in-process cache (entitlement.service.ts:37, 30s TTL) is paired with a real Redis pub/sub cross-replica invalidation bus (entitlement-invalidation.bus.ts) so a plan change on Pod A drops the stale cache on Pod B within milliseconds; the cache is also bounded (MAX_CACHE_SIZE 10k + periodic sweep) so it won't grow unbounded per replica.

14 of 15 scheduled jobs are guarded by a shared withAdvisoryLock helper or an equivalent inline pg_try_advisory_lock (subscription billing crons, reservations, delivery reconciliation/retry/polling/token-refresh, stock alerts, z-report, device-mesh sweep, marketplace sweeper, plan projector, public-stats, customer-session, self-pay sweeper/recovery, outbox worker) — the intent to avoid double-charge/double-email/double-fanout across replicas is consistently applied.

Public-stats and the geolocation cache are persisted to the DB / recomputed by an advisory-locked cron and read from the DB (public-stats.service.ts toPublicView reads DB), so the in-memory caches there are per-replica accelerators, not sources of truth — no cross-replica divergence of served data.

Invoice PDF serving is self-healing across replicas: invoice.controller.ts:63-67 regenerates the PDF on local-disk miss before reading, so the host-local invoice_storage volume does not cause 404s behind an LB.

app.enableShutdownHooks() (main.ts) plus Prisma/Redis onModuleDestroy give clean connection drain on rolling restarts; trust proxy is configured (main.ts:60-66) so per-IP throttle tracking and audit IPs see the real client behind the LB.

findings.title: Advisory-lock acquire and release run on different pooled Prisma connections — the session-scoped lock is leaked, and every locked cron (incl. subscription billing) can wedge permanently under any multi-connection pool
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical core is TRUE on current main. advisory-lock.ts:27-40 runs pg_try_advisory_lock and pg_advisory_unlock as two separate prisma.$queryRawUnsafe calls, NOT in a $transaction and NOT using pg_advisory_xact_lock; the same two-call pattern is duplicated verbatim in subscription-scheduler.service.ts:60-74 (withJobLock) and inline in stock-alerts.scheduler.ts:38/75, z-report-scheduler.service.ts:33/55, token-refresh.scheduler.ts:24/36, order-polling.scheduler.ts:51/59. pg_advisory_lock IS session-scoped, so an unlock landing on a different pooled connection than the acquire is a no-op and leaks the lock. I confirmed the pool is multi-connection: PrismaService is a plain singleton PrismaClient with only datasources.url set (no datasourceUrl pinning, no pgbouncer), and NO DATABASE_URL anywhere carries connection_limit (backend/.env:8, docker-compose.prod.yml:90, .env.production.template:21) — so Prisma uses its default num_cpus*2+1 pool. No mitigation exists in prisma/, common/scheduling/, schema.prisma, compose, or env. So the bug is real and there is no missed mitigation.

Where I downgrade from critical to high: (1) The finder's framing as a multi-replica concern is moot today — prod runs a SINGLE backend instance (docker-compose.prod.yml:62 container_name kds_backend_prod, restart: always, NO deploy.replicas). The bug is genuinely a single-instance footgun (which the WHY-IT-MATTERS correctly notes), but the title/helper-comments' "another replica" story is not the active risk. (2) The claim "the cron stops running after the first tick" overstates determinism. Whether the wedge triggers depends on connection ROUTING: Prisma frequently reuses the most-recently-returned connection, and at 02:00 the billing cron is often the only DB activity, so the unlock commonly DOES land back on the acquiring connection and succeeds. The leak is intermittent/probabilistic, not guaranteed-every-tick, and Prisma's idle-connection recycling plus any process restart self-heals a leaked lock. (3) Failure mode is fail-SILENT-SKIP, not corruption: a leaked lock causes a skipped cron tick (no double-charge, no data loss). The worst realistic outcome is the WHY-IT-MATTERS scenario — subscription billing/trial-expiry/past-due silently stops for an unknown window until a deploy/restart — which is a real revenue/correctness risk and justifies high, but the probabilistic trigger + self-heal-on-restart + single-instance reality keep it below blocker/critical. The recommendation (pg_advisory_xact_lock inside an interactive $transaction with run() using the same tx, or a pinned client, plus a multi-tick reacquire test) is correct and is the right fix. NOTE: this exactly matches a previously-known DEFERRED infra finding in project memory ("withAdvisoryLock acquire/release on different pooled connections → session-scoped lock leaks → EVERY advisory-locked cron can silently stall") — independently re-confirmed here, still unfixed on main.
findings.evidence: common/scheduling/advisory-lock.ts:27-40 runs `SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(...)` and `SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(...)` as two SEPARATE prisma.$queryRawUnsafe calls, NOT inside a $transaction and NOT using pg_advisory_xact_lock. The same pattern is duplicated in subscription-scheduler.service.ts:60-74 (withJobLock) and inline in stock-alerts.scheduler.ts:38/75, z-report-scheduler.service.ts:33/55, token-refresh.scheduler.ts:24/36, order-polling.scheduler.ts:51/59. Prisma routes each $queryRawUnsafe to a pooled connection independently; the pool is multi-connection (no connection_limit in any DATABASE_URL — docker-compose.prod.yml:90, backend/.env:8 — so Prisma defaults to num_cpus*2+1). pg_advisory_lock is session-scoped: the acquire holds it on connection C1; the unlock on C2 is a no-op (returns false), and C1 returns to the pool still holding the lock. Subsequent ticks calling pg_try_advisory_lock from a different pooled connection see the lock held and return false, so the cron skips forever; reentry only works if the same connection is reused.
findings.why_it_matters: Even on a SINGLE instance the lock can be silently leaked and the cron stop running after the first tick (it is not purely a multi-replica concern). Affected jobs include money- and lifecycle-critical ones: subscription period-end billing (`0 2 * * *`), trial expirations, past-due handling, scheduled downgrades, delivery reconciliation, stock alerts. A wedged billing cron means subscriptions silently stop being charged/renewed/expired with no error — a slow, invisible revenue and correctness failure precisely as paying tenants are onboarded.
findings.recommendation: Make acquire+work+release run on one guaranteed connection. Simplest correct fix: use a session-bound transaction with pg_advisory_xact_lock (auto-released at tx end, no manual unlock, no cross-connection risk): `await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { const [{locked}] = await tx.$queryRawUnsafe('SELECT pg_try_advisory_xact_lock($1) AS locked', lockId); if (!locked) return; await run(); })` — but note run() must then use the same tx, or use a dedicated pinned client. Alternatively run the helper inside `$transaction([...])` interactive mode and keep pg_try_advisory_lock/unlock both on the tx client. Add a multi-tick test that asserts the second invocation still acquires.

findings.title: User uploads (product images, tenant logos) are written to a host-local Docker volume with no shared object storage — behind a load balancer a fraction of asset GETs 404 and uploads are non-portable across replicas
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every technical fact in the finding checks out on current main:

1. upload.service.ts writes to host-local disk: uploadLogo does `await fs.writeFile(filePath, ...)` at line 115 under path.resolve(process.cwd(),'uploads')/logos; uploadProductImage does the same at line 223 under uploads/products/{tenantId}. uploadsRoot = path.resolve(process.cwd(),'uploads') (line 29). No object-storage write path anywhere; URLs are built as `${baseUrl}/${relativePath}` pointing back at the same backend's static handler.

2. main.ts serves them via express.static on local disk: lines 133-137 register `/uploads` -> expressStatic(join(process.cwd(),'uploads')) behind the uploads-acl middleware. No regenerate-on-miss.

3. Storage is the host-local named volume: docker-compose.prod.yml:131 `uploads_storage:/app/uploads`, declared at :261. nginx (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:48-49) proxies /uploads/ to the single backend 127.0.0.1:3000.

4. No object-storage abstraction exists: grep of backend/package.json for aws-sdk/@aws-sdk/client-s3/minio/cloudinary/@google-cloud/storage returns nothing (exit 1). The only AWS reference is modules/kms (AWS KMS for credential encryption — key management, not blob storage). So the "no S3/MinIO/etc." claim is correct.

5. The invoice contrast is accurate: subscriptions/controllers/invoice.controller.ts:64 calls generateInvoicePdf on miss (self-heals because the PDF is reconstructable from DB rows), whereas uploaded image bytes are NOT reconstructable, so an upload landing on replica A is permanently absent on replica B.

6. The horizontal-scale premise is real, not hypothetical for the codebase's direction: main.ts:226-228 wires RedisIoAdapter, whose own spec (common/adapters/redis-io.adapter.spec.ts:4) calls it "load-bearing infra for horizontal scale." Combined with the advisory-lock cron-singleton work, the project is deliberately building toward multi-replica. So local-disk uploads are correctly identified as the remaining piece of host-local statefulness that breaks that goal — the finder did not invent the scaling intent.

WHY OVERSTATED (-> partly, medium not high): the present-tense title/impact ("behind a load balancer a fraction of asset GETs 404", "uploads are non-portable across replicas") describes a state that does NOT exist in the deployed topology today. docker-compose.prod.yml defines a SINGLE backend service with a fixed host-port binding `3000:3000` (line 128) and no deploy.replicas/scale; nginx points at one upstream (127.0.0.1:3000). With one replica there is no LB fan-out and therefore zero 404s right now — the defect is LATENT, materializing only when a 2nd replica is actually added. A real high-severity finding in a production-impact frame should be causing user-visible breakage now; this one is a correctly-diagnosed blocker to a not-yet-enabled scaling step plus a single-point-of-data-durability concern (volume loss = all images gone, no DB-reconstruct path). That is a legitimate medium: real, architecturally sound, durable user data on a single host volume with no shared/object backend, and a hard prerequisite before the Redis-adapter scale-out can be turned on — but not a live production incident today. Recommendation (StorageService port over S3/GCS/MinIO/R2, or interim NFS) is sound; the uploads-acl allowlist would indeed need re-expression as bucket-level/signed-URL access control.
findings.evidence: upload.service.ts:115 and :223 do `await fs.writeFile(filePath, ...)` under path.resolve(process.cwd(),'uploads')/{products,logos}; these are served statically by main.ts:181-188 via express.static on /uploads with no regenerate-on-miss. The storage is the local named volume uploads_storage:/app/uploads (docker-compose.prod.yml:131,261) and nginx proxies /uploads/ to the single backend (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:48-50). A repo-wide grep confirms NO object-storage abstraction exists: no S3/@aws-sdk/MinIO/@google-cloud/storage/cloudinary in backend/package.json or src. Unlike the invoice PDF path (invoice.controller.ts:63-67 regenerates on miss), uploads have no self-heal — once written to replica A's volume they are simply absent on replica B.
findings.why_it_matters: The moment a second backend replica is added (the explicit goal of the Redis adapter + advisory locks), an upload handled by Pod A lands only on Pod A's disk; menu/logo image GETs that the LB routes to Pod B return 404, so newly uploaded product photos and tenant logos appear broken intermittently on the QR menu and dashboards. It also blocks zero-downtime rollouts and any move to ephemeral/containers-without-persistent-volume orchestration.
findings.recommendation: Introduce an object-storage backend (S3/GCS/MinIO/R2) behind a small StorageService port; have UploadService write through it and serve uploads via the bucket/CDN (or a signed-URL redirect) instead of express.static on local disk. As an interim single-host measure, mount uploads_storage as a shared network volume (NFS) across replicas, but the durable fix is object storage. The ACL allowlist in uploads-acl.middleware.ts can be re-expressed as bucket-level access control.

findings.title: Every @Throttle({ default: ... }) decorator is INERT — ~49 route overrides (login/register/forgot-password brute-force, webhook, QR-menu, device-pair limits) silently fall back to the lax global 100/min
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is TRUE on current main. ThrottlerModule.forRoot (app.module.ts:82-97) registers only throttlers named 'short' (10/1s), 'medium' (50/10s), 'long' (100/60s) — none named 'default'. I verified the v6.4.0 guard mechanic from source: @nestjs/throttler/dist/throttler.decorator.js setThrottlerMetadata stores route overrides under reflector key `THROTTLER_LIMIT + <key>` (so `@Throttle({default:{...}})` writes to `THROTTLER_LIMITdefault`), while throttler.guard.js canActivate iterates `this.throttlers` (short/medium/long) and reads `this.reflector.getAllAndOverride(THROTTLER_LIMIT + namedThrottler.name, ...)` — i.e. it only ever looks up `THROTTLER_LIMITshort/medium/long`, never `THROTTLER_LIMITdefault`. So routeOrClassLimit resolves to undefined and falls back to `namedThrottler.limit` (the global 10/50/100). Confirmed 49 `{default:...}` overrides vs 10 `{long:...}` overrides via grep; the `long:` ones DO resolve (registered name) and work, the `default:` ones are dead. auth.controller.ts:40-58 confirms intended budgets (LOGIN 5/min, REGISTER 3/hr, FORGOT/VERIFY/CHANGE_PASSWORD 5/min, SOCIAL 3/hr, REFRESH 10/min) — all keyed `default`, all inert.

Checked for mitigations and found NONE that rescue the intent: (a) the custom MachineThrottlerGuard (common/guards/machine-throttler.guard.ts) only overrides getTracker (IP-primary keying), it does not touch canActivate or override resolution, so it inherits the broken behavior; (b) auth routes carry no @SkipThrottle, so the global guard DOES apply — meaning routes are not literally open; (c) no nginx limit_req/limit_req_zone exists in ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf or staging conf (the earlier filename grep matched files, not directives — confirmed zero directives), so there is no upstream rate limit either.

Nuance that tempers the finder's wording but not the verdict: the routes are NOT 'unthrottled' — they fall back to the live global throttlers, so per-IP they are capped at ~100/min (long) with a 10/sec burst ceiling (short). The finding's '~20x to ~1200x weaker' is accurate as a worst-case framing (REGISTER's 3/hr intent vs 100/min effective is the extreme), but practical brute-force is bounded at 100/min/IP, not infinite. That is still a genuine, exploitable security regression: a 100/min/IP credential-stuffing / password-reset / OTP-verify window is ~20x looser than designed and well within abuse range, and the global cap is per-instance/per-IP only. Severity 'high' is justified for a public multi-tenant SaaS; I would not downgrade to medium because the inert limits include the brute-force-sensitive auth surface and there is no compensating control anywhere in the stack.
findings.evidence: ThrottlerModule.forRoot in app.module.ts:82-97 registers throttlers named 'short'/'medium'/'long' — none named 'default'. But the override decorators all use the key 'default': auth.controller.ts:40-58 (LOGIN_THROTTLE={default:{limit:5}}, REGISTER, FORGOT, VERIFY, SOCIAL, REFRESH, CHANGE_PASSWORD), superadmin-auth.controller.ts:36, delivery-webhook.controller.ts:33, plus ~49 sites total grepped (qr-menu, public-reservations, devices pair limit:5, desktop-app, caller, local-bridge, catalog). The bundled guard (node_modules/@nestjs/throttler/dist/throttler.guard.js canActivate) iterates this.throttlers (short/medium/long) and reads the route override under THROTTLER_LIMIT+namedThrottler.name (e.g. '+short'); it never reads the 'default' key, so the override is discarded and the route uses the global short/medium/long limits (effective cap 100/min via 'long').
findings.why_it_matters: Auth endpoints intended to allow 5 logins/min, 3 registrations/hour, 5 password-reset requests/min, and a 5/min device pair attempt are actually capped at 100/min — a ~20x to ~1200x weaker limit. Credential-stuffing, password-reset abuse, OTP/verify brute force, and webhook flooding are effectively unthrottled at the intended granularity. This is an ACTIVE security regression even on a single instance.
findings.recommendation: Either rename the registered throttlers to include one named 'default' (add a 'default' entry to the forRoot array) so the existing override keys resolve, or change all override objects to target a real registered name (e.g. {short:{...}} or a new dedicated 'auth' throttler). Add an integration test that hits an auth route limit+1 times and asserts a 429 to lock this contract.

findings.title: Rate-limit counters use in-memory ThrottlerStorage — under N replicas the effective limit is N× the configured value
findings.evidence: app.module.ts:82 calls ThrottlerModule.forRoot([...]) with no `storage:` option, and there is no ThrottlerStorage provider in app.module providers (grep found none) and no throttler-storage-redis dependency in backend/package.json (only @nestjs/throttler ^6.4.0). The default storage is the in-process ThrottlerStorageService. MachineThrottlerGuard (common/guards/machine-throttler.guard.ts) only customizes the tracker key (IP/screen/partner), not the storage.
findings.why_it_matters: Per-replica counters mean each replica independently allows the full quota, so with 3 replicas the global 100/min becomes ~300/min and any auth/webhook limit is diluted by replica count — brute-force and DoS protections weaken exactly as you scale out. (Today single-instance, so latent, but compounds finding #3.)
findings.recommendation: Add a Redis-backed ThrottlerStorage (e.g. @nest-lab/throttler-storage-redis or a small custom adapter over the existing Redis client) and pass it as `storage` to ThrottlerModule.forRoot so counters are shared across replicas. Fix finding #3 first so the intended per-route limits actually apply.

findings.title: AnalyticsGateway routes edge-device commands via a replica-local connectedDevices Map — config/commands sent from a non-owning replica are silently dropped
findings.evidence: analytics.gateway.ts:70 holds `connectedDevices: Map<string,EdgeDeviceConnection>` populated only on the replica the edge device connected to. sendConfigToDevice/sendCommandToDevice/sendCalibrationToDevice (lines ~530-580) call findDevice() which scans only the LOCAL map, and on miss log 'device not connected' and return false. The subsequent `this.server.to(device.socketId).emit(...)` would actually work cross-replica via the Redis adapter (per-socket rooms are replicated), but the local-map gate prevents reaching that line when the request lands on a different replica. (Mitigating: grep found no HTTP/service caller of these methods today, so this is currently latent/dead — but it is wired for the on-prem analytics edge-device feature.)
findings.why_it_matters: If/when edge-device command/config issuance is wired to an HTTP route, a command issued on a replica where the device isn't connected returns false and is silently lost, making edge-device control flaky and non-deterministic behind an LB. The membership state, not the emit, is the scale bug.
findings.recommendation: Don't gate on local membership for routing. Track device->socket presence in Redis (or rely on Socket.IO's replicated rooms: join each device to a deterministic room `edge:${tenantId}:${deviceId}` on connect and always emit `server.to(room).emit(...)` without a local-map precheck). Use the Redis adapter's allSockets()/serverSideEmit for liveness checks if a connected/not-connected answer is required.

findings.title: Demo data reset cron has no distributed lock — fires on every replica simultaneously
findings.evidence: demo.service.ts:343 `@Cron(CronExpression.EVERY_DAY_AT_4AM) resetDemoData()` performs a deleteMany of the demo tenant's orders/payments then reseeds, with NO withAdvisoryLock/pg_try_advisory_lock (confirmed: grep for 'advisory' in demo.service.ts returns nothing). It is the only scheduled job among 15 lacking the guard.
findings.why_it_matters: Under multi-replica all replicas run the delete+reseed concurrently at 04:00, risking duplicated seed orders or transaction conflicts on the demo tenant. Impact is confined to the demo tenant (no real-customer data), hence low severity, but it breaks the otherwise-uniform locking discipline.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the body in withAdvisoryLock(this.prisma,'demo-data-reset', ...) like the other crons (after fixing finding #1 so the lock helper is correct).

findings.title: WebSocket scale-out has no sticky sessions and relies entirely on the Redis adapter being up — silent fallback to in-memory adapter breaks cross-replica realtime
findings.evidence: redis-io.adapter.ts connectToRedis() falls back to the in-memory adapter (returns/logs a warn) when REDIS_URL is unset OR after 3 failed retries (reconnectStrategy retries>3 → Error, caught at the try/catch → in-memory). nginx (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:52-58) proxies /socket.io/ to the single backend with no ip_hash/sticky directive and no upstream block. There is no startup gate that REQUIRES the Redis adapter in production.
findings.why_it_matters: When you scale to 2+ replicas, if Redis is briefly unreachable at boot the backend comes up healthy but with the in-memory Socket.IO adapter, so server.to(room).emit() reaches only same-replica clients — KDS tickets, POS payment events, and notifications silently drop for ~half of connected clients with no error surfaced. Default Socket.IO long-polling also needs sticky sessions across replicas during the upgrade handshake, which the nginx config does not provide.
findings.recommendation: In production, fail startup (or emit a loud, alerting-grade error) if the Redis Socket.IO adapter cannot connect, rather than silently degrading. When moving to multi-replica, add an nginx upstream with ip_hash (or cookie-based sticky) for /socket.io/, or force websocket-only transport to avoid the polling-handshake stickiness requirement.

summary: The backend security posture is genuinely above-average in several areas: a well-designed SSRF guard (assertPublicHttpUrl with full private-CIDR/IMDS coverage, re-checked just before the network call), strict tenant-isolating BranchGuard (DB-verified ownership + active + role allow-list, no JWT fallback), hardened file upload (5MB cap, file-count cap, magic-byte sniff via sharp, explicit SVG rejection, server-generated UUID filenames, sharp re-encode, path-containment on delete), an uploads allowlist ACL that closed a previous world-readable static mount, httpOnly+secure+sameSite=strict refresh cookies in a dedicated path with tokens deliberately kept out of localStorage, a separate superadmin JWT realm with 2FA, and tightened CORS (regex tenant-subdomain matcher + reserved-name deny-list, no reflective '*' with credentials). However, three runtime realities undercut this on the path to real paying tenants at scale: (1) the global rate limiter is effectively defeated — every per-endpoint @Throttle uses a 'default' named bucket that is NOT registered (only short/medium/long exist), so login/register/2FA/refresh/password-reset throttles are inert, and the storage is in-memory so even the global limits multiply per replica; (2) the backend container publishes 0.0.0.0:3000 (unlike postgres which is correctly 127.0.0.1-only), exposing the full API plus an unauthenticated /api/metrics directly on the public host IP, bypassing nginx/TLS; (3) no HSTS or security headers exist at the nginx edge, and Helmet only covers /api + /uploads, leaving the user-facing SPA with no HSTS/clickjacking protection. SSRF has a residual redirect-follow + DNS-rebind hole. These are concrete, fixable, and should be closed before scaling tenant onboarding.
strengths: Strong SSRF defense for tenant-supplied webhook URLs: src/common/net/url-safety.ts blocks non-http(s), userinfo, internal service ports, and resolves+rejects all private/loopback/link-local/CGNAT/IPv4-mapped-IPv6 ranges incl. 169.254.169.254 (AWS IMDS); the webhook worker re-validates immediately before fetch (webhook-delivery-worker.service.ts:156-173) to narrow the DNS-rebind window, with a 15s AbortSignal timeout.

Strict tenant isolation at the guard layer: BranchGuard (src/modules/auth/guards/branch.guard.ts) DB-verifies the X-Branch-Id belongs to req.user.tenantId AND is active, then applies a role-conditional allow-list (restricted roles pinned to primaryBranchId, MANAGER/ADMIN by allowedBranchIds) with no JWT/first-branch fallback.

File-upload handling is hardened end-to-end: multer enforces 5MB + file-count limits and a MIME prefilter (upload.controller.ts:45-58), and upload.service.ts:46-59 does a real magic-byte sniff via sharp.metadata(), explicitly rejects SVG (XSS), discards client filenames in favor of randomUUID, re-encodes via sharp, and contains delete paths within uploadsRoot (upload.service.ts:287).

Uploads are served through an allowlist ACL (src/common/middleware/uploads-acl.middleware.ts) that only serves public-by-design categories (products/logos), rejects dotfiles, traversal, NUL bytes, and non-GET — replacing a prior world-readable blanket static mount.

Refresh tokens use httpOnly + secure(in prod) + sameSite:strict cookies scoped to /api/auth (auth.controller.ts:64-70) and are stripped from the response body (stripRefresh); superadmin mirrors this on /api/superadmin/auth with a separate JWT realm + 2FA (superadmin-auth.controller.ts:48-56).
CORS is not reflective: main.ts:158-205 uses a length-capped tenant-subdomain regex with a reserved-name deny-list and an explicit allowlist, never echoing arbitrary Origin with credentials:true.

Body-size limits are tuned for DoS: generic 100KB JSON/urlencoded with a path-scoped 2MB only for /api/webhooks (main.ts:70-87); fail-fast env validation enforces 32-char, cross-realm-distinct JWT secrets in production (env-validation.ts).

Postgres and Redis are correctly bound loopback-only / network-internal (docker-compose.prod.yml:27 binds 127.0.0.1:5432; redis has no host port and requires a password), and Swagger is disabled in production unless SWAGGER_ENABLED is explicitly set (main.ts:235-238).

findings.title: Per-endpoint rate limits (login/register/2FA/refresh) are inert: @Throttle uses an unregistered 'default' bucket and storage is in-memory per-replica
findings.verify_reasoning:
The core technical claim is TRUE on current main. (1) ThrottlerModule.forRoot in src/app.module.ts:82-98 registers ONLY named throttlers short/medium/long — no 'default'. (2) Every sensitive auth route binds @Throttle({default:{...}}): auth.controller.ts constants at lines 40-58 (LOGIN/REGISTER/FORGOT/VERIFY/SOCIAL/REFRESH/CHANGE_PASSWORD) applied at lines 114/131/152/251/263/273/287/300/310/328, and superadmin-auth.controller.ts:36-38 (LOGIN/VERIFY_2FA/REFRESH). (3) Verified the actual guard at node_modules/@nestjs/throttler@6.4.0/dist/throttler.guard.js canActivate: it iterates `this.throttlers` (the registered short/medium/long) and reads overrides via reflector.getAllAndOverride(THROTTLER_LIMIT + namedThrottler.name). Metadata keyed under 'default' is orphaned and never read, so routeOrClassLimit is undefined and the code falls back to namedThrottler.limit (the global values). The tight per-route limits (5/min login, 3/hr register, 5/min 2FA) genuinely never bind.

Two refinements to the finding (it slightly overstates one detail but the conclusion holds):
- NOT fully "unthrottled (~100/min)": a global ThrottlerGuard IS registered (MachineThrottlerGuard extends ThrottlerGuard, APP_GUARD at app.module.ts:180-183), runs on every route (no @SkipThrottle on auth — grep returned none), and applies ALL THREE global buckets per IP: short 10/sec, medium 50/10s, long 100/min. So the effective cap is 100/min/IP (and 50/10s, 10/s), not infinite. The finder's headline "effectively unthrottled" is loose; the accurate statement is "auth is capped at the loose global 100/min/IP instead of the intended 5/min" — 20x looser than designed. That is still a serious weakening (a 6-digit superadmin TOTP at 100 attempts/min/IP, login/credential-stuffing at 100/min/IP, password-reset spam at 100/min/IP). The intended defense is dead.
- Multi-replica multiplier claim is currently NOT realized: docker-compose.prod.yml runs a single backend container (container_name kds_backend_prod, no `replicas:`/scale directive). The in-memory-store-is-per-replica concern is real architecturally (no ThrottlerStorageRedis anywhere — grep empty; forRoot has no `storage:`), so it becomes exploitable the moment they scale out, but it is not an active multiplier today. Also no nginx-layer rate limiting exists as a compensating control (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf has no limit_req/limit_conn), so there is no other mitigation.

Net: the per-route auth/2FA/refresh throttles are inert exactly as described; the only correction is that a loose global 100/min/IP floor still applies (so not zero throttling) and the per-replica multiplication is latent rather than active on the current single-replica prod. Real-world impact for a payment-handling multi-tenant SaaS — superadmin 2FA and tenant login brute-force at 100/min/IP instead of 5/min, no compensating nginx layer — is a genuine high-severity auth-hardening gap, but it is not a literal full-bypass (a global cap exists) so 'blocker' is a notch too high; 'high' is the accurate severity. The recommended fix (add a {name:'default'} entry or retarget @Throttle to a registered name, plus Redis-backed storage before scaling) is correct.
findings.evidence: ThrottlerModule.forRoot in src/app.module.ts:82-98 registers ONLY named throttlers 'short'/'medium'/'long' — there is no 'default'. Every sensitive throttle is declared against 'default', e.g. auth.controller.ts:40-58 LOGIN_THROTTLE={default:{limit:5,ttl:60_000}}, REGISTER_THROTTLE={default:{limit:3,ttl:3_600_000}}, FORGOT/VERIFY/SOCIAL/REFRESH/CHANGE_PASSWORD, and superadmin-auth.controller.ts:36-38 LOGIN/VERIFY_2FA/REFRESH. @nestjs/throttler@6.4.0 guard (node_modules/@nestjs/throttler/dist/throttler.guard.js:66-77) iterates the REGISTERED throttlers and reads overrides keyed by each registered name; metadata keyed 'default' is never read. So the tight per-route limits never apply — endpoints fall back to the global 100/min(long). Additionally no Redis storage is configured (no ThrottlerStorageRedis anywhere), so the default in-memory store is per-replica; under the multi-replica deployment the codebase is built for, the effective limit is multiplied by replica count and an LB-round-robined attacker gets N× the budget.
findings.why_it_matters: Auth brute-force, credential-stuffing, password-reset spam, and superadmin login brute-force are effectively unthrottled (~100/min/IP per replica instead of 5/min, and superadmin 2FA codes are brute-forceable). For a payment-handling multi-tenant SaaS onboarding real tenants this is a direct account-takeover and abuse vector.
findings.recommendation: Either rename the registered throttler to 'default' (add a {name:'default',...} entry) so the existing @Throttle({default}) overrides bind, or change every @Throttle to target a registered name. Verify with an integration test that the 6th login in 60s returns 429. Add Redis-backed throttler storage (e.g. @nest-lab/throttler-storage-redis pointed at the existing REDIS_URL) so limits are shared across replicas.

findings.title: Backend container publishes 0.0.0.0:3000, exposing the full API and unauthenticated /api/metrics directly on the public host IP (bypassing nginx/TLS)
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every technical claim checks out on main. docker-compose.prod.yml:127-128 maps the backend as '3000:3000' (all host interfaces), in contrast to postgres at line 27 ('127.0.0.1:5432:5432') and redis (no host port). frontend ('8080:80', line 168) and landing ('3100:3000', line 194) are likewise on all interfaces. Staging is identical: docker-compose.staging.yml:90-91 maps backend '3002:3002' while its postgres (:18) and redis (:47) are explicitly loopback-bound with comments stating off-box exposure would widen blast radius — so the asymmetry is real and the team clearly knows the loopback pattern.

The nginx vhost (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:44-63) proxies EVERY upstream via http://127.0.0.1:3000 / :8080; staging conf does the same via 127.0.0.1:3002/5175. nginx never needs the public binding — loopback-only ('127.0.0.1:3000:3000') would serve all legitimate traffic unchanged. The public binding is surplus attack surface.

/api/metrics: main.ts:141 setGlobalPrefix('api') + @Get('metrics') (actual path backend/src/common/metrics/metrics.controller.ts, not the root path the finder implied, but @Public() is indeed line 33 and assertAuthorized early-returns at 43-44 exactly as claimed). Token-gated only IF METRICS_TOKEN is set. METRICS_TOKEN appears NOWHERE in prod/staging compose or any env template — only as optional in env.validation.ts:67 and as a placeholder in the opt-in ops/monitoring stack. So in shipped prod config the token is unset → assertAuthorized returns early → /api/metrics is open. The controller docstring (22-24) itself says the unset posture REQUIRES keeping the endpoint off public ingress — a precondition the '3000:3000' binding violates.

Mitigation hunt — none in repo: no ufw/iptables/firewall automation anywhere in scripts/ or ops/ (grep empty); backend/.env.production.template only has an unchecked '[ ] Configure firewall rules' checklist line. So 'host firewall blocks it' is an unverifiable operator assumption, not an in-repo control. The intended monitoring design (docker-compose.monitoring.yml:60 KDS_BACKEND_TARGET=kds_backend_prod:3000, joining the app docker network as external at 116-123) scrapes the backend over the internal docker network by container name — it does NOT rely on the public host port, so the public binding is unnecessary even for Prometheus.

Severity nuance: the title's 'exposing the full API ... unauthenticated' overstates — the API routes still enforce the global JWT + branch-scope guards, so this is NOT an auth bypass for app data. The real concrete impacts are: (1) genuinely-unauthenticated /api/metrics leaking Prometheus telemetry (tenant counts, route labels, request volumes) to the public internet; (2) bypass of nginx TLS/edge controls (plain-HTTP reach to the API on :3000); (3) X-Forwarded-For throttle/audit spoofing since requests skip the proxy hop. All real prod-security regressions, fixable with a one-line bind change + setting METRICS_TOKEN. Because the primary directly-exposed asset is metrics/telemetry + a TLS/edge bypass (not credentials, PII, or RCE, and app routes remain auth-gated), I rate this high rather than critical — 'critical' is modestly overstated, but the underlying defect and the recommendation are entirely valid.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.prod.yml:127-128 maps the backend as '3000:3000' (binds all host interfaces). Contrast postgres at line 27 which is correctly '127.0.0.1:5432:5432'. nginx only proxies via 127.0.0.1 (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:45). /api/metrics is @Public (metrics.controller.ts:33) and only token-gated IF METRICS_TOKEN is set — but METRICS_TOKEN is absent from docker-compose.prod.yml, .env.production.example/.template, and staging, so assertAuthorized returns early (metrics.controller.ts:43-44) and the endpoint is open. Staging is the same (docker-compose.staging.yml:91 '3002:3002').
findings.why_it_matters: Anyone can reach http://<server-ip>:3000/api/* over plain HTTP, bypassing nginx TLS, edge controls, and any future WAF; and http://<server-ip>:3000/api/metrics returns Prometheus output leaking tenant counts, request volumes, internal route labels and operational telemetry to the public internet. It also defeats X-Forwarded-For-based IP throttling/audit since requests arrive without the proxy hop.
findings.recommendation: Bind the backend to loopback only: '127.0.0.1:3000:3000' (matching postgres). Set METRICS_TOKEN in production env (and document it in .env.production.template) and have Prometheus scrape over the internal network with the bearer token. Confirm host firewall denies inbound 3000/3002/8080/3100 from the internet as defense-in-depth.

findings.title: No HSTS or security headers at the nginx edge; Helmet only covers /api and /uploads, leaving the user-facing SPA unprotected
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is TRUE on main. (1) All four ops/nginx/*.conf vhosts and the SPA container's own frontend/nginx.conf contain zero response security headers — only request-direction proxy_set_header and Cache-Control/Pragma/Expires. grep confirms exit 1 for HSTS/X-Frame-Options/nosniff/CSP/Referrer-Policy. (2) Helmet (main.ts:93-111) is bound to the NestJS app, which only serves /api/* and /uploads/* (127.0.0.1:3000). nginx routes location / to the separate SPA container (127.0.0.1:8080 prod, 5175 staging), so the operator SPA's HTML/assets never traverse Helmet. (3) The SPA container's own nginx adds only cache headers, so login/dashboard/POS responses ship with no HSTS, no frame-ancestors/X-Frame-Options, no nosniff. The HTTP->HTTPS 301 exists (conf:24) but options-ssl-nginx.conf (certbot mozilla-intermediate) does not add HSTS, so HSTS is genuinely absent.

Mitigation the finder partly missed but which does NOT cover the SPA: landing/next.config.ts:70-81 sets X-Frame-Options SAMEORIGIN, X-Content-Type-Options nosniff, Referrer-Policy, and a CSP with frame-ancestors 'self' — but that's the decoupled marketing/landing app (marketing.hummytummy.com), not the user-facing operator SPA, and even it omits HSTS. So the finder's core claim (the primary web app is unprotected) stands.

Severity nuance: this is a real, easy-to-fix hardening gap on the highest-value pages (admin/payment/POS), so it is legitimate. But it is a missing defense-in-depth control rather than a directly exploitable vuln: SSL-strip requires active on-path MITM, the site is TLS-only with a 301 upgrade, and clickjacking requires a framing-based social-engineering chain. There is also a genuine evidentiary caveat — hummytummy.com.conf self-describes as RECONSTRUCTED (header lines 4-12 say capture live `nginx -T` and reconcile), so the repo cannot prove the live prod edge lacks these headers; however the SPA container nginx (the actually-deployed artifact) demonstrably sets none. Net: confirmed but I'd calibrate high->medium.
findings.evidence: grep across all of ops/nginx/*.conf finds zero add_header / Strict-Transport-Security / X-Frame-Options / X-Content-Type-Options / Content-Security-Policy directives (verified: exit 1, no matches). Helmet is applied in main.ts:93-111 but NestJS only serves /api/* and /uploads/*; nginx proxies '/' to the separate frontend SPA container (hummytummy.com.conf:62-64) and landing/docs to their own containers, none of which get Helmet headers. So the primary web app (login, dashboard, POS) is served with no HSTS, no X-Frame-Options/frame-ancestors, no nosniff. The HTTP->HTTPS redirect exists (conf:24) but without HSTS the first request each session is downgrade/SSL-strip eligible.
findings.why_it_matters: Without HSTS, a paying tenant's owner/staff on a hostile network can be SSL-stripped on first visit and have their session captured; without frame-ancestors/X-Frame-Options on the SPA, the dashboard/POS can be framed for clickjacking. These protect the highest-value pages (admin + payment + POS).
findings.recommendation: Add a shared nginx snippet applied to all server blocks: 'add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload" always;', plus X-Content-Type-Options nosniff, X-Frame-Options DENY (or a CSP frame-ancestors), and Referrer-Policy. Use 'always' so headers are emitted on error responses too. Consider HSTS preload once verified across all subdomains.

findings.title: Outbound webhook fetch follows redirects and does not pin the validated IP — SSRF bypass via 30x redirect / DNS rebind
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is accurate on main. webhook-delivery-worker.service.ts:176 calls `fetch(d.url, {...})` with no `redirect` option, so Node's undici fetch uses the default `redirect: "follow"` (up to 20 redirects). The worker DOES re-run assertPublicHttpUrl at line 157 right before the fetch (the finder under-credited this — it closes the simple validate-then-connect DNS-rebind-at-subscribe gap), BUT that validation only covers the INITIAL URL. A tenant-controlled endpoint that resolves to a public IP and passes the guard can return a 302/301/307 Location pointing at http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/... or any internal host, and undici will transparently follow it with no re-validation. The redirect bypass is deterministic and trivial — strictly easier than DNS rebind.

The finding is also correct that the `resolvedIp` returned by assertPublicHttpUrl (url-safety.ts:124,161) is computed and then discarded by both callers — there is no connect-time IP pinning anywhere. I searched the whole backend: no `setGlobalDispatcher`, no custom undici `Agent`/`ProxyAgent`, no custom DNS `lookup`, no `redirect:"manual"` on any fetch. url-safety.ts:20-24 explicitly documents that full DNS-rebind mitigation (pinning the IP into the connect socket) is DEFERRED, so there is no compensating control elsewhere — this is an acknowledged open gap, not something handled in nginx/config/a guard.

Real-world severity is HIGH, as claimed, not overstated:
- This is NOT blind SSRF. The response body is captured (`text.slice(0,500)`, line 204) into `lastResponseSnippet`, which is persisted and surfaced to the tenant via the webhook-delivery dashboard. So internal data (IMDS IAM credentials, internal admin/JSON responses) is directly exfiltrated back to the attacker, up to 500 chars per attempt, retried across multiple deliveries.
- The exact threat assertPublicHttpUrl was written to stop (the file's own header names 169.254.169.254 IMDS, Redis, Postgres) is reachable via the redirect path.
- Aggravating: if this runs on AWS with IMDSv1 / an attached IAM role, a single 302→IMDS chain yields temporary AWS credentials = cloud account pivot.

Bounding factors that keep it HIGH rather than CRITICAL: exploitation requires an authenticated tenant with webhook-subscribe privilege (ADMIN/MANAGER), and it's an outbound POST (though 301/302/303 convert to GET, and 307/308 keep POST — internal GET targets like IMDS are reachable either way). In a mutually-untrusted multi-tenant SaaS, self-serve webhook registration is a normal feature, so the privilege bar is low. Recommendation in the finding is correct: set `redirect:"manual"` and treat 3xx as failure (cheap, full coverage for the redirect vector), and for the residual rebind race, pin the validated IP via an undici Agent with a custom connect/lookup. Apply to any other tenant-URL fetcher (none found here besides the webhook worker; webhook-outbound.service.ts only validates at subscribe, doesn't fetch).
findings.evidence: webhook-delivery-worker.service.ts:176-190 calls fetch(d.url, {...}) with no redirect:'manual' option, so Node fetch follows up to 20 redirects by default. assertPublicHttpUrl (url-safety.ts) validates only the initial URL's resolved IP; a tenant-controlled public endpoint can return a 302 to http://169.254.169.254/... or an internal host, which fetch will follow without re-validation. url-safety.ts:22-24 itself documents that full DNS-rebind mitigation requires pinning the resolved IP into the connect-time socket and explicitly defers it; the resolved IP from assertPublicHttpUrl is computed but discarded (worker ignores the returned {resolvedIp}).
findings.why_it_matters: The SSRF guard (otherwise strong) can be bypassed by any tenant who controls a webhook endpoint, turning the server into a proxy to cloud metadata (IMDS), internal admin services, Redis/Postgres, etc. — the exact threat url-safety.ts was written to stop. Severity is bounded by it being an outbound POST with a signed body and 500-char response capture, but response snippets can exfiltrate internal data.
findings.recommendation: Set redirect:'manual' on the webhook fetch and treat 3xx as a delivery failure (or re-run assertPublicHttpUrl on the Location). For full coverage, pin the validated IP: use an undici Agent with a custom connect/lookup that forces connection to the assertPublicHttpUrl-resolved address (Host header preserved) so DNS rebind between validate and connect cannot occur. Apply the same to other tenant-URL callers (provisioning/marketing-event-relay).

findings.title: Global ValidationPipe runs with forbidNonWhitelisted:false — unknown/over-posted fields are silently stripped, not rejected
findings.evidence: main.ts:209-218 sets whitelist:true but forbidNonWhitelisted:false with enableImplicitConversion:true. whitelist strips unknown properties so they don't reach services, which prevents classic mass-assignment IF every write path consumes typed DTO fields (not the raw body). The risk surfaces where a DTO intentionally exposes sensitive fields on a public endpoint: register.dto.ts:65-81 accepts role and tenantId on the @Public POST /auth/register. The service mitigates this (auth.service.ts: ADMIN rejected in the join branch, non-ADMIN joiners forced to PENDING_APPROVAL with no tokens until admin approval), so it is not a direct privilege escalation — but a stranger who learns a tenant UUID can self-register a PENDING_APPROVAL MANAGER into that tenant.
findings.why_it_matters: forbidNonWhitelisted:false means an over-posting bug is silently tolerated rather than surfaced as a 400, so any future DTO that forgets to omit a sensitive field becomes exploitable without a loud failure. The register role/tenantId surface allows unsolicited pending-staff records in arbitrary tenants (approval-phishing / inbox spam).
findings.recommendation: Set forbidNonWhitelisted:true globally so over-posting becomes a hard 400 and regressions are caught immediately. For register, gate self-join by an invite token rather than a guessable tenant UUID, and reject any role above WAITER on the public path. Add a test asserting unknown body fields 400.

findings.title: secure cookie flag and trust-proxy correctness hinge on NODE_ENV/TRUST_PROXY env being right; no hard guarantee
findings.evidence: Refresh cookies set secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production' (auth.controller.ts:66, superadmin-auth.controller.ts:51). docker-compose.prod.yml:81 sets NODE_ENV=production so this is correct today, but the flag is environment-derived rather than tied to request protocol. trust proxy defaults to 1 hop (main.ts:58-64); the comment notes 'true' is X-Forwarded-For-spoofable. Because the backend is also directly reachable on 0.0.0.0:3000 (see separate finding), a client connecting directly (no proxy hop) can present a forged X-Forwarded-For that req.ips[0] trusts, skewing IP-based throttling/audit IPs.
findings.why_it_matters: If NODE_ENV is ever unset in a prod-like deploy, refresh cookies go out without Secure and can leak over plaintext. Combined with the direct 0.0.0.0 exposure, X-Forwarded-For spoofing can poison audit-IP columns and per-IP rate-limit buckets.
findings.recommendation: Once the backend is bound to loopback-behind-nginx, keep trust proxy pinned to the exact hop count via TRUST_PROXY. Consider deriving Secure from req.secure || X-Forwarded-Proto rather than NODE_ENV, or assert NODE_ENV==='production' at boot for prod images. Document TRUST_PROXY in the env template.

summary: The realtime layer is mature: the Socket.IO Redis adapter is correctly wired in main.ts so room-scoped emits fan out across replicas; every gateway authenticates at handshake with the same BranchGuard predicate as HTTP (closing cross-tenant/cross-branch room subscription); staff sockets re-check tokenVersion/status at connect and auto-disconnect at JWT expiry; the device command queue is fully DB-durable. Two production problems remain: (1) on-prem device and local-bridge bearer tokens have a fixed TTL (24h/30d) set only at pair/claim with no server renewal and no client re-claim, so the mesh fleet stops authenticating on a fixed timer with no self-recovery (prior review flagged it; still present despite memory claiming a fix); (2) every default-named Throttle decorator (49 routes incl. the device pair brute-force guard) is inert because the ThrottlerModule registers only short/medium/long throttlers, so the documented tight limits never apply.
strengths: Redis adapter actually enabled in prod: main.ts:226-228 connectToRedis then useWebSocketAdapter; redis-io.adapter.ts:105-111 installs the pub/sub adapter so room emits fan out across replicas; prod compose enforces a Redis URL.

Per-room authorization enforced/consistent: kds.gateway.ts:199-220 and notifications.gateway.ts:100-112 validate handshake auth.branchId with BranchGuard.canAccessBranchStatic (same predicate as HTTP); marketing/superadmin tokens rejected (kds.gateway.ts:145-150).
Live WS security re-checks: kds.gateway.ts:169-190 re-reads tokenVersion/status at connect and on switchBranch (301-327); all socket types auto-disconnect at token/session expiry.

Command-queue durability excellent: command-queue.service.ts DB-backed, idempotent on (deviceId,idempotencyKey), atomic claimNext via FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED (173-199), crash-recovery sweeper (313-387) never auto-redelivers side-effecting kinds.

Heartbeat/liveness tenant-scoped: 1-min advisory-locked sweep marks stale units offline; writes scoped by (deviceId,tenantId) (analytics.gateway.ts:455-462); occupancy bound to registered cameraId (327-357).

findings.title: Device and local-bridge bearer tokens have a fixed TTL with NO renewal and NO client re-claim; mesh fleet stops authenticating on a timer
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every technical claim is TRUE on current main, and no mitigation exists anywhere.

DEVICE side (device.service.ts): pair() sets tokenExpiresAt = now + tokenTtlMs at line 256/286, default 24h (line 53-56). authenticateToken() returns null when tokenExpiresAt < now (line 340). heartbeat() (344-380) writes ONLY status + lastSeenAt (+ a deviceLog row) — it never touches tokenExpiresAt. devices.controller.ts has exactly: admin routes (list/create/retire/commands) and device-side pair/heartbeat/next-command/ack — NO refresh/renew route. device-token.guard.ts:32-34 throws UnauthorizedException when authenticateToken returns null, so every device call 401s after expiry.

BRIDGE side (local-bridge.service.ts): claim() sets tokenExpiresAt = now + tokenTtlMs (line 98/104), default 30d (line 39-42), and nulls provisioningTokenHash for single-use (line 105). authenticateToken() rejects past-expiry (line 163). heartbeat() (167-182) writes only status/lastSeenAt/hostname/os/agentVersion — never slides tokenExpiresAt. local-bridge.controller.ts has only claim/heartbeat (+admin) — no refresh route.

AGENT cannot self-recover: main.rs:116-140 runs claim() exactly once, gated on config::resolve_bearer_token().is_none(); after a token is persisted it never re-claims. grep over apps/local-bridge-agent/src found NO 401/Unauthorized/re-claim handling anywhere. Even if it retried, the server already nulled the single-use provisioning token, so a fresh provisioning token would be required.

The claimed fix is NOT in tree: branch fix/device-mesh-token-renewal exists locally but is unmerged; main's heartbeat methods confirm no slide. (The device.service.ts:26 comment even says tokens are issued "at pair time and on refresh" — but the refresh path it references does not exist, a documentation/implementation gap.)

WHY 'high' not 'blocker': the impact is real and severe — a fleet-wide hardware authentication outage on a timer (devices ~24h, bridges ~30d) hitting cash/fiscal/printer flows with no auto-recovery, requiring per-device manual re-pair and per-bridge re-provisioning. That is genuinely production-grade serious. However I down-rank from blocker to high because (1) device-mesh / local-bridge is on-prem hardware that ships DISABLED by default per the MEMORY notes (CONFIGURED_NOT_ACTIVE — not every tenant runs it), (2) the failure is recoverable operationally (re-pair / re-provision) rather than data-loss or money-loss, and (3) the 24h/30d TTLs are env-overridable (DEVICE_TOKEN_TTL_MS / LOCAL_BRIDGE_TOKEN_TTL_MS), so an operator could blunt it by setting a long TTL — a workaround, not a fix, but it means it is not an instantaneous unconditional outage. Still clearly high: any tenant that DOES enable the mesh with default TTLs will silently lose its terminals/printers/yazarkasa on a timer.
findings.evidence: device.service.ts:256 sets tokenExpiresAt=now+24h only in pair(); authenticateToken rejects past-expiry (340); heartbeat (344-380) updates only status/lastSeenAt, never tokenExpiresAt; no refresh route (devices.controller.ts only pair/heartbeat/next-command/ack). local-bridge identical: claim +30d (local-bridge.service.ts:98), rejects past-expiry (163), heartbeat never slides (167-182). Agent cannot self-recover: main.rs:116-140 claims ONCE with single-use provisioning token (server nulls it, service.ts:105); config.rs:51-67 in-process only; no 401 re-claim. Memory slide-on-heartbeat fix is NOT in tree.
findings.why_it_matters: Every paired terminal/printer/yazarkasa dies ~24h after pairing and every bridge ~30d after provisioning with no auto recovery; devices start 401ing on heartbeat/next-command/ack and an operator must re-pair each device and re-provision a fresh token per bridge. Fleet-wide hardware outage on a timer, hitting cash/fiscal flows.
findings.recommendation: Slide tokenExpiresAt forward on every successful heartbeat in DeviceService.heartbeat and LocalBridgeService.heartbeat, or add authenticated refresh endpoints and have the agent call them / handle 401 by re-claiming.

findings.title: All default-named Throttle limits are inert; no default throttler registered, so device pair/heartbeat and ~49 routes never enforce tight limits
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is TRUE on current main, and the scope is actually broader (more severe) than reported.

MECHANISM (verified end-to-end):
1. app.module.ts:82-98 registers ThrottlerModule.forRoot with ONLY three named tiers: short(1s/10), medium(10s/50), long(60s/100). No "default" tier. grep confirms this is the only forRoot/forFeature call in src/.
2. The @nestjs/throttler@6.4.0 guard (node_modules/.../throttler.guard.js:66-96) iterates this.throttlers (= the 3 registered tiers). For each tier `namedThrottler` it reads route overrides via reflector key THROTTLER_LIMIT + namedThrottler.name (lines 76-77), then line 81: `limit = routeOrClassLimit || namedThrottler.limit`. A `@Throttle({ default: {...} })` stores its override under the reflector key THROTTLER_LIMIT + "default", which matches NONE of short/medium/long. So routeOrClassLimit is undefined for every iterated tier, and each tier silently falls back to its GLOBAL limit. The route is therefore governed by short+medium+long globals, never the intended tight value.
3. MachineThrottlerGuard (common/guards/machine-throttler.guard.ts) is the registered APP_GUARD (app.module.ts:180-183) and only overrides getTracker — it does NOT register a default tier or change limit resolution, so it does not mitigate.

CONFIRMED IMPACT, and worse than reported:
- 49 default-named @Throttle sites (grep count exactly 49), all inert: device pair (devices.controller.ts:146, claims 5/min budget in its own comment — actually 100/min, 20x looser), heartbeat/next-command/ack (:164/:174/:184), local-bridge claim (:83), QR menu, public reservations, self-pay, customer-orders, contact, desktop-app, caller, customer-public.
- ESCALATION the finder missed: auth.controller.ts:40-58 defines LOGIN_THROTTLE/REGISTER_THROTTLE/FORGOT_THROTTLE/VERIFY_THROTTLE/SOCIAL_THROTTLE/REFRESH_THROTTLE/CHANGE_PASSWORD_THROTTLE ALL as `{ default: {...} }`. So login brute-force protection (intended 5/min) and register (3/hr), forgot-password, social login are ALSO inert and fall back to global long=100/min. There IS a passing spec (auth.controller.throttle.spec.ts) but it only asserts a @Throttle decorator is ATTACHED, not that the resolved limit is enforced — a false-confidence test, exactly the failure mode the finder's recommended test would catch.

PROOF the team knows the correct pattern: catalog.controller.ts:82-95 contains a detailed comment explaining tier-name merging and correctly uses `{ short: ..., long: ... }`; payments/partner/display controllers correctly use `long:`/`short:`. So the default-named sites are genuine mistakes, not a design choice, and they degrade silently (route still rate-limited by the loose globals, so it "looks working").

No mitigation exists elsewhere (nginx/compose/env not needed to check — the limit resolution is purely in-process and demonstrably falls back to globals). Severity is justified and arguably under-stated because auth login/register brute-force ceilings are affected, not just device mesh. The fix is a one-liner (add a `default` tier to forRoot, or rename) that re-arms all 49 sites at once.
findings.evidence: app.module.ts:82-98 registers only short/medium/long; no default. Guard (throttler.guard.js:66-81) iterates this.throttlers reading overrides by THROTTLER_LIMIT+name; a default-named override matches none so the route uses global namedThrottler.limit. 49 routes use default-named Throttle incl. devices.controller.ts:146 (pair 5/min), :164, :174, :184, local-bridge claim. MachineThrottlerGuard only overrides getTracker.
findings.why_it_matters: Pair brute-force budget (5/min) is false; pair actually gets the global long limit 100/min (~20x looser). Device-command flood, public reservation, QR menu, self-pay, contact only get the generic 10/s-50/10s-100/min ceiling, a real abuse/DoS gap at scale.
findings.recommendation: Register a default throttler in ThrottlerModule.forRoot, or rename each default-named Throttle to an existing throttler; add a test asserting the resolved limit on pair.

findings.title: Redis unreachable at boot silently degrades to in-memory adapter; under multi-replica loses half of every realtime event while pod looks healthy
findings.verify_reasoning:
The code-level claim is factually CORRECT and well-described. redis-io.adapter.ts:30-93: connectToRedis() catches a failed connect (line 83-93), logs, and RESOLVES (never rejects) — so the in-memory fallback is silently kept. createIOServer (105-111) attaches the Redis adapter only `if (this.adapterConstructor)`; otherwise it keeps super's in-memory adapter. main.ts:226-228 awaits connectToRedis() then unconditionally calls useWebSocketAdapter — there is genuinely NO readiness gate, no process exit, no re-attempt, so a boot-time Redis failure pins that process on in-memory for its lifetime. env.validation.ts:51-54 confirms REDIS_URL is @IsOptional, so nothing forces it in prod. No mitigation exists elsewhere for the re-attach/readiness gap.

BUT the WHY-IT-MATTERS impact ("loses half of every realtime event", "missed orders/payments per replica") is conditioned on 2+ replicas, and the actual deployment refutes that premise: docker-compose.prod.yml runs a SINGLE backend container (container_name: kds_backend_prod, no `replicas:`/`scale`/`deploy:` block anywhere in prod — grep for `replicas` returns nothing; staging `deploy:` blocks are resource limits, not replica counts). With one replica the in-memory adapter is functionally identical to the Redis adapter — all sockets are on the one process, so zero events are lost. Furthermore the boot-race trigger is largely designed out: backend `depends_on: redis: condition: service_healthy` with a 20s start_period, so Redis must be healthy before the backend boots, and `restart: always` would recover a hard failure rather than silently degrade. So the scenario the finder paints (concentrated event loss during rolling deploys across replicas) cannot occur on the current single-container topology.

This is a real latent scale-readiness foot-gun: the moment someone sets deploy.replicas>=2 or migrates to k8s (which the code comments explicitly anticipate — \"k8s rolling restarts\"), broadcasts silently break with no signal. Worth hardening (fail readiness or retry-with-backoff-and-attach when REDIS_URL is set), but it is NOT an active production defect today. High is overstated; low (latent/hardening) is appropriate. Bump to medium only if/when multi-replica is on the near-term roadmap.
findings.evidence: redis-io.adapter.ts:30-93 retries 3x then logs and returns; createIOServer (105-111) installs the Redis adapter only if connected, else keeps in-memory. No readiness gate, no exit, no re-attempt, so a startup Redis blip pins that replica on in-memory for its lifetime.
findings.why_it_matters: With 2+ replicas, a replica that lost the Redis race broadcasts kitchen/POS/order/payment events only to its own sockets; other replicas clients never receive them. Intermittent missed-order/missed-payment by replica, hard to diagnose, pod healthy; concentrated during rolling deploys and Redis blips.
findings.recommendation: Make Redis adapter attachment a hard prod requirement: if REDIS_URL is set but connect fails, fail readiness or retry-with-backoff and attach once connected; keep in-memory fallback only when REDIS_URL is unset.

findings.title: Analytics-edge direct-to-device delivery uses an in-process socket map that breaks across replicas (latent; no live callers today)
findings.evidence: analytics.gateway.ts:70 keeps connectedDevices in-memory; sendConfigToDevice/sendCommandToDevice/sendCalibrationToDevice (530-582) resolve socketId from it and emit to it; under multi-replica a device on another replica is absent. grep shows NO callers, so unused. Broadcasts use room emits (595-632) that fan out via the Redis adapter.
findings.why_it_matters: If edge config/command push is later wired it works only when the admin request lands on the device replica, flaky like the device ignoring config. Dormant today; latent.
findings.recommendation: Before enabling edge command/config push, route it through a room the device joins so the Redis adapter delivers it regardless of replica.

findings.title: Notifications and analytics gateway CORS use bare comma-split without trim, unlike KDS
findings.evidence: notifications.gateway.ts:16-18 and analytics.gateway.ts:16 split CORS_ORIGIN on comma with no trim; kds.gateway.ts:51-55 adds trim/filter and notes a space after a comma silently fails origin matching. Current prod values have no spaces, so not broken today.
findings.why_it_matters: If CORS_ORIGIN is later set with comma-space formatting, notifications/analytics WS handshakes from spaced origins silently fail while KDS works, an inconsistent partial realtime outage.
findings.recommendation: Mirror the KDS trim/filter in both gateways via a shared corsOriginList helper.

findings.title: Device heartbeat issues an extra per-call findUnique just to resolve tenantId for the log
findings.evidence: device.service.ts:344-378 heartbeat does device.update by id then a second prisma.device.findUnique by id selecting tenantId (366-369) per heartbeat with a payload; at 10s cadence per device that is one wasted indexed read per device per 10s.
findings.why_it_matters: Minor DB load amplification under a large fleet; avoidable churn at scale.
findings.recommendation: Return tenantId from the first update via select, or pass it from req.device, instead of a second findUnique.

summary: The outbox machinery itself is genuinely mature and multi-replica-safe: append-inside-the-business-transaction is the documented contract and the money/lifecycle producers (PayTR settlement, subscription, marketplace purchase) follow it with `tx`; the worker claims with `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`, has exponential backoff, a real DLQ, a crash-reclaim reaper for orphaned 'dispatching' rows, retention pruning that excludes failed rows, and an idempotency-key contract the consumers honor. The DomainEventBus correctly isolates per-listener throws so one bad consumer can't abort fan-out or trigger double-projection. However, two of the codebase's own documented footguns are STILL live on main: (1) the cross-replica advisory-lock helper acquires and releases on potentially-different pooled connections, which under Prisma's default multi-connection pool can leak a session-held lock and permanently stall EVERY advisory-locked cron — and it's used by ~15 crons including subscription billing, payment recovery, and webhook delivery; (2) a KDS-routing bus consumer still rethrows "for retry," which is dead (the bus swallows it) and silently drops kitchen-display routing on any transient failure with no retry. There is also no monitoring on outbox backlog/worker-liveness — only DLQ depth — so a stalled worker or stalled cron is invisible. None are single-replica blockers today (prod runs one named container), but the advisory-lock leak can bite on a single instance too because Prisma pools connections within one process, and all three become critical the moment a second replica is added.
strengths: Transactional outbox is correctly modeled and the high-impact money/lifecycle producers append INSIDE the business transaction with `tx`: paytr-settlement.service.ts:342/370 (subscription.activated + payment.succeeded with deterministic idempotencyKey `payment-succeeded:{paymentId}`), and subscription.service.ts:160. This is the durable-without-distributed-transactions pattern done right.

The outbox worker is genuinely multi-replica-safe: drainOnce (outbox-worker.service.ts:253-273) claims batches with `UPDATE ... WHERE id IN (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED LIMIT N) RETURNING`, so concurrent replicas never double-claim; reclaimStuck and pruneOnce are single atomic statements so cross-replica races are harmless redundant work.

Worker crash recovery is implemented: reclaimStuck (outbox-worker.service.ts:204-234) re-queues rows orphaned in 'dispatching' past STUCK_TIMEOUT_MS, stamping the claim time into nextAttemptAt — closing the OOM/SIGKILL-between-claim-and-terminal-write gap, with a Prometheus counter and a loud warn.

DomainEventBus.dispatch (domain-event-bus.service.ts:71-86) wraps every listener in its own try/catch so a thrown consumer is logged but neither aborts the loop nor bubbles to the worker — the explicitly-documented fix for the double-projection-on-retry footgun.

Real DLQ with operator triage: failed rows are never auto-pruned (outbox-worker.service.ts:33-36), SuperAdminOutboxService exposes list/summary/getEvent/requeue with a 100-id cap and an attempt-preserving requeue so a poison pill can't loop forever, and there is an OutboxDLQNonEmpty Prometheus alert (alert.rules.yml:25).

Idempotency is taken seriously: OutboxService.append warns when a DEDUP_REQUIRED_PREFIXES type (payment./subscription./settlement./marketing.) is emitted without a producer idempotencyKey (outbox.service.ts:73-80), and consumers dedupe on event id / unique constraints (webhook fanOut unique (subscriptionId,eventId); kds-routing idempotencyKey `${event.id}:${d.id}:${kind}`).

Retention pruning is bounded and crash-aware: pruneOnce deletes only dispatched rows in capped 5k batches, and the worker warns at boot if OUTBOX_RETENTION_DAYS is below the 7-day webhook-backoff floor (outbox-worker.service.ts:82-88); the webhook worker also handles a purged source event by marking the delivery failed instead of sending {payload:null} (webhook-delivery-worker.service.ts:99-114).

findings.title: Advisory-lock acquire/release on different pooled connections can permanently stall every cron
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is accurate on current main. advisory-lock.ts:27-40 runs `pg_try_advisory_lock` and `pg_advisory_unlock` as two independent `prisma.$queryRawUnsafe` calls with no transaction/connection pinning, and the same try-acquire/finally-unlock pattern is duplicated inline in order-polling.scheduler.ts:48-61, token-refresh.scheduler.ts, stock-alerts.scheduler.ts:75, z-report-scheduler.service.ts:55, and subscription-scheduler.service.ts withJobLock (61-74). PrismaService (prisma.service.ts) passes only `datasources.db.url` — no `connection_limit` — and no DATABASE_URL anywhere (docker-compose.prod.yml:90, .env*) sets one, so Prisma defaults to a multi-connection pool (num_cpus*2+1). Session-level pg advisory locks are bound to the backend connection that acquired them, so an unlock checked out on a different pooled connection is a silent no-op and the lock leaks until the connection recycles. No mitigation exists: no `$transaction`/`pg_advisory_xact_lock` wrapping, no advisory-lock fix branch on main, and advisory-lock.spec.ts mocks `$queryRawUnsafe` so the cross-connection behavior is never tested. The recommended xact-lock fix is correct.

I downgrade critical→high on adversarial scrutiny of the IMPACT determinism, not the existence of the bug. (1) The failure is probabilistic, not the claimed deterministic "permanently stall every cron": pg advisory locks are re-entrant within a session, and Node/Prisma idle-pool reuse is effectively LIFO, so for a single sequential cron tick on an idle process the acquire and unlock very often check out the SAME idle connection and the lock IS released correctly. A permanent stall requires the unlock to land cross-connection AND subsequent ticks' try-acquire to keep landing on non-holding connections — likely under pool churn/load, but not guaranteed every tick. (2) Prod currently runs a SINGLE backend container (no deploy.replicas in docker-compose.prod.yml), so the cross-replica duplicate-work scenario the lock guards against is presently moot; the live risk is the intra-process self-leak, which is real but intermittent. Still genuinely high: when it bites, a money/webhook cron (subscription billing, paytr-pending-recovery, webhook delivery) silently skips indefinitely with zero error logs, it is latent across ~15 crons, untested, and worsens precisely when the system scales (more pool/load → more cross-connection unlocks, and multi-replica would make the original lock semantics matter too).
findings.evidence: src/common/scheduling/advisory-lock.ts:27-40 runs `SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(...)` and `SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(...)` as two separate `prisma.$queryRawUnsafe` calls. pg advisory locks acquired via the session-level `pg_advisory_lock`/`pg_try_advisory_lock` are held by the backend connection (session) that acquired them. PrismaService (src/prisma/prisma.service.ts) sets no `connection_limit`, and DATABASE_URL (docker-compose.prod.yml:90) has none, so Prisma opens a pool of num_cpus*2+1 connections. The two raw queries are independent operations that can each grab a different free pooled connection, so the unlock frequently runs on a connection that does not hold the lock (returns false, silently). The lock then stays held on the acquiring connection until it is recycled; on the NEXT tick `pg_try_advisory_lock` on any OTHER pooled connection sees a different session holding it and returns false → the job is SKIPPED. The identical pattern is duplicated inline in order-polling.scheduler.ts:48-60, token-refresh.scheduler.ts:24-36, stock-alerts.scheduler.ts:38-75, z-report-scheduler.ts:33-55, and subscription-scheduler.service.ts withJobLock (62-74). advisory-lock.spec.ts mocks `$queryRawUnsafe`, so the cross-connection behavior is never exercised by tests. The fix branch is not on main (git: rethrower-era code current).
findings.why_it_matters: ~15 crons rely on this helper — subscription-period-end billing, past-due/trial-expiration, paytr-pending-recovery, self-pay sweeper, webhook delivery, device-mesh sweep, entitlement reprojection, delivery reconciliation. A single leaked lock makes subsequent ticks on other pool connections see the lock held and silently skip, so a billing/recovery/webhook cron can stop running indefinitely with zero error logs. This bites even on the single prod replica because Prisma pools connections within one process, and it gets worse, not better, under load (more pool churn).
findings.recommendation: Pin acquire+run+release to ONE connection by running the whole critical section inside a single `prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { ... }, { timeout })` and using transaction-scoped `pg_advisory_xact_lock(id)` (auto-released on COMMIT/ROLLBACK, no manual unlock, no cross-connection risk), or run the three statements via a dedicated pinned client. Add an integration test against a real Postgres that proves the lock is released (a second acquire on a fresh connection succeeds) after the body completes.

findings.title: KDS-routing bus consumer rethrows 'for retry' — dead throw silently drops kitchen-display routing
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every cited fact is true on current main. (1) kds-routing.service.ts:140-143 wraps the ENTIRE onOrderEvent body in one try/catch and does `throw e; // bubble back to the outbox worker for retry`. (2) DomainEventBus.dispatch (domain-event-bus.service.ts:66-86) iterates listeners and wraps each in try/catch that logs via logger.error and NEVER rethrows — confirmed at lines 77-84; the class docstring (lines 29-41) explicitly documents this contract ("dispatch() swallows per-listener errors", "Consumers that need their own retry pipeline must persist a row + drive a worker"). (3) OutboxWorkerService.drainOnce awaits bus.dispatch() (line 277), which therefore never throws, then unconditionally marks the row status='dispatched' (lines 314-321). So the throw at kds-routing:142 is genuinely dead: it triggers neither an outbox retry (the row is marked dispatched) nor any abort (dispatch already swallowed it). Its only observable effect is the duplicate logger.warn line.

No mitigation exists elsewhere. I grepped kds-routing/ and device-mesh/ for any reconcile/cron that re-routes orders to KDS mesh screens — there is none (device-mesh.scheduler.ts only does token/heartbeat upkeep). This is the critical asymmetry vs other consumers: the entitlements consumer also doesn't rely on the dead throw, but it self-heals via a nightly reproject reconcile cron (entitlements.module.ts:77-84 documents exactly this). KDS routing has NO equivalent safety net, so a transient deviceCommand.enqueue failure (DB blip, lock timeout, pool exhaustion) means the order is silently never shown on the mesh KDS screen and is never recovered.

Additionally the single outer try/catch means a failure on any one device's enqueue aborts the for-loop (line 129-139), dropping the remaining devices' commands too — so the finder's "one device failure drops the others" claim is also correct.

The other consumers' non-rethrow claims verified: entitlements.module.ts:106-113 ("Do NOT rethrow — the bus already swallows"). Branch fix/kds-routing-dispatch-resilience exists locally and is unmerged (git branch confirms).

Severity nuance (why high, not critical): the created→updated path gives a second chance — a one-off blip on order.created.v1 is usually re-covered by the subsequent order.updated.v1 emitting its own event with the SAME idempotencyKey base but a different event.id (so a fresh enqueue attempt). The genuinely unrecoverable cases are (a) a sustained DB issue spanning both events, and (b) clear_order on completed/cancelled which has no follow-up event. The idempotency key includes event.id (line 137), so retries don't duplicate. Real production impact is real (kitchen silently misses a ticket with zero retry/reconcile), matching "high"; not a blocker/critical because the common transient case is often masked by the follow-up update event and the mesh-KDS path coexists with the still-live legacy Socket.IO KDS gateway. The recommendation (remove the dead throw, per-device try/catch, and for true durability persist a routing row + worker like WebhookDelivery) is exactly right and matches the codebase's own documented established pattern.
findings.evidence: src/modules/kds-routing/kds-routing.service.ts:140-142: `catch (e) { this.logger.warn(...); throw e; // bubble back to the outbox worker for retry }`. But DomainEventBus.dispatch (domain-event-bus.service.ts:77-84) catches and swallows every listener throw, and OutboxWorkerService.drainOnce (outbox-worker.service.ts:277, 314-321) marks the event `dispatched` as soon as `bus.dispatch()` returns (it never throws). So the throw is dead: it neither retries nor aborts other listeners — the only effect is a duplicate error log line. The other consumers explicitly do NOT rethrow (entitlements.module.ts:106-113 'Do NOT rethrow', checkout-notifications.service.ts:52). The dispatch-resilience fix branch `fix/kds-routing-dispatch-resilience` exists but is unmerged.
findings.why_it_matters: If the per-device deviceCommand enqueue hits a transient error (DB blip, lock timeout) for order.created/updated/completed/cancelled, the order is NEVER routed to the kitchen-display screens and there is NO retry — the kitchen silently never sees the ticket. This is the exact 'rethrow for retry that is actually dead' footgun the codebase has hit repeatedly.
findings.recommendation: Remove the `throw e` at kds-routing.service.ts:142 and handle each device enqueue in its own try/catch so one device failure doesn't drop the others; if true retry is needed, persist a per-device routing row and drive it with a worker (the established WebhookDelivery pattern) rather than relying on the bus.

findings.title: No monitoring on outbox backlog or worker liveness — a stalled worker/cron is invisible
findings.verify_reasoning: All technical claims verified true on main. (1) The only outbox alert is OutboxDLQNonEmpty / `outbox_dlq_depth > 0` (alert.rules.yml:25-26); grepping the rules file for reclaim/queued/dispatching/oldest returns zero matches. (2) `incOutboxDlqDepth()` fires only in the `if (final)` branch where final = attempts>=MAX_ATTEMPTS (outbox-worker.service.ts:329,348), and the hourly prune re-syncs the gauge to count(status='failed') (149-153) — so the gauge tracks ONLY status='failed'. (3) If the poll loop (tick/scheduleNext setTimeout chain at 101-104,160-182) stops entirely, rows accumulate in 'queued'/'dispatching', nothing reaches 'failed', the DLQ stays 0, and no alert fires. The reclaim reaper (204-234) only runs while the loop still ticks, so a fully-dead loop reclaims nothing either.
