(4) `outbox_events_reclaimed_total` is incremented (228) but has NO alert rule — the code comment at 221-227 literally says it was exposed 'so an alert can fire' yet none was added. (5) SuperAdminOutboxService.summary() (60-70) computes queued/dispatching counts only on-demand via the admin API; MetricsService has only outboxDlqDepthGauge + deliveryDlqDepthGauge and no generic gauge setter (42-86), confirming no queued-depth/oldest-age gauge and the worker comment (226) calls adding one out of scope. No mitigation elsewhere: BackendDown (up==0) only catches a process/endpoint that is fully unreachable — a live backend whose outbox loop silently died keeps `up`=1 and keeps serving /api/metrics, so worker liveness is genuinely unmonitored. Severity adjusted from high to medium: this is a real and well-argued observability gap on money/entitlement paths (and the existing DLQ alert gives false confidence), but it is a detection-latency/monitoring defect rather than a correctness or data-loss defect — the delivery machinery (reclaim reaper, backoff, DLQ, at-least-once dedup) is sound, so events are delayed-not-lost while a stalled worker is undetected. Strong medium, leaning high.
findings.evidence: The only outbox alert is OutboxDLQNonEmpty (ops/monitoring/alert.rules.yml:25, expr `outbox_dlq_depth > 0`). DLQ depth only increments when an event exhausts MAX_ATTEMPTS (outbox-worker.service.ts:348). If the worker stops draining entirely (deploy bug, unhandled rejection, or — per the advisory-lock finding — a cron/worker stall), events accumulate in status='queued'/'dispatching' and NOTHING reaches 'failed', so the DLQ stays at 0 and no alert fires. SuperAdminOutboxService.summary() (superadmin-outbox.service.ts:60-70) computes queued/dispatching/dispatched counts but only on-demand via the admin API — there is no gauge and no alert. The reclaim counter `outbox_events_reclaimed_total` (outbox-worker.service.ts:228) also has no alert rule.
findings.why_it_matters: The whole point of the outbox is at-least-once delivery of money/entitlement events (payment.succeeded → commission, subscription.activated → entitlement reprojection). A silently-stalled worker means paid upgrades never grant features and commissions never credit, with no page firing until a customer complains. Backlog/oldest-event-age is the leading indicator; DLQ depth is a trailing one.
findings.recommendation: Add a periodically-set gauge for outbox queued depth and oldest-pending-event age (and dispatching depth), expose `outbox_events_reclaimed_total` as an alert (rate > 0 = a worker crashed), and add alerts: queued depth or oldest-age above a threshold for N minutes = worker not draining.

findings.title: Cron status-write and outbox.append are separate non-transactional ops with a swallowed catch — lost event on append failure, never re-emitted
findings.evidence: src/modules/marketplace/tenant-addon-sweeper.service.ts: the status flip `tenantAddOn.updateMany({status:'expired'})` (105-108) / `'past_due'` (168-171) commits, then a SEPARATE `outbox.append({AddOnCancelled/AddOnPastDue}).catch(captureSwallowedEmit(...))` (117-132, 144-159, 177-193) runs without a `tx` and swallows failures. Because the next cron run re-reads the row, finds the status already terminal, and `claim.count === 0 → continue` (109-112, 172-175), the append is NEVER retried. If the DB blips between the status commit and the append, the entitlement-revoke/notify event is silently lost forever. Same pattern shape (create/update then separately .append().catch()) appears at customer-orders.service.ts:69, caller.service.ts:97, kds.service.ts:80, local-bridge.service.ts:135.
findings.why_it_matters: A lost AddOnCancelled means the projector never revokes the grant, so a tenant keeps a paid add-on capability for free after their period ends (or never gets the past-due grace/notification). It is the recurring 'row then separately .append().catch() the outbox event' lost-event-on-failure class the codebase has fixed elsewhere (caller, kds-routing) by wrapping in one $transaction with a tx-aware append.
findings.recommendation: For the sweeper's terminal transitions, wrap the status updateMany and outbox.append in a single $transaction and pass `tx` to append (OutboxService.append already accepts a tx), so the event is durable iff the status change committed; or make the cron re-emit on a status it can re-observe.

findings.title: Outbox DLQ-depth gauge is per-replica but re-synced to the global COUNT — misleading value under multiple replicas
findings.evidence: MetricsService.outboxDlqDepthGauge is a process-local prom-client Gauge (metrics.service.ts:69-76). incOutboxDlqDepth() fires on whichever replica processed the failing event (outbox-worker.service.ts:348), but pruneOnce re-syncs with `setOutboxDlqDepth(COUNT(status='failed'))` (outbox-worker.service.ts:149-153) — a GLOBAL count — on every replica. So with N replicas, each instance's gauge reports the full global DLQ depth, and between prunes the inc()s drift per-instance.
findings.why_it_matters: The OutboxDLQNonEmpty alert (>0) still fires correctly so it is not a reliability hole, but any dashboard summing or comparing the gauge across instances over-counts, which can mislead an operator triaging a DLQ incident. Only relevant once more than one replica runs.
findings.recommendation: Either scrape this as a single-source gauge (only the leader sets it) or document that it is a global snapshot and aggregate with max() not sum() in Prometheus/dashboards.

dimension: Health checks, graceful shutdown & deploy gating
summary: The deploy machinery is unusually mature for a single-VPS docker-compose SaaS: scripts/deploy.sh does pre-deploy DB backup with content verification, immutable :vX.Y.Z → :current atomic retag, a real health-gated swap loop (wait_until_healthy), automatic image-SHA snapshot + ERR-trap rollback, and a CI pre-flight that refuses to deploy onto an already-unhealthy prod. However, the actual health-check contract used for gating is broken in a way that defeats much of that safety: the endpoint every gate relies on (/api/health) returns HTTP 200 even when Postgres or Redis is down ("status":"degraded"), so a backend that cannot reach its database will pass the deploy gate and be promoted to :current, and the docker healthcheck will report the container "healthy". The well-designed liveness/readiness split (/healthz/live, /healthz/ready) exists in code but is (a) never wired into either the container healthcheck or the deploy gate, and (b) the readiness endpoint never actually returns 503 despite a code comment claiming it does. Graceful shutdown is partial: Nest enableShutdownHooks drains Prisma/Redis-health clients, but the Socket.IO Redis pub/sub clients are leaked (disconnectRedis is dead code), there is no pre-stop drain delay, and prod has no stop_grace_period. Because this is single-replica with no nginx upstream/retry, every container swap is a hard cutover with a 502 window. None of these are catastrophic at one or two tenants, but they will produce silent bad deploys and visible blips as paying tenants scale.
strengths: Deploy is genuinely health-gated, not sleep-based: scripts/deploy.sh wait_until_healthy() (lines 414-442) polls the API on a 5s interval up to HEALTH_BUDGET_SEC and aborts the deploy (firing the ERR-trap rollback) if it never goes 200, plus it dumps the last 200 backend log lines + container state on failure for forensics.

Robust automatic rollback: snapshot_image_ids() records the prior image SHAs to a state file outside the repo before the swap, and on_failure()/restore_image_ids() re-pin :current to the previous SHA and recreate containers; the ERR trap is installed only for deploy (not rollback) so it can't recurse.

Pre-deploy safety: backup_database() takes a gzip'd pg_dump and verifies it (>=1KB, >=5 CREATE statements) before proceeding, and the release-deploy.yml workflow (lines 65-78) refuses to start a deploy if prod /api/health is already unhealthy ('deploying on a fire = worse fire').

Health probes are bounded against hung dependencies: AppService.getHealth() wraps both the prisma SELECT 1 and redis.ping() in a 2s withTimeout race (app.service.ts:16-25,58-75) and the Redis health client is configured maxRetriesPerRequest:1 / retryStrategy:()=>null, so a half-dead Redis socket can't stall the probe and cause restart storms.

Liveness and readiness ARE conceptually separated (app.controller.ts:62-129): /healthz/live is pure no-I/O, /healthz/ready short-circuits on first failed subsystem and bounds the KMS probe with its own 2s timeout — the design intent is correct even though it isn't deployed.

Data layer is correctly ordered: prod compose backend depends_on postgres+redis with condition: service_healthy, and both have real healthchecks with sensible start_period (redis 20s cold-start, backend 240s to cover the ~3-4min cold route registration); ensure_data_layer() independently waits for both to report healthy before the backend swap.

Nest enableShutdownHooks() is enabled (main.ts:232) and PrismaService.onModuleDestroy $disconnect()s the DB while AppService.onModuleDestroy quit()s the health Redis client — the core DB connection drains cleanly on SIGTERM.

findings.title: /api/health returns HTTP 200 when the database or Redis is down — the deploy gate and container healthcheck cannot detect a backend that lost its DB
findings.verify_reasoning:
The core technical claim is TRUE on current main. app.service.ts:41-83 getHealth() only sets checks.status='degraded' (lines 66,79) on DB/Redis failure and always returns the object; app.controller.ts:41-46 getHealth() has no @HttpCode and no throw → HTTP 200 in all cases. Both gates point at this exact endpoint: docker-compose.prod.yml:138 uses curl -f http://0.0.0.0:3000/api/health, and deploy.sh sets API_LOCAL_URL=.../api/health (lines 116/150) which wait_until_healthy (line 414-428) probes with `curl -sf`. Since curl -f/-sf only fails on HTTP>=400, a 200-degraded passes the gate. I confirmed there is NO status-code mitigation anywhere: the only @HttpCode in the controller is the explicit 200 on /healthz/live; no interceptor/filter rewrites the health response to 503.

Important missed nuance #1 (against the finder): A dedicated correct route was attempted but is intentionally NOT used. /healthz/ready (controller lines 72-129) reports degradation as body `{ok:false}` — but it ALSO returns HTTP 200 (no @HttpCode/throw), so even it would pass `curl -f`. The compose comments (prod:133-137, staging:95-102) show the team deliberately chose /api/health over /healthz/ready for gating because /healthz/ready doesn't exist in older images and broke rollback probing. So the finder's recommendation to 'point the gate at /healthz/ready' would NOT fix anything today and conflicts with a documented rollback constraint — the real fix is to make the chosen endpoint emit 503 on status!='ok'.

Important nuance #2 (severity overstated): The finder's flagship trigger examples — 'wrong DATABASE_URL, password drift, DB still recovering' at deploy time — are mostly already caught. prisma.service.ts:32-35 onModuleInit awaits $connect(), and main.ts:273 app.listen() runs only after Nest finishes init. A bad/unreachable DATABASE_URL therefore throws during bootstrap, the listener never opens, and the gate gets connection-refused (curl fails) → rollback fires. deploy.sh:434 even special-cases the 'never opened its listener' path for forensic logs. So the gate IS blind only to a narrower window: DB/Redis loss AFTER a successful boot (pool can't reconnect mid-deploy, DB failover/recovery, network partition during the health-budget poll). That is a genuine real-user risk (promotes a degraded image to :current and Docker keeps it 'healthy' / in nginx routing while DB-touching requests 500), but it is materially narrower than 'the most common failure mode,' so 'high' is overstated.

Net: real, exploitable-in-a-window, worth fixing (emit 503 from getHealth/readiness when status!='ok', keeping the durable /api/health path so rollback stays probe-able), but severity is medium not high given the boot-time cases self-protect and a same-endpoint readiness scaffold already exists.
findings.evidence: backend/src/app.service.ts:41-83 getHealth() sets checks.status='degraded' (lines 66,79) on DB/Redis failure but always returns the object; backend/src/app.controller.ts:41-46 getHealth() returns it with no @HttpCode and no throw → HTTP 200 in all cases. Both gates use this exact endpoint as a pass/fail: docker-compose.prod.yml:138 healthcheck test ['CMD','curl','-f','http://0.0.0.0:3000/api/health'] and scripts/deploy.sh:421 `curl -sf ... "$url"` against API_LOCAL_URL=.../api/health. `curl -f` only fails on HTTP >=400, so a 200-degraded passes.
findings.why_it_matters: A backend image that boots but cannot reach Postgres (wrong DATABASE_URL, password drift, DB still recovering, network partition) will return 200 'degraded', so deploy.sh promotes it to :current and Docker keeps the container 'healthy' and in nginx's routing path. Every tenant request that touches the DB then 500s while monitoring shows green. The rollback safety net never triggers because the gate it depends on is blind to the most common failure mode.
findings.recommendation: Make /api/health (and/or a dedicated readiness route) return 503 when status!='ok' — e.g. throw new ServiceUnavailableException(checks) from the controller, or set res.status(HttpStatus.SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE). Keep a separate always-200 liveness route for restart decisions. At minimum, point the deploy gate and container healthcheck at /healthz/ready and fix that endpoint's status code (see next finding).

findings.title: /healthz/ready never returns 503 despite a code comment claiming it does — readiness is non-functional as a gate
findings.evidence: backend/src/app.controller.ts:53-54 comment: 'Returns 503 when any subsystem is unhealthy so the orchestrator stops routing.' But readiness() (lines 72-129) has NO @HttpCode decorator and every branch returns a plain object ({ ok:false, base, checks } on failure, line 84/107/116/125 and { ok:true } at 128). NestJS returns 200 for a GET handler that resolves an object, so a failed readiness check is served as HTTP 200 {ok:false}. No spec exercises the status code (no app.controller.spec.ts and no e2e references /healthz/ready except staging-diagnose.yml which only prints it).
findings.why_it_matters: The one endpoint specifically designed to gate traffic on dependency health is silently a no-op: any consumer (a future K8s readiness probe, an LB, or a corrected deploy gate) that trusts the HTTP status would treat a fully-broken backend as ready. The misleading comment will also cause an engineer to wire it in believing it works.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit status handling: inject @Res({passthrough:true}) and set res.status(503) (or throw ServiceUnavailableException with the checks payload) on every !ok return path; keep 200 only for the all-ok case. Add a spec asserting 503 when base.status!=='ok' and when a subsystem check fails.

findings.title: Single-replica hard-cutover swap with no proxy retry → guaranteed 502 window on every backend deploy
findings.evidence: Deployment is single-replica (docker-compose.prod.yml backend has no replicas/scale; staging 'deploy:' blocks at lines 61/117 are resource limits only). scripts/deploy.sh:444-462 swap_backend() runs `dc up -d --force-recreate backend` which stops the old container before the new one is listening, then sleeps 3s and polls health for up to HEALTH_BUDGET_SEC (300s). nginx (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:44-57) proxies /api/, /socket.io/, /uploads/ to a single `http://127.0.0.1:3000` with no upstream block, no proxy_next_upstream, no max_fails/retry. grep confirms 'no upstream/retry directives'.
findings.why_it_matters: From the moment the old container stops until the new one passes its first health poll (seconds to minutes on a cold 240s-start_period boot), nginx gets connection-refused and returns 502 to every API/websocket/upload request. For active POS terminals and KDS screens mid-service this is a visible outage on every release, and the long cold-start makes the window worse. It is not zero-downtime despite the atomic :current retag design.
findings.recommendation: Move to a brief overlap: start the new backend on a second port/container, wait for it to pass /healthz/ready, then atomically switch nginx upstream (or run 2 replicas behind an nginx upstream with max_fails/proxy_next_upstream so the proxy drains the draining instance). At minimum add proxy_next_upstream error timeout http_502 + a short retry to nginx so transient swap errors are retried rather than surfaced.

findings.title: Graceful shutdown leaks Socket.IO Redis pub/sub clients and has no in-flight drain delay
findings.evidence: RedisIoAdapter.disconnectRedis() (backend/src/common/adapters/redis-io.adapter.ts:96-103) quits the pub/sub clients but `grep disconnectRedis` shows it is NEVER called — no provider implements OnApplicationShutdown (grep for onApplicationShutdown/beforeApplicationShutdown across src returns nothing). enableShutdownHooks (main.ts:232) only fires Nest lifecycle hooks on providers; the websocket adapter is not one. There is also no pre-stop delay: SIGTERM → app.close() begins immediately with no 'fail readiness, sleep N, then close' step. Prod compose has no stop_grace_period (docker default 10s; grep confirms NONE in prod).
findings.why_it_matters: On every restart/deploy the two Socket.IO Redis connections are abandoned to TCP timeout instead of a clean QUIT (connection churn on Redis, noisy error logs). More importantly, with no drain delay and no readiness-fail-before-close, in-flight HTTP requests and active websocket sessions are cut at SIGTERM; combined with the 10s default grace, a shutdown that takes longer (DB disconnect, OTel flush) risks a SIGKILL mid-drain. Under real load this drops requests/orders during routine deploys.
findings.recommendation: Wire disconnectRedis() into shutdown (make RedisIoAdapter a provider implementing OnApplicationShutdown, or call it explicitly in a SIGTERM handler before app.close()). Add a pre-stop step that flips readiness to unhealthy, waits a few seconds for the proxy/clients to drain, then app.close(). Set an explicit stop_grace_period (e.g. 30s) in prod compose to give Nest time to finish app.close().

findings.title: Node runs as PID 1 with no init — zombie reaping and signal-forwarding edge cases on crash/OOM restart
findings.evidence: backend/Dockerfile:76 production stage CMD ['node','dist/main'] with no tini/dumb-init (grep for STOPSIGNAL/tini/dumb-init returns nothing). The app spawns child processes (e.g. PDF/canvas native libs, npx prisma migrate deploy is exec'd into the container during deploy at scripts/deploy.sh:461). Prod backend also has no memory limit (only staging sets deploy.resources.limits.memory 1G), so OOM behavior is host-OOM-killer driven.
findings.why_it_matters: As PID 1, Node won't reap orphaned child processes (slow zombie accumulation) and PID-1 signal semantics differ subtly — SIGTERM is delivered but only because Nest installs its own handler; any path that bypasses that (or a wedged event loop) means Docker falls back to SIGKILL after the grace period, skipping graceful shutdown entirely. No prod memory limit means a leak takes the whole host's OOM killer rather than a bounded container restart.
findings.recommendation: Add tini/dumb-init as the entrypoint (ENTRYPOINT ['/usr/bin/tini','--'] then CMD node dist/main) for correct reaping and signal forwarding, and set a deploy.resources.limits.memory on the prod backend so OOM is contained to the container (with restart:always already set) instead of the host.

findings.title: Stale legacy root deploy.sh references a non-existent path — manual-run footgun
findings.evidence: Root ./deploy.sh (separate file; `diff -q deploy.sh scripts/deploy.sh` => DIFFERENT) is a legacy 'KDS Deployment Script' with PROJECT_DIR=/opt/kds (deploy.sh:18), while the real prod path is scripts/deploy.sh invoked by .github/workflows/release-deploy.yml:485. The two are not interchangeable and the root one points at a directory the live deploy doesn't use.
findings.why_it_matters: An operator under incident pressure who runs ./deploy.sh from the repo root (the obvious name) gets the wrong, /opt/kds-targeted script instead of the maintained scripts/deploy.sh — risking a confusing failure or acting on the wrong directory during a recovery.
findings.recommendation: Delete the legacy root deploy.sh or replace its body with an exec shim that forwards to scripts/deploy.sh (or prints a clear 'use scripts/deploy.sh' error and exits).

findings.title: Public /api/health leaks internal version/environment and uptime to unauthenticated callers
findings.evidence: backend/src/app.controller.ts:41-46 getHealth is @Public(); backend/src/app.service.ts:43-53 returns service name, hardcoded version '1.0.0', environment (process.env.NODE_ENV), process.uptime(), and per-subsystem database/redis health strings. nginx exposes /api/ publicly (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:44). (Note: the richer /healthz/ready, which would also enumerate installed payment/fiscal provider ids, is also @Public but not currently routed by nginx beyond /api/.)
findings.why_it_matters: Unauthenticated exposure of environment, uptime, and subsystem-up/down state gives an attacker free reconnaissance (e.g. detect a Redis outage to time an attack, infer deploy cadence from uptime). Low impact alone, but readiness endpoints in this codebase are designed to expose KMS/payment/fiscal provider inventories which must never be public.
findings.recommendation: Keep /api/health minimal for the LB (just {status} + 503 mapping), and gate the detailed/ready endpoints (subsystem breakdown, provider inventories) behind an internal-network restriction (nginx allow 127.0.0.1/internal CIDR; deny all on /api/healthz/ready) or the existing INTERNAL_SERVICE_TOKEN, so detailed internals aren't world-readable.

summary: The edge is functional but minimal: certbot-managed TLS with HTTP->HTTPS redirect + ACME, correct websocket Upgrade headers for socket.io, and well-thought-out static-asset caching in the frontend container. However, several production-readiness gaps will bite a multi-tenant, multi-replica deployment fronted by Cloudflare. The most serious are (1) every app container (including the backend API) is published on 0.0.0.0 so the origin is directly reachable bypassing nginx/TLS, with no firewall codified in the repo; (2) the topology is Cloudflare -> host nginx -> backend (two hops) but the app trusts only one proxy hop and nginx never restores the real client IP from CF-Connecting-IP, so req.ip is the Cloudflare edge IP — which silently breaks IP-keyed rate limiting, audit IPs, and the PayTR allowlist fallback; and (3) Prometheus/Alertmanager are exposed publicly and unauthenticated. The edge also lacks any rate limiting, HSTS, compression, proxy timeouts, and a real upstream/health-failover model. The prod vhost is self-described as "reconstructed" and unverified against the live server.
strengths: HTTP->HTTPS is enforced on every vhost with a 301, and the ACME webroot challenge is preserved on :80 so cert renewal keeps working (ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:19-25, staging:21-28, developer/help:21-27).

Websocket upgrade is correctly handled: the /socket.io/ location sets proxy_http_version 1.1 + Upgrade $http_upgrade + Connection 'upgrade' (hummytummy.com.conf:52-57), and the docs portals forward the same headers for Next RSC streaming (developer.conf:48-53, help.conf:48-53). Real-time is socket.io only (no SSE found), so this is the right surface to cover.

Frontend container nginx has a careful caching policy: fingerprinted assets get expires 1y + immutable, while index.html / SPA-fallback are explicitly no-store — with an inline comment explaining the prior Cloudflare-edge-cache-freeze incident (frontend/nginx.conf:19-58).

TLS config is delegated to certbot's options-ssl-nginx.conf + ssl-dhparams.pem (hummytummy.com.conf:34-35), which on current certbot disables TLS 1.0/1.1 and ships a modern cipher suite; HTTP/2 is enabled on all 443 listeners.

Datastores are correctly locked to loopback: Postgres and Redis publish only on 127.0.0.1 in prod and staging, with an explicit comment about minimizing blast radius (docker-compose.prod.yml:27, staging.yml:17-18,46-47).

apply.sh is a safe deploy primitive: it backs up the live vhost, runs nginx -t, and only reloads on pass (restoring the backup otherwise), and deploys deliberately do NOT auto-apply vhosts (ops/nginx/apply.sh:20-38, README.md:47-50).
client_max_body_size 25m provides a sane DoS ceiling on request bodies at the edge while comfortably covering single-file (5MB) uploads (hummytummy.com.conf:37).

findings.title: Backend API and all app containers are published on 0.0.0.0 — origin is directly reachable, bypassing nginx/TLS/edge, with no firewall in the repo
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every cited line is accurate on main. docker-compose.prod.yml publishes backend '3000:3000' (line 128), frontend '8080:80' (168), landing '3100:3000' (194), developer '3200:3000' (217), help '3201:3000' (244) — all with no host-IP prefix, so Docker binds 0.0.0.0 and the host iptables DOCKER chain accepts WAN traffic to those ports. By deliberate contrast, postgres/redis ARE pinned to 127.0.0.1 (prod:27; staging:17-18,46-47) with an explicit "public exposure would widen its blast radius" comment (staging:9) — confirming the maintainers know the loopback-bind technique and simply didn't apply it to the app tier. The nginx vhost proxies to 127.0.0.1:3000/8080 (hummytummy.com.conf:45,53,63) and terminates TLS via Let's Encrypt (32-35), so legitimate ingress is HTTPS through nginx — but http://<VPS-IP>:3000/api/* is reachable directly in plaintext, bypassing TLS/HSTS and (per ops/nginx/README:4) Cloudflare. The firewall claim is also true: a repo-wide grep for ufw/iptables/nftables/cloudflared finds only prose (SETUP.md:438, docs/GITHUB_SECRETS.md:349, EMAIL_SETUP.md:168); scripts/deploy*.sh codify no host firewall and ops/nginx has no Cloudflare-IP allow/deny. No mitigation exists elsewhere.

Two findings make the exposure WORSE, not better, than the report implies: (1) ThrottlerModule.forRoot (app.module.ts:82-98) defines only short/medium/long named throttlers and NO 'default' — a known deferred infra defect — so the ~60 @Throttle({default}) decorators across the app are inert; direct-to-origin brute-force/flood is genuinely close to unthrottled. (2) main.ts sets 'trust proxy' = 1 (or TRUST_PROXY), so an attacker hitting :3000 directly controls X-Forwarded-For, letting them spoof source IPs in logs and defeat any IP-based throttling that did exist.

Minor nuances that keep this from being overstated: the origin IP is plainly in the repo (38.242.233.166) so 'scanning' is trivial; the repo is also PUBLIC (per memory), so the IP is internet-discoverable. nginx does NOT restrict ingress to Cloudflare ranges either, so even port 80/443 origin-pull is unauthenticated — the :3000 hole is the sharpest edge but the same host-firewall gap covers 80/443. Severity is justified: this is a multi-tenant SaaS about to onboard paying tenants, the full authenticated+unauthenticated API surface answers in plaintext off-edge, and the only protections claimed (Cloudflare WAF/TLS/rate-limit) are entirely bypassable. Blocker is appropriate.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.prod.yml:127-128 publishes the backend as '3000:3000' (binds 0.0.0.0), and frontend '8080:80' (167-168), landing '3100:3000' (193-194), developer '3200:3000' (216-217), help '3201:3000' (243-244) are all 0.0.0.0-bound. Only Postgres/Redis are pinned to 127.0.0.1 (compose:27, staging:17-18,46-47), with a comment that public exposure 'would widen its blast radius' — the same reasoning was not applied to the API. The nginx vhosts proxy to 127.0.0.1:3000/8080 (hummytummy.com.conf:45,63), but http://<VPS-IP>:3000/api/* answers directly in plaintext, no TLS, and no edge controls. A grep for ufw/iptables/nftables/cloudflared across the repo finds only prose mentions in SETUP.md:438 and docs/GITHUB_SECRETS.md:349 — no firewall is codified anywhere.
findings.why_it_matters: Any attacker who knows or scans the VPS IP (38.242.233.166 is in the repo) reaches the full multi-tenant API over plaintext HTTP, completely bypassing TLS, HSTS, the (admittedly absent) edge rate limits, and Cloudflare's WAF/DDoS shield. Cloudflare-only protection is worthless if the origin IP is hittable directly: an attacker scrapes tenant data, brute-forces auth, and floods the backend unthrottled. This is the single biggest edge exposure before onboarding paying tenants.
findings.recommendation: Bind every public app container to 127.0.0.1 (e.g. '127.0.0.1:3000:3000') exactly as Postgres/Redis already are, so nginx is the only ingress. Additionally codify a host firewall (ufw/nftables) that allows only 80/443 (and 22 from admin) from the internet, and ideally restrict 80/443 to Cloudflare's published IP ranges. Add this to deploy/bootstrap so a rebuilt host is not silently wide open.

findings.title: trust proxy = 1 under a 2-hop Cloudflare->nginx chain — real client IP is lost, silently breaking IP rate-limiting, audit IPs, and the PayTR allowlist fallback
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is TRUE on current main. Verified the full chain:

1. main.ts:58-64 — `trust proxy` = `process.env.TRUST_PROXY` or default `1`. Grep confirms TRUST_PROXY is set NOWHERE (only referenced in main.ts and a QUALITY_AUDIT.md mention) — never in docker-compose.prod.yml or any env file. So prod runs with the default of 1 hop.

2. Topology = TWO proxy hops in front of Express: visitor -> Cloudflare -> host nginx -> backend. Confirmed by ops/nginx/README.md (CF terminates, host nginx routes to 127.0.0.1:3000) and hummytummy.com.conf (proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000; X-Forwarded-For via $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for, line 41).

3. NO Cloudflare real_ip config anywhere: grep of all ops/nginx/*.conf finds X-Real-IP/X-Forwarded-For proxy_set_header lines but ZERO `set_real_ip_from`, `real_ip_header CF-Connecting-IP`, or `real_ip_recursive`. Confirmed across all 5 conf files.

4. Express math: backend is published `3000:3000` in docker-compose.prod.yml, so host nginx hits it through the Docker NAT — Express's socket peer is the docker bridge gateway, NOT loopback/CF. The XFF reaching Express is `<visitor>, <cf-edge>` (Cloudflare appends the real visitor, nginx's $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for appends the CF edge IP). With trust proxy=1, Express strips ONE trusted hop from the right of [xff..., socketPeer], yielding req.ip = <cf-edge IP>, not the visitor. To resolve the true visitor you'd need trust proxy=2 (or CF real_ip rewrite). Finding's conclusion is correct.

Consumers verified to use this wrong value:
- machine-throttler.guard.ts:21,38 — global APP_GUARD keys throttle buckets on req.ips[0]/req.ip = CF edge IP. With limits short=10/s, medium=50/10s, long=100/min (app.module.ts:82-98), all visitors sharing a handful of CF edge IPs collapse into a few buckets → either mass false-positive 429s OR ineffective per-attacker limiting.
- client-ip.helper.ts:12-13 — getClientIp returns req.ip first → audit columns (auth/superadmin/payments/self-pay/display controllers) record CF edge IPs, crippling abuse attribution.
- paytr-ip-allowlist.guard.ts:77-82 — falls back to req.ip.

Severity correction to HIGH (not critical): real, fleet-wide, live misconfiguration, but defense-in-depth blunts the worst cases. (a) PayTR allowlist is HMAC-primary AND disabled unless PAYTR_WEBHOOK_ALLOWED_IPS is set (guard line 27, returns null/allow-all when unset) — so no auth bypass; if it WERE set it would reject all real webhooks, but that's a self-DoS the boot self-test/ops would catch, not an exploit. (b) Cloudflare itself provides edge rate-limiting/WAF in this topology, so app-throttler collapse is not the only protection. (c) This is a config/ops fix with no direct data breach or auth bypass. The concrete impact (rate-limiting useless or self-DoSing + audit IP attribution loss) is genuinely serious but below 'critical'. Recommendation (add CF real_ip config OR set TRUST_PROXY=2) is sound.
findings.evidence: main.ts:58-64 sets `trust proxy` to TRUST_PROXY or defaults to 1 (one hop); TRUST_PROXY is never set in docker-compose.prod.yml (grep finds no occurrence in compose). The deploy/README topology is Cloudflare -> host nginx -> backend (ops/nginx/README.md:3-9; scripts/deploy.sh:488 'nginx/cloudflare -> frontend -> backend'), i.e. TWO proxy hops in front of Express. nginx forwards X-Forwarded-For via $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for (hummytummy.com.conf:41) but does NOT configure set_real_ip_from <cloudflare-CIDRs> + real_ip_header CF-Connecting-IP (grep of all ops/nginx/*.conf finds none). With trust proxy=1, Express strips only the last XFF entry (nginx's connection = the Cloudflare edge IP), so req.ip becomes the Cloudflare edge IP, not the visitor. The throttler keys on exactly this value (machine-throttler.guard.ts:21 `req.ips?.length ? req.ips[0] : req.ip` and :38), and the PayTR allowlist falls back to it (paytr-ip-allowlist.guard.ts:77-82), and getClientIp prefers req.ip for audit columns (client-ip.helper.ts:12-13).
findings.why_it_matters: All app-level IP throttling collapses to a handful of buckets keyed by Cloudflare edge IPs shared across ALL tenants and ALL visitors — so one abusive client exhausts the bucket for everyone, or conversely an attacker is never individually rate-limited. Audit/security logs record Cloudflare IPs instead of the real source, crippling incident response and abuse attribution. The PayTR webhook IP allowlist, if ever relied on without HMAC, would compare against the wrong address.
findings.recommendation: In nginx, add Cloudflare's real-IP config: `set_real_ip_from <each CF CIDR>; real_ip_header CF-Connecting-IP; real_ip_recursive on;` so $remote_addr/X-Real-IP/req.ip become the true visitor. Then set TRUST_PROXY appropriately (e.g. 1 once nginx itself presents the corrected single XFF, or the exact CF+nginx hop count). Add a startup assertion/log of the resolved req.ip on a test request to prevent silent regression.

findings.title: No edge rate limiting (limit_req / limit_conn) — the only throttling is the app-level limiter that #2 has already neutered
findings.verify_reasoning:
The core technical claim is TRUE on main. grep for limit_req/limit_conn across all ops/nginx/*.conf returns zero hits (count=0), and there is no global nginx.conf or limit_req_zone/limit_conn_zone anywhere in the repo. The prod vhost hummytummy.com.conf:44-64 proxies /api/, /uploads/, /socket.io/, and / straight to single 127.0.0.1 upstreams with NO connection or request-rate ceiling. The sole rate control is the NestJS ThrottlerModule (app.module.ts:82-98: short 10/1s, medium 50/10s, long 100/60s) applied via MachineThrottlerGuard, which keys primarily on req.ips[0]||req.ip (machine-throttler.guard.ts:21,38). Finding #2's dependency also checks out: Cloudflare IS in front (ops/nginx/README.md, vhost headers confirm CF -> host nginx -> container), nginx sets X-Real-IP $remote_addr (the CF edge IP) and has NO real_ip/set_real_ip_from directive to restore the true client IP; with trust proxy defaulting to 1 (main.ts:58-63) the throttler's IP key is degraded toward CF edge IPs, so it does not cleanly isolate real clients. Notably there IS an ip-detection.util.ts that prefers CF-Connecting-IP, but the throttler guard does NOT use it. So the origin has no working per-client rate limit at either the edge-nginx OR a reliably-keyed app layer. No mitigation in config/guards/compose was missed.

Severity nuance (why high, not critical): the claim 'effectively no working first line of defense' is overstated. Cloudflare itself sits in front of the VPS and is the actual first line for volumetric floods/bot/credential-stuffing via CF's own edge rate-limiting and DDoS protection — those rules are user-configured outside this repo and not verifiable here, but the layer exists and is best-positioned to shed volumetric load. What is genuinely missing is the ORIGIN safety net (nginx limit_req/limit_conn) protecting Node event-loop/DB pool/Redis even if CF rules are loose or an attacker hits the origin IP directly (the repo's own paytr guard warns about 'direct origin IP exposure'). That is a real defense-in-depth gap and the app throttler still functions (just coarsely keyed), so it does not rise to critical. The recommendation (add limit_req_zone/limit_conn_zone keyed on real client IP after fixing the real-ip/trust-proxy issue, tighter on /api/auth + webhooks) is sound.
findings.evidence: grep of all ops/nginx/*.conf for limit_req / limit_conn returns nothing. The sole rate control is NestJS ThrottlerModule (app.module.ts:82-98, short/medium/long buckets) via MachineThrottlerGuard, which keys on req.ip — and per finding #2 that IP is the Cloudflare edge, so it does not isolate real clients. nginx forwards every request straight to a single 127.0.0.1 upstream (hummytummy.com.conf:44-64) with no connection or request-rate ceiling.
findings.why_it_matters: There is effectively no working first line of defense against credential-stuffing, scraping, or volumetric floods at the edge — the layer best positioned to shed load cheaply before it consumes Node event-loop, DB connections, and Redis. Under real multi-tenant load or a single abusive tenant, the backend absorbs the full request rate, risking event-loop saturation and DB pool exhaustion that takes down all tenants at once.
findings.recommendation: Add nginx limit_req_zone / limit_conn_zone keyed on the real client IP (after fixing #2) with per-location budgets — tighter on /api/auth and webhooks, looser on static. This complements (does not replace) the app throttler and protects the origin even if the app limiter is misconfigured.

findings.title: Prometheus (9090) and Alertmanager (9093) are published on 0.0.0.0, unauthenticated
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is TRUE on main. docker-compose.monitoring.yml:61-62 binds prometheus "9090:9090" and :103-104 binds alertmanager "9093:9093" with no IP prefix, which Docker maps to 0.0.0.0 — publicly reachable on a VPS. There is NO nginx vhost fronting them (ops/nginx/ only has the 4 app vhosts: hummytummy.com, staging, developer, help) and Prometheus v2.53.0 / Alertmanager v0.27.0 have no built-in auth, so both UIs/APIs are unauthenticated. The contrast the finder drew is real and damning: docker-compose.prod.yml:27 and docker-compose.staging.yml:18/47 deliberately bind Postgres/Redis to 127.0.0.1, so the loopback convention exists in the repo and was simply not applied to the monitoring ports. The README even calls the UIs "localhost:9090/9093" (lines 76-78) while the binding is actually 0.0.0.0 — a genuine intent-vs-config gap. The metrics exposed (http_request_duration 5xx counts/latencies, outbox_dlq_depth, payment_intents_total, webhook_delivery_total) are real infra-topology/operational disclosure, and Alertmanager's unauthenticated API does allow silence creation (suppressing pages). So info-disclosure + observability-integrity are both real.

I downgrade high -> medium for two reasons grounded in the files: (1) this is an explicitly OPT-IN, operator-bootstrapped stack that is SEPARATE from the deployed prod/staging app stacks (compose header lines 1-20, README lines 3-7/107-110) — the exposure only materializes if an operator manually runs `docker compose -f docker-compose.monitoring.yml up`, so it is not an always-on default-deployment exposure; (2) the disclosed data is RED-style infra metrics and counters, not tenant PII, credentials, or secrets (the secrets/tokens are sed-rendered into the container, not served). The fix is exactly as recommended and trivial (prefix 127.0.0.1: on both port maps, mirroring prod's Postgres binding, plus host firewall / authed nginx vhost if remote access is wanted). Real, fix-worthy misconfiguration, but conditional on operator action and limited to infra metrics — medium, not high.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.monitoring.yml:61-62 exposes prometheus '9090:9090' and :103-104 alertmanager '9093:9093', both on 0.0.0.0. Neither has auth or a reverse-proxy auth gate; there is no nginx vhost fronting them. Prometheus 2.x and Alertmanager have no built-in authentication.
findings.why_it_matters: Anyone scanning the VPS reads the full internal metrics surface (request rates/latencies, error counts, tenant-scoped counters, infra topology) and can call Alertmanager's API to create silences — suppressing the very alerts meant to page ops during an incident. It is both an information disclosure and an availability/observability-integrity risk.
findings.recommendation: Bind both to 127.0.0.1 (or an internal-only interface) and, if remote access is needed, front them with an authenticated nginx vhost (basic-auth or mTLS) plus the host firewall. Same pattern as the Postgres/Redis loopback binding already used.

findings.title: No HSTS at the edge; HSTS is partial (helmet on /api only) and absent on the SPA/static and docs hosts
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is accurate on main. Verified across all four edge vhosts (ops/nginx/{hummytummy,staging,developer,help}.com.conf): every 443 server block sets only proxy_set_header and ssl_* directives — zero add_header Strict-Transport-Security (or any security header). A repo-wide grep for Strict-Transport returns nothing in ops/, compose, or any Cloudflare config (none exists in-repo).

Helmet (backend/src/main.ts:93-111, helmet ^8.1.0) is the ONLY thing emitting HSTS. helmet 8.x default DOES enable strictTransportSecurity (max-age ~180d, includeSubDomains) and it is not disabled in this config, so HSTS IS asserted — but only on responses served by the backend, which nginx routes for /api/, /uploads/, and /socket.io/. The finder said "/api only"; in fact it covers a few backend paths, but the load-bearing point is unchanged and correct: these are JSON/asset/websocket endpoints, NOT the HTML document the browser actually navigates to. The SPA HTML is served by the frontend container nginx (frontend/nginx.conf), which adds Cache-Control/Pragma/Expires but no Strict-Transport-Security. The docs hosts (developer.* → :3200, help.* → :3201) just proxy_pass to Next with no add_header at all. So the primary browser-facing origin and the docs origins never send HSTS.

Severity assessment for real-user prod: this is a genuine gap. Without HSTS on the HTML origin, a first visit or any plaintext/typed-http navigation is exposed to SSL-strip/downgrade MITM, and the site is not preload-eligible. The port-80 blocks do 301→https, which helps but is itself strippable (the redirect itself can be MITM'd on first contact). The one mitigating reality the finder acknowledges: prod almost certainly sits behind Cloudflare, which commonly injects HSTS at the edge — but that is unverifiable from the repo and the vhost comments warn the file is a reconstruction (prod 'nginx -T' is the source of truth), so coverage genuinely cannot be asserted from code. Given it's a missing-defense-in-depth hardening header (not an active exploit, partially covered for API, and likely-but-unconfirmed Cloudflare backstop), high is slightly generous; medium better reflects a missing-but-not-actively-exploited transport hardening header on a TLS-terminated, http-redirecting origin. The fix (add_header ... always in each 443 block + frontend nginx) is exactly right.
findings.evidence: grep for Strict-Transport / add_header across all ops/nginx/*.conf returns nothing — the edge vhosts add no headers at all (hummytummy.com.conf:39-42 only sets proxy_set_header). Helmet's default HSTS is applied only to backend responses (main.ts:93-111), i.e. /api/*; the SPA and static assets are served by the frontend container nginx, which adds Cache-Control headers but no Strict-Transport-Security (frontend/nginx.conf:32-58). The developer/help docs hosts proxy to Next with no HSTS either.
findings.why_it_matters: The browser-facing origin (the SPA users actually load) never receives HSTS, so a first-visit or any plaintext navigation is vulnerable to SSL-strip / downgrade MITM, and there is no preload eligibility. Relying on Cloudflare to inject HSTS is fragile and invisible in this repo; the origin should assert it. Inconsistent HSTS (only on JSON API responses) gives a false sense of coverage.
findings.recommendation: Add `add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload" always;` in the 443 server block of each edge vhost (and/or the frontend container nginx). Confirm Cloudflare is not stripping/duplicating it. Consider an `always` security-header bundle (X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy) at the edge.

findings.title: No explicit proxy timeouts, request buffering control, upstream keepalive, or health/failover — defaults will misbehave under load and on uploads
findings.evidence: grep of ops/nginx/*.conf for proxy_read_timeout / proxy_send_timeout / proxy_connect_timeout / keepalive / proxy_request_buffering / upstream returns nothing. Every location uses a bare `proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:<port>` to a single hardcoded address (hummytummy.com.conf:44-64) — there is no `upstream{}` block, so no health checks, no failover, and a new TCP connection per upstream request (no keepalive pool). nginx defaults to proxy_read_timeout 60s and full request buffering.
findings.why_it_matters: Multi-replica scale-out has no edge-level upstream pool/failover (a dead replica is not routed around). Lack of keepalive adds connection churn and latency under load. Default 60s proxy_read_timeout can cut off idle long-poll/websocket fallbacks and any slow report query; default full request buffering forces large uploads to spool to disk before reaching the backend, adding latency and disk pressure. None of this is tuned for the long-request (reports/uploads) and real-time (socket.io) traffic the app actually serves.
findings.recommendation: Introduce an `upstream` block per service (enabling keepalive and, with nginx-plus or passive checks, failover), set explicit proxy_connect/read/send timeouts (generous on /socket.io/ and report endpoints, tight elsewhere), and consider proxy_request_buffering off for /api/upload to stream large bodies. This becomes necessary the moment a second backend replica exists.

findings.title: No gzip/brotli at the edge or in the frontend container — compression depends entirely on Cloudflare
findings.evidence: grep for gzip/brotli across ops/nginx/*.conf and frontend/nginx.conf returns nothing (frontend/nginx.conf:1-59 sets caching but no gzip). The prod vhost header even notes gzip is among the things not captured (hummytummy.com.conf:10).
findings.why_it_matters: If Cloudflare is ever bypassed (direct-origin access per #1, or Cloudflare in DNS-only/grey-cloud mode), JSON API payloads and JS/CSS are served uncompressed, inflating bandwidth and TTFB for real users on mobile/slow links. Edge compression is cheap insurance independent of the CDN.
findings.recommendation: Enable gzip (and brotli if available) for text/JSON/JS/CSS in the edge nginx and/or frontend container, with a sane min length and type list. Keep it even if Cloudflare normally compresses, for defense in depth.

findings.title: client_max_body_size (25m) is below the backend's 50MB multi-file upload ceiling — batch uploads can 413 at the edge
findings.evidence: All edge vhosts set client_max_body_size 25m (hummytummy.com.conf:37, staging:42, developer:39, help:39). The backend's multi-file upload accepts up to 10 files at 5MB each = ~50MB (upload.controller.ts:142 `limits: { fileSize: 5*1024*1024, files: 10 }`), while single-file endpoints are 5MB (upload.controller.ts:46,95). Webhook bodies up to 2mb are also configured in the app (main.ts:70-78).
findings.why_it_matters: A user uploading a full batch of product images (or several near-5MB files) gets an opaque nginx 413 before the request ever reaches the app, which can surface as a confusing failure rather than the app's own validation message. The edge ceiling and app ceiling are not reconciled.
findings.recommendation: Either raise client_max_body_size on the upload path to match the real 10x5MB ceiling (e.g. a /api/upload-scoped 55m) or lower the backend's per-request batch ceiling to fit 25m, and align the two so the limit is intentional and the error is the app's clear message.

findings.title: Prod vhost is self-described as 'reconstructed' and never verified against the live server — routing source-of-truth is ambiguous
findings.evidence: ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:4-12 warns the file is 'RECONSTRUCTED to mirror the observed prod behaviour' and 'Do NOT apply this to prod blind'; README.md:18 calls it 'Reconstructed — capture the live prod vhost and reconcile before treating as authoritative'. Only the staging file is marked 'Verified live'. Deploys do not auto-apply vhosts (README.md:47-50), so the live prod edge config exists only on the server and may already diverge from this repo (extra headers, gzip, timeouts unknown).
findings.why_it_matters: The edge config — the place TLS, security headers, rate limits, and routing actually live — is not under verified version control for production. A host rebuild, accidental reload, or incident-time change has no trustworthy source to restore from, and reviewers (including this audit) cannot be certain whether prod already has or lacks the protections discussed above.
findings.recommendation: Run the documented `nginx -T` capture (README.md:44) once, commit the verbatim prod vhost as the authoritative file, and then keep it in sync via apply.sh. This both removes the ambiguity and lets the hardening fixes (HSTS, real-IP, rate limits, timeouts) be reviewed and applied from the repo.

summary: The database backup *mechanism* is genuinely good — scripts/backup-database.sh and the backup_database() step in scripts/deploy.sh both produce a streamed pg_dump, gzip-verify it, sanity-check the CREATE-statement count, reject sub-1KB dumps, and enforce a 14-day (prod) / 3-day (staging) retention. But the surrounding DR posture is not production-ready for paying tenants. Backups are taken ONLY at deploy time (no cron/scheduled/CI-schedule backup exists anywhere), so RPO is unbounded between releases; they are written to the SAME single VPS disk that runs Postgres (no offsite copy), are unencrypted at rest, real prod dumps containing customer PII are committed to the now-public git repo, restore has never been tested and is absent from the modern prod path, and the uploads_storage + invoice_storage volumes (fiscal receipts/invoices, product images) are never backed up at all. A host loss = total, unrecoverable loss of everything created since the last deploy plus all uploaded files; a bad data-corrupting migration is not auto-recoverable because rollback only restores image SHAs, not the DB.
strengths: Backup integrity is verified, not assumed: scripts/backup-database.sh:89-121 checks PIPESTATUS for pg_dump (not just gzip), runs `gzip -t`, rejects sub-1KB files, and requires >=5 CREATE statements before accepting a dump — this catches the classic 'connection died mid-dump but gzip wrote a happy empty file' failure. Same logic mirrored in scripts/deploy.sh:234-257.

A DB backup is forced BEFORE every prod migration: run_deploy() runs step '2/10 Backup database' (scripts/deploy.sh:647) ahead of the migration doctor (651) and migrate step (652), and backup_database() `return 1` aborts the whole deploy on failure (set -euo pipefail + ERR trap at :696) — so you never migrate prod without a fresh verified snapshot on disk.

The migration doctor (scripts/db-migration-doctor.sh:261-287) has a real destructive-operation detector (DROP/TRUNCATE/UPDATE/DELETE/ALTER...DROP/RENAME/TYPE/CHECK) that hard-fails (exit 1) a non-idempotent failed-migration recovery rather than blindly re-running it — solid guard against compounding data loss during recovery.

Durable domain state is in Postgres, not Redis: the at-least-once event buffer is the Postgres OutboxEvent table (backend/prisma/schema.prisma:4092,4137), so the DB dump captures it. Redis is used as cache only, which correctly limits what must be backed up.

Postgres and Redis use named docker volumes (postgres_data, redis_data at docker-compose.prod.yml:257-261) with `restart: always`, so data survives ordinary container recreation/restart — the image is major-pinned (postgres:15-alpine) specifically to avoid a minor-bump volume-incompat recreate (documented at docker-compose.prod.yml:10-15).

findings.title: No scheduled/automated backup — DB is only dumped at deploy time, so RPO is unbounded between releases
findings.verify_reasoning: Every technical claim checks out on main. (1) scripts/backup-database.sh:15-18 explicitly lists off-site upload, WAL/PITR streaming, and restore-drill as un-done TODOs. (2) The only automatic backup is deploy-time: scripts/deploy.sh has an INLINE backup_database() (def at line 215, invoked at line 647 as step "2/10 Backup database" inside run_deploy) — it doesn't even call the standalone script. The standalone scripts/backup-database.sh is only reachable as a MANUAL subcommand via scripts/test-deploy.sh:33 (staging) and scripts/deploy-production.sh:35 (prod). (3) No scheduled mechanism anywhere: grep found no `schedule:`/`cron` in any of the 9 .github/workflows files; the only crontab-shaped `0 2 * * *` is a comment in docker-compose.prod.yml:82 about the app's NestJS @Cron TZ, not a backup; the only systemd unit in the tree is edge-device-cpp/docker/kds-edge-device.service (edge hardware, not DB backup). db-baseline.yml is workflow_dispatch manual-only and is migration baselining, not a backup. (4) No backup-freshness alerting: ops/monitoring/alert.rules.yml has zero backup/pg_dump rules. (5) The committed backups/database/ listing corroborates the deploy-clustered, irregular cadence (several dumps on 2026-04-07/08/09 deploy days, then a multi-day gap, then test_ dumps on 2026-04-23). The finder missed no mitigation; I checked ConfigModule-adjacent ops dirs, compose, nginx, monitoring, and all workflows. Severity is justified for this context: this is a money-handling, fiscally-regulated multi-tenant POS. Between releases (a quiet weekend), a full day+ of paid-tenant orders/payments/fiscal receipts/stock movements exists with no recovery point; a disk/Postgres failure at that moment is permanent, unbounded data loss plus a fiscal-compliance gap. The deploy-time dump is a fine pre-migration safety net but provides no RPO guarantee for steady-state. One small nuance (not severity-changing): this is an absence-of-control / DR-readiness gap rather than an exploitable code defect, and it depends on an actual infra-failure event to cause harm — but for a fiscally-regulated POS the expected-loss magnitude keeps it at the top tier. I'd rate it critical rather than the absolute highest "blocker" bucket, since "blocker" usually connotes something actively breaking/exploitable in the running product today; the recommendation (out-of-band cron/systemd timer every 1-6h + daily, plus an Alertmanager rule on stale-backup) is exactly right.
findings.evidence: The ONLY callers of backup_database / backup-database.sh are deploy-time: scripts/deploy.sh:647 (step '2/10 Backup database', inside run_deploy). Grep for any schedule across .github/workflows, ops/, scripts/, compose files finds NO `schedule:` cron workflow and no host crontab/systemd timer (the only `0 2 * * *` hit is the app's billing @Cron, docker-compose.prod.yml:82). scripts/backup-database.sh:15-18 explicitly lists offsite/WAL/restore-drill as un-done TODOs. The committed backups in backups/database/ jump from 2026-04-09 straight to test dumps on 2026-04-23 — i.e. backups happen sporadically, only when someone deploys.
findings.why_it_matters: On a quiet weekend with no release, a full day+ of real paying-tenant orders, payments, fiscal receipts and stock movements can exist with NO backup. If Postgres/the disk fails at that point, every transaction since the last deploy is permanently lost. For a money-handling, fiscally-regulated restaurant POS this is data-loss and a compliance problem, not just an inconvenience.
findings.recommendation: Add an out-of-band scheduled backup independent of deploys: a host cron/systemd timer (or a tiny sidecar) running scripts/backup-database.sh prod every 1-6h, plus a daily run. Alert (Alertmanager/email) if no successful backup in the last N hours. Keep the deploy-time backup as the pre-migration safety net.

findings.title: Backups live only on the same single VPS disk as the database — no offsite copy; a host loss loses the DB and every backup together
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is true on main. backup-database.sh:57-61 writes backups to BACKUP_DIR="$PROJECT_ROOT/backups/database" — a plain host directory under the single checkout (/root/kds), not even a separate Docker volume, let alone a separate host. deploy.sh:215-219 does the same (backup_dir="$PROJECT_ROOT/backups/database"). DEPLOYMENT.md:6-23 confirms BOTH staging and prod run as Docker Compose projects on ONE VPS (38.242.233.166) out of one checkout, with the Postgres data also living in local Docker volumes on that same box. A repo-wide grep for any offsite mechanism (rclone|aws s3|s3 cp|backblaze|gsutil|restic|borg|wal-g|pgbackrest|offsite) returns ONLY the un-done TODO at backup-database.sh:16 ("rclone/aws s3 cp off-site upload") and docs/reviews/upload.md:32 ("Storage is local disk only"). No cron, no CI step, no compose volume to remote storage, no ConfigModule/env-driven upload anywhere. So I found no missed mitigation: the primary DB volume and every backup genuinely share one disk on one machine. Cloudflare fronts the edge but provides zero DB DR.

Severity: the finder's reasoning holds for a real-user production context — this is the canonical DR single-point-of-failure. On VPS disk failure, host compromise/ransomware, or provider instance termination, the database AND all 14 days of backups are lost together; RTO is effectively infinite and RPO is total (all tenant data). For a paid multi-tenant SaaS holding financial/order/customer data, irrecoverable total data loss is the worst-case outcome, which justifies blocker. The only minor nuance (not a downgrade): the backups being on the same disk is somewhat redundant with the DB volume itself being on that disk — even a perfect offsite design needs the offsite copy to be the authoritative restore source — but that is exactly the recommendation and doesn't reduce impact. I keep severity at blocker.
findings.evidence: Backups are written to `$PROJECT_ROOT/backups/database` on the box (scripts/deploy.sh:216, scripts/backup-database.sh:57). docs/DEPLOYMENT.md:8-10 confirms staging+prod run on ONE VPS (38.242.233.166). There is no rclone/aws s3/object-storage upload anywhere (grep finds only the un-done TODO at backup-database.sh:16 and docs/reviews/upload.md:32 'Storage is local disk only'). So the primary DB volume and all its backups share one disk on one machine.
findings.why_it_matters: The entire DR story collapses on the single most likely catastrophic event: VPS disk failure, host compromise, ransomware, or the provider terminating the instance. Backups that die with the server they protect are not backups. RTO is effectively infinite (nothing to restore from) and RPO is total.
findings.recommendation: Push every verified backup offsite immediately after creation (Backblaze B2 / S3 / another provider's object storage) via rclone or aws s3 cp, with its own retention/lifecycle. This is the single highest-value DR fix. Treat the offsite copy as authoritative.

findings.title: Real production database dumps containing customer PII are committed to git in a now-public repo
findings.verify_reasoning: Every technical claim checks out against the live main branch. `git ls-files backups/` returns exactly 20 tracked `.sql.gz` files under backups/database/ (plus scripts/backup-database.sh). The cited file backups/database/backup_20260409_142738.sql.gz decompresses to a full prod dump: 85 `COPY public.*` blocks including customers, users, api_keys, audit_logs, payments, invoices, subscriptions, super_admins. It contains 133 email matches including the three exact addresses the finder quoted (abdukahar1212@gmail.com, ahmet@sultanahmet-sofra.com, beytullahmurat7@gmail.com), and the users table dumps the `password` column with 16 distinct bcrypt hashes plus resetToken/googleId/appleId — even more sensitive than the finder claimed. These are in committed history (cited file last in commit 673ebf8a; backups first introduced around ce415265). NO mitigation exists: `git check-ignore` reports NOT IGNORED; .gitignore only covers .env* — the single "backup" hit there is an unrelated comment about .env.default.backup. The remote is github.com/mtarikucar/kds, which MEMORY confirms was made PUBLIC 2026-06-23. Root cause confirmed in scripts/backup-database.sh:57 (BACKUP_DIR="$PROJECT_ROOT/backups/database" writes dumps inside the repo tree). This is a genuine GDPR/KVKK-class breach distinct from the leaked .env-secrets runbook, and arguably higher data-subject impact (live PII + password hashes). Blocker severity is fully justified for a real-user production context. The only nuance: the recommendation should explicitly note that gitignore + rm --cached alone is insufficient — the data persists in public git history and (as the finder did say) must be included in the planned git filter-repo purge; the leak is already public so rotation of exposed api keys/credentials and breach-notification considerations also apply.
findings.evidence: git ls-files backups/ returns 20 tracked `.sql.gz` files, e.g. backups/database/backup_20260409_142738.sql.gz (768KB, committed in 673ebf8a). Decompressing it shows a full prod dump: 85 COPY/INSERT blocks including COPY public.customers, public.users, public.api_keys, public.audit_logs, and real email addresses (abdukahar1212@gmail.com, ahmet@sultanahmet-sofra.com, beytullahmurat7@gmail.com, ...). MEMORY notes the repo mtarikucar/kds was made PUBLIC on 2026-06-23.
findings.why_it_matters: This leaks real tenant and customer PII (and the api_keys table) to anyone who clones the repo — a GDPR/KVKK-class data breach independent of the separately-tracked leaked-secrets issue. Even after deletion the data stays in git history forever unless purged.
findings.recommendation: Stop tracking backups: add `backups/` to .gitignore (currently only env files are ignored), `git rm --cached` the dumps, and include this directory in the planned `git filter-repo` history purge alongside the leaked .env files. Never write backups inside the repo working tree — point BACKUP_DIR at a path outside /root/kds.

findings.title: User uploads and fiscal invoice/receipt files are NEVER backed up — only Postgres is
findings.verify_reasoning:
The core technical claim is TRUE: the deploy/backup pipeline backs up ONLY Postgres. scripts/deploy.sh:215-262 (backup_database) and the dedicated scripts/backup-database.sh both do nothing but pg_dump|gzip with retention; deploy.sh:647 runs only "Backup database". Neither touches the two host volumes invoice_storage:/app/storage and uploads_storage:/app/uploads (docker-compose.prod.yml:129-131). backup-database.sh:15-18 even has an explicit TODO acknowledging "rclone/aws s3 cp off-site" and PITR are out of scope, and docs/reviews/upload.md:32 confirms "S3/object-storage adapter — none exists. Storage is local disk only." So there is genuinely no off-host backup of these volumes — no mitigation exists anywhere (no rsync/s3/tar in scripts/, .github/, compose, or env config). That part is confirmed.

However the severity is OVERSTATED on its most alarming premise. The finder frames /app/storage as holding "generated invoices/receipts (fiscal/legal documents)" whose loss creates "a fiscal-records gap." That is wrong on two counts:

1) The ONLY writer to /app/storage is InvoicePdfService (invoice-pdf.service.ts:13 → process.cwd()/storage/invoices), and these are SaaS *subscription-billing* invoice PDFs (HummyTummy billing the tenant), not the restaurant's customer fiscal receipts. The real fiscal documents (ÖKC fiş / e-Fatura) are NOT written to disk at all — a grep for fiscal/efatura/okc file writes returns nothing; those go out over external accounting/ÖKC rails. So there is no on-disk "fiscal-records gap."

2) Even those subscription invoice PDFs are fully REGENERABLE from DB rows, which ARE backed up. invoice-pdf.service.ts:20-23 comments "we can regenerate URLs freely"; generateInvoicePdf rebuilds the entire PDF via pdfkit purely from the invoice row + relations; and crucially invoice.controller.ts:63-65 lazily re-generates the file on download if invoicePdfExists() is false. So losing the entire invoice_storage volume causes ZERO permanent data loss — every PDF is reconstructed on next access from the (backed-up) database. The invoice_storage volume is effectively a regenerable cache.

The legitimately non-regenerable data is in uploads_storage:/app/uploads — product/menu images and tenant logos (upload.service.ts, served unauthenticated via main.ts useStaticAssets). The original uploaded bytes are not stored anywhere else, so a host/disk loss permanently destroys them and leaves ProductImage rows (schema.prisma:317-332) and logo URLs pointing at 404s. That IS real, unrecoverable data loss — but it is re-uploadable branding/menu imagery, an operational annoyance and cosmetic degradation, not a fiscal/legal or money-correctness loss. There is no DB-row-to-file integrity break that breaks orders or billing.

Net: the gap is real (uploads are genuinely never backed up off-host) but the impact is medium, not critical — the catastrophic "fiscal documents lost forever" scenario is refuted by lazy regeneration + the absence of any on-disk fiscal records. Recommendation should narrow to: back up uploads_storage off-host (invoice_storage is regenerable and lower priority), or migrate uploads to object storage.
findings.evidence: Only Postgres is dumped (scripts/deploy.sh:234). The backend persists files to two host volumes — invoice_storage:/app/storage and uploads_storage:/app/uploads (docker-compose.prod.yml:129-131) — and writes to local disk via multer/diskStorage (backend/src/modules/upload/upload.service.ts, main.ts useStaticAssets('uploads')). docs/reviews/upload.md:32 confirms 'S3 / object-storage adapter — none exists. Storage is local disk only.' Nothing copies these volumes anywhere.
findings.why_it_matters: Product/menu images and, more seriously, generated invoices/receipts (fiscal/legal documents in /app/storage) exist ONLY on the single VPS disk. A disk/host loss destroys them with no recovery, even if the DB itself were restored from an offsite copy — leaving DB rows that reference files that no longer exist, and a fiscal-records gap.
findings.recommendation: Include /app/storage and /app/uploads in the backup routine (tar + offsite, or rsync to object storage), or migrate uploads/invoices to S3-compatible object storage with its own durability/versioning. At minimum back up the invoice_storage volume on the same schedule as the DB.

findings.title: Restore is never tested and is absent from the modern prod deploy/rollback path; rollback restores images, not data
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every factual claim in the finding is true on main.

1) scripts/deploy.sh run_rollback() (lines 670-675) calls ONLY preflight + restore_image_ids() — it restores container image SHAs (restore_image_ids at line 580 just re-tags previous image digests), never the database. The ERR-trap on_failure() (line 677-685) likewise only calls restore_image_ids(). There is NO `restore` action: configure_env (lines 86-96) accepts exactly staging|prod|staging-rollback|prod-rollback; ACTION is only ever 'deploy' or 'rollback' (line 55), so there is no code path that runs a DB restore.

2) The CI prod path confirms this is the live path: release-deploy.yml line 485 runs `./scripts/deploy.sh prod ${VERSION}` for deploy and line 549 runs `./scripts/deploy.sh prod-rollback` for rollback — both routing through the modern script, never the legacy root deploy.sh.

3) The only DB-restore logic is in the legacy root /home/tarik/Projects/kds/deploy.sh rollback() (lines 223-236): an interactive `read -p ... yes/no` then `cat "$LATEST_BACKUP" | docker-compose exec -T postgres psql`. It is interactive (cannot run unattended in CI), references *.sql (not the .sql.gz the modern backup actually produces — a real mismatch that would break even a manual attempt), and is not invoked by any CI path. So it's effectively dead for the modern pipeline.

4) backup-database.sh line 18 explicitly lists 'monthly restore drill runbook' as an undone TODO (alongside off-site upload and WAL/PITR, both also absent). The modern backup is genuinely verified on the WRITE side (gzip -t, size floor, CREATE-statement count, lines 97-123) — but verifying a dump is well-formed is NOT the same as verifying it restores cleanly (PostGIS extension, role/ownership, password). No restore is ever exercised.

5) docs/DEPLOYMENT.md exists but a grep for restore/disaster/recover/RPO/RTO/drill/backup returns ZERO hits — no DR section, no documented recovery procedure, no stated RPO/RTO.

I searched the whole repo for any mitigation: no *restore* script exists anywhere outside node_modules. So there is no missed mitigation.

Severity assessment for a real-user production context: this is a public-facing, multi-tenant money/POS system (orders, payments, invoices). Backups are taken and write-verified, so this is not "no backups" (which would be a flat blocker). But the restore path is untested, undocumented, absent from the automated rollback, AND the one manual fallback has a format mismatch (.sql vs .sql.gz) that means the first real restore attempt during an incident would fail on the obvious step and require improvisation under pressure. An untested restore is a hypothesis, not a recovery plan; restores commonly fail on PostGIS-extension/role/ownership specifics this setup never exercises. That is a serious operational gap. I'd put it at HIGH rather than CRITICAL: backups do exist and are integrity-verified, and the data isn't actively being lost or corrupted today, so it's a latent recovery-readiness risk rather than an active production-breaking defect. The finder's 'critical' is defensible but slightly overstated given the working, verified backup half; HIGH is the more precise rating. The recommendation (restore runbook in DEPLOYMENT.md with exact gunzip|psql + role/db/PostGIS steps, a `restore` action in scripts/deploy.sh, a scheduled drill into a throwaway DB asserting row counts, and explicit RPO/RTO) is sound and directly closes the gap.
findings.evidence: scripts/deploy.sh's run_rollback() (line 670-675) only calls restore_image_ids() — it restores container image SHAs, never the DB. There is no restore command in the modern prod script. The only restore logic is in the legacy root deploy.sh:223-233 (interactive `read -p ... yes/no` then `cat backup | psql`), which is not used by the CI prod path (release-deploy.yml invokes scripts/deploy.sh). backup-database.sh:18 lists 'monthly restore drill runbook' as an un-done TODO; docs/DEPLOYMENT.md has no DR/restore section (grep for backup/restore/disaster/RPO/RTO returns nothing).
findings.why_it_matters: A backup you have never restored is a hypothesis, not a recovery plan. During a real incident (corruption, bad migration, host rebuild) the team would be improvising restore + decompress + psql + role-password + PostGIS-extension steps for the first time on a public-facing money system, with no runbook and no measured RTO. Untested restores routinely fail (extension/role/ownership mismatches).
findings.recommendation: Write a restore runbook in docs/DEPLOYMENT.md (exact commands: gunzip | docker exec psql, recreate role/db, ensure PostGIS), add a `restore` action to scripts/deploy.sh, and run a scheduled restore drill into a throwaway DB that asserts row counts. State target RPO/RTO explicitly.

findings.title: No PITR / WAL archiving — recovery granularity is whole-dump only, and a bad data-mutating migration cannot be rolled back to a point in time
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every technical claim is true on main, and no mitigation exists anywhere.

1) No PITR/WAL config: docker-compose.prod.yml:7-36 runs stock postgres:15-alpine with only POSTGRES_DB/USER/PASSWORD/TZ env and a postgres_data volume. There is no command override, no mounted postgresql.conf, no archive_command, no wal_level, no archive volume. A repo-wide grep for archive_command/wal_level/pgbackrest/wal-g/pg_basebackup/restore_command/PITR across compose, scripts, conf, and docs returns ZERO real hits — the only matches are the TODO comment itself (backup-database.sh:17) and an unrelated "point-in-time" phrase in QUALITY_AUDIT.md. So no WAL archiving or shipping exists.

2) Recovery is whole-dump only: both backup-database.sh and deploy.sh's backup_database() (deploy.sh:215-244) do a logical pg_dump | gzip. That is a coarse snapshot, not continuous archiving; you cannot replay to an arbitrary point between dumps. backup-database.sh:17 explicitly lists "WAL streaming for PITR" as a TODO, and line 16 also flags off-site upload as a TODO (so dumps may be local-only — an additional DR gap the finder didn't even claim).

3) Deploy migrates in place, rolls back images only: run_deploy (deploy.sh:644-668) runs prisma migrate deploy against prod (steps 6/7, lines 651-652, exec'ing `prisma migrate deploy` at 387/461). run_rollback (670-675) and on_failure (677-685) call only restore_image_ids — DB is never restored. The script itself prints the smoking gun at lines 623-625: "image state is at previous SHA, DB state is forward... If a migration applied in this failed deploy is incompatible... manual DB intervention is required." So a data-mutating migration that corrupts/deletes rows has no automated point-in-time recovery; the operator's only anchor is the pre-deploy dump (step 2), losing all writes since.

Severity: 'high' is justified for a real-user production multi-tenant SaaS handling orders/payments/invoices. RPO is bounded only by dump cadence — and critically, dumps appear to run ONLY at deploy time (backup_database is a deploy step; I found no cron/systemd timer scheduling backup-database.sh independently). Between deploys (which can be days apart), there is effectively NO recovery point at all for a data-loss event, and a subtle data-corrupting migration forces the corruption-vs-total-rollback dilemma the finder describes. Not refuted, not overstated. I would not raise to critical because no active data loss has occurred and a (coarse) backup path does exist and is integrity-verified; this is a latent DR capability gap, the textbook definition of high. The finder's recommendation (pgBackRest/wal-g) is the correct standard fix.
findings.evidence: Postgres runs the stock postgres:15-alpine with no archive_command / wal_level=replica configuration (docker-compose.prod.yml:7-36) and no WAL shipping; backup-database.sh:17 lists 'WAL streaming for PITR' as a TODO. Combined with deploy-only dumps, the only recovery points are the sporadic full dumps. The deploy migrates prod in-place (scripts/deploy.sh:652) and on failure rolls back ONLY images (run_rollback:670-675), so a migration that silently corrupts/deletes data leaves you to restore the whole pre-deploy dump and lose everything written after it.
findings.why_it_matters: Best achievable RPO is 'since the last deploy dump' (potentially many hours/days). A subtle data bug shipped in a release and noticed an hour later forces a choice between living with the corruption or rolling the entire DB back to the deploy snapshot and dropping all legitimate traffic in between.
findings.recommendation: Enable WAL archiving to offsite storage (pgBackRest or wal-g against the same object store) to get continuous PITR with minutes-level RPO. This also makes the pre-migration dump a true point-in-time anchor.

findings.title: Backups are stored unencrypted at rest
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is factually accurate on main. Both backup code paths produce unencrypted archives:

1. scripts/backup-database.sh:86-87 — `docker exec ... pg_dump -U ... -d ... | gzip -c > "$BACKUP_FILE"`. gzip is compression, not encryption; the .sql.gz trivially decompresses to full plaintext SQL.
2. scripts/deploy.sh:234-235 — identical `pg_dump ... | gzip -c > "$backup_file"` in backup_database().

I searched the entire scripts/ tree, docker-compose files, and TS for any gpg/age/openssl enc/sops/cipher/s3/rclone step — none exist in the backup path. The only "encryption" hit (docker-compose.prod.yml:116) is an unrelated app-level at-rest key for integration credentials, not backups. There is exactly one backup script plus the inline deploy function; no other mitigation.

No bucket-side encryption fallback exists either: offsite upload is an explicit unimplemented TODO (backup-database.sh:16, "rclone/aws s3 cp off-site upload"), so the finder's "will be uploaded as-is once that's added" caveat is correct and the future risk is real, but today the exposure is local-disk-only.

Severity assessment for real production: a pg_dump of this DB contains the full multi-tenant dataset — users, password hashes, API keys, payment/fiscal metadata, customer PII. An unencrypted backup is exactly as sensitive as the live DB and survives in BACKUP_DIR for 14 days (prod retention). Defense-in-depth-wise this is a genuine gap. However, two factors keep it at high rather than critical/blocker: (a) the backups currently never leave the DB host (no offsite yet), so an attacker already needs host/container access — at which point they could also just dump the live DB themselves; the backup adds a 14-day historical window and a copy-able artifact but isn't a new remote attack surface; (b) this is standard backup-hygiene best practice that most teams defer. The memory note that "in-repo dumps already became a PII exposure" reinforces this is a real, demonstrated risk class for this project. high is justified — real, sensitive, and the right priority once offsite upload lands (which would escalate it). Not overstated, not critical.
findings.evidence: Backups are plain `gzip -c` of pg_dump output (scripts/backup-database.sh:86-87, scripts/deploy.sh:234-235) — no GPG/age/openssl encryption step. They sit world-readable-ish on disk and (per the offsite finding) will be uploaded to object storage as-is once that's added.
findings.why_it_matters: A pg_dump contains the entire tenant dataset — customers, users, password hashes, API keys, payment metadata, fiscal records. An unencrypted backup is as sensitive as the live DB; if the box, a backup bucket, or a copied archive is compromised, the whole dataset leaks. This is exactly how the in-repo dumps already became a PII exposure.
findings.recommendation: Encrypt each backup before it leaves the DB host (age/GPG with a key held outside the server, or server-side+client-side encryption on the bucket). Verify the integrity check still runs on the pre-encryption stream.

findings.title: Additive-only migration policy is NOT enforced at deploy time — a destructive migration would run on prod after the backup but with no PITR safety net
