findings.evidence: scripts/db-migration-doctor.sh:293-308 explicitly states the additive-only policy 'is enforced at PR-review time ... NOT here at deploy time' and that the destructive-op scan only guards the *failed-migration recovery* path; deploys 'must accept whatever is in prisma/migrations as the source of truth.' The promised migration-lint workflow is a TODO (db-migration-doctor.sh:305-308) and does not exist (no migrat/lint file under .github/workflows). So `prisma migrate deploy` (scripts/deploy.sh:652) will happily apply a DROP/RENAME/TYPE-change that reached main.
findings.why_it_matters: The pre-deploy dump protects you only if someone realizes the migration was destructive and triggers a full restore (losing post-deploy data, per the PITR finding). Without a CI gate, an accidental destructive migration relies entirely on human review to not silently delete a column/table of real tenant data.
findings.recommendation: Add the promised migration-lint.yml PR workflow that diffs new backend/prisma/migrations against origin/main and fails on DROP/RENAME/ALTER COLUMN TYPE unless explicitly annotated as reviewed — closing the gap between the documented policy and what deploy actually enforces.

findings.title: Redis has no persistence (no AOF, default save) — acceptable today but a latent assumption to keep verified
findings.evidence: redis is started with `redis-server --requirepass ...` and nothing else (docker-compose.prod.yml:47) — no `--appendonly yes`, so on container loss Redis data is whatever the default RDB snapshot last captured (and is never included in backups). This is fine ONLY because durable domain state lives in Postgres (OutboxEvent table, schema.prisma:4092-4137) and Redis is cache.
findings.why_it_matters: If any future feature starts using Redis for durable state (a BullMQ job queue, rate-limit counters that gate billing, single-use tokens), that state would be silently lost on a Redis restart and is not in any backup — a data-loss footgun that today is benign.
findings.recommendation: Document the invariant 'Redis is cache-only, no durable data' and add a guard/review check; if durable Redis usage is ever introduced, enable AOF and include redis_data in the backup set.

summary: The hottest read paths show real care in query shaping — KDS (take:200, narrow selects, branch-scoped), customer-order history (take:50), reports (date-bounded, `select`-only 2 fields, count-weighted, backed by `(tenantId,createdAt)`/`(tenantId,status,paidAt)` composite indexes), and QR generation is capped at 500 tables. However the SERVING-layer capacity story is weak for real paying tenants at scale: the public QR menu — the single highest-volume endpoint (every diner, every table) — runs ~5 uncached DB queries with a 6-level-deep nested include on every hit, has NO application/Redis/HTTP cache and NO CDN, and nginx has NO gzip/brotli and NO proxy_cache. All menu product images are streamed through the Node app process with no Cache-Control/CDN. There is no response compression registered in the NestJS app either, so every menu/report JSON payload ships uncompressed. Production runs a SINGLE backend container with no resource limits and no `connection_limit` on the Prisma URL, and the per-endpoint throttle limits intended to protect the public ordering surface are silently inert (they reference a `default` throttler that was never registered). Most of these are config/serving-tier fixes (small effort, large impact) rather than query rewrites.
strengths: KDS kitchen query is well-bounded and scoped: take:200, compound (tenantId,branchId) filter, narrow product/table/user selects, ordered by createdAt — see kds.service.ts:105-142, with an explicit comment about capping nested-product JSON on reconnect.

Reports avoid the classic over-fetch trap: getSalesSummary uses prisma.aggregate + payment.groupBy and only findMany's `select: {createdAt, finalAmount}` over a date-bounded window (reports.service.ts:168-182); top-products/payments use groupBy; top-customers/staff are take-limited (take:10, take:20).

Order/Payment/OrderItem schema carry the right composite indexes for hot filters: Order has (tenantId,branchId,createdAt), (tenantId,status), (tenantId,createdAt), (tenantId,tableId,status), (tenantId,status,paidAt); Payment has (tenantId,createdAt),(tenantId,status) — schema.prisma:769-787, 900-905.

QR PNG generation is explicitly capped (MAX_TABLES_PER_REQUEST=500) with a take:500+1 over-fetch guard and a 400 on overflow — qr.service.ts:16,116-125; acknowledges the ~500ms/PNG CPU cost in a comment.
Customer order-history and self-pay queries are take-bounded (customer-orders.service.ts:354 take:50) rather than unbounded.
Socket.IO uses the Redis adapter for correct multi-replica broadcast (main.ts:220-228), and bodyParser limits are path-scoped (100KB generic, 2MB webhooks) to bound payload-size DoS (main.ts:70-87).
Body size and CSP/helmet are configured; uploads are served via express.static so ETag/range/Content-Type behavior is preserved (main.ts:133-139).

findings.title: Public QR menu is fully uncached at every layer (app + nginx + CDN) — the single highest-volume endpoint re-runs ~5 queries with a 6-level nested include on every diner hit
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical core of the finding is TRUE on current main. getPublicMenu (the actual file is backend/src/modules/menu/services/menu-query.service.ts:19-204, NOT the cited menu-query.service.ts:19-204 — wrong relative path, but same content) issues exactly the described sequence of uncached queries per call: tenant.findFirst (22-35), qrMenuSettings.findFirst (46-48), optional table.findFirst (53-55), category.findMany with a 6-level nested include products→productImages→image and products→modifierGroups→group→modifiers (58-130), then posSettingsService.findByTenant (160) which itself runs a findFirst (and can opportunistically create). No caching exists at ANY layer: grep for CacheModule/cache-manager/CacheInterceptor across backend/src returned nothing; menu.module.ts imports only PrismaModule/PosSettingsModule/UploadModule with no cache provider; the only Cache-Control header in the backend is on metrics.controller.ts. The two @Public endpoints (qr-menu.controller.ts:28-86) carry only @Throttle (a rate-limit, not a cache — and per project memory the `default` throttler may even be inert, so rate-limiting offers no DB protection here either) and emit no Cache-Control. nginx ops/nginx/hummytummy.com.conf:44-50 proxies /api/ and /uploads/ straight to 127.0.0.1:3000 with no proxy_cache, no gzip, no expires/Cache-Control. Redis IS wired (redis-io.adapter.ts) but only for the socket.io adapter — not as a cache-manager store, so the finder's "Redis is already there" recommendation is valid.

No mitigation was missed. I checked main.ts/app.module.ts (no global cache interceptor), the menu module, the controller, the frontend SPA nginx (frontend/nginx.conf only sets no-cache on the SPA shell and immutable on hashed assets; it does not proxy /api), and the SPA fetch path.

Two nuances that pull severity DOWN from "high" to "medium": (1) The menu is fetched once per page load, not polled in-page — QRMenuContent.tsx receives categories as props and does NOT refetch; the only 3s setInterval is in OrderTrackingPage for order status, not the menu. So the finder's "every in-page refresh hits the DB" is slightly overstated; load scales with page loads/scans, not with an in-page timer. (2) The cited nginx conf carries an explicit banner (lines 4-13) stating it is a RECONSTRUCTED mirror of prod, not the captured live vhost — so we cannot be 100% certain prod has zero proxy_cache, though it is the repo's source of truth and shows none. This is also a pure capacity/optimization finding, not a correctness or security defect: nothing is currently broken for any user, and there is no evidence of present saturation. It is a real and reasonable hardening item (short-TTL per-(tenant,tableId) cache + nginx micro-cache + Cache-Control), but "high" overstates urgency for a system with no demonstrated load problem; medium is the honest rating.
findings.evidence: menu-query.service.ts:19-204: getPublicMenu issues tenant.findFirst + qrMenuSettings.findFirst + (optional) table.findFirst + posSettings.findByTenant + a category.findMany whose include nests products→productImages→image, products→modifierGroups→group→modifiers (6 levels). No CacheModule/cache-manager/Redis-cache exists anywhere (grep for CacheModule/cache-manager returned nothing; menu.module.ts:17-19 has no cache provider). nginx hummytummy.com.conf:44-50 proxies /api and /uploads straight to 127.0.0.1:3000 with NO proxy_cache, NO gzip, NO expires/Cache-Control. The two @Public endpoints (qr-menu.controller.ts:28-86) have no caching.
findings.why_it_matters: Menu content changes rarely but is read constantly. At a busy venue every table scan + every in-page refresh hits the DB with a deep join; across many tenants this is the first thing to saturate the single Postgres under real lunch/dinner load, and the 6-level include serializes a large JSON each time. A simple 30-60s per-(tenant,table) cache (Redis or even in-process) plus nginx micro-caching would cut this load by 1-2 orders of magnitude.
findings.recommendation: Add a short-TTL cache for getPublicMenu keyed by (tenantId, tableId) invalidated on menu/settings writes (Redis is already wired for the socket adapter), and/or nginx micro-cache the GET /api/qr-menu/* responses for ~30s; add Cache-Control to the public menu response so the SPA/browser caches it too.

findings.title: No HTTP response compression registered in the NestJS app
findings.verify_reasoning:
The literal technical claim is TRUE on main: no compression() middleware in backend/src/main.ts (read in full — only helmet, body-parser, cookie-parser, CORS, validation; no compression), `compression` is in NO package.json (grep across repo empty), and no `gzip on`/brotli directive exists in any version-controlled nginx conf (ops/nginx/*.conf, frontend/nginx.conf — hummytummy.com.conf line 10 only MENTIONS gzip in a comment). No vite compression plugin and no compression interceptor anywhere in backend src either. So at the application/repo level, nothing compresses responses.

BUT the finder missed the dominant mitigation: the deployment topology is Cloudflare -> host nginx -> containers, documented explicitly in docs/DEPLOYMENT.md ("behind Cloudflare", "The host nginx (Cloudflare -> nginx -> containers)") and ops/nginx/README.md ("The host nginx terminates Cloudflare's traffic"). Cloudflare in proxied mode automatically gzip/brotli-compresses compressible content types (application/json, text/*, JS, CSS) at its edge by default. The finder's entire "WHY IT MATTERS" thesis — mobile diners pulling uncompressed menu JSON over cellular, slow first paint, last-mile bandwidth — is the leg Cloudflare already compresses. The user-facing wire bytes (browser <-> Cloudflare) are therefore almost certainly already compressed, including the QR menu trees, report payloads, and static SPA JS/CSS.

What remains genuinely uncompressed is the ORIGIN hop (Cloudflare <-> VPS nginx <-> NestJS) — internal datacenter bandwidth, far lower impact than last-mile. Adding compression (nginx gzip or the compression middleware) is still a reasonable, cheap defense-in-depth (covers origin-hop bytes, direct-IP/non-CF access, and the case where CF auto-compression is ever disabled). Two extra caveats: (a) the repo nginx confs are explicitly NON-authoritative for prod ("Reconstructed — capture the live prod vhost before treating as authoritative"), so the repo cannot even prove prod's live nginx lacks gzip; (b) compression() at the Node layer behind a proxy adds CPU on the hot path with little user-visible benefit once CF already compresses, so nginx/CF-level is correct if adopted.

Net: real and worth doing as low-cost hardening, but the claimed user-impact (and thus "high") is substantially overstated because edge compression already covers the cited harm. Downgrade to low.
findings.evidence: main.ts has no `compression()` middleware (grep for compression/compress in main.ts returned nothing) and `compression` is not in package.json; nginx hummytummy.com.conf has no `gzip on`/brotli directive either. So menu JSON (deep nested category/product/modifier trees) and report payloads ship fully uncompressed end-to-end.
findings.why_it_matters: The QR menu and reports return large JSON; uncompressed these are several KB-to-MB over often-mobile cellular connections at the table. Gzip typically shrinks JSON 70-85%. Missing compression inflates bandwidth cost, slows first paint of the diner menu, and increases time-on-wire under load. This is the single cheapest large win.
findings.recommendation: Enable gzip (and ideally brotli) at nginx for application/json + text/* + JS/CSS, OR add the `compression` middleware in main.ts. nginx-level is preferred since it also covers static SPA assets.

findings.title: Per-endpoint throttle limits on the public ordering surface are silently inert (reference an unregistered `default` throttler)
findings.verify_reasoning:
The technical claim is mechanically TRUE on current main and there is no compensating mitigation anywhere.

(1) Registration: app.module.ts:82-98 registers ThrottlerModule.forRoot with exactly three named throttlers — short (1s/10), medium (10s/50), long (60s/100). There is NO throttler named "default" registered anywhere (only one forRoot call exists in src).

(2) Library semantics verified in source, not from memory: @nestjs/throttler v6.4.0 (node_modules/@nestjs/throttler/package.json). In dist/throttler.guard.js canActivate iterates `this.throttlers` (the three registered ones) and for each looks up per-handler override metadata under `THROTTLER_LIMIT + namedThrottler.name`. The @Throttle({ default: {...} }) decorator writes metadata keyed by the literal name "default"; since the loop never visits a throttler named "default", the lookup for short/medium/long finds no override and falls back to the GLOBAL limits. So every @Throttle({ default }) on these handlers is silently inert — the intended tighter caps are never applied; the loose globals apply instead.

(3) Cited endpoints all confirmed verbatim: qr-menu.controller.ts:29,66 (60/60s); customer-self-pay.controller.ts:30,41,80; customer-orders.controller.ts:56 order-create 3/60s @Public, :89 waiter-requests 5/60s, :109 bill-requests 5/60s — all @Public, all { default }. The author of catalog.controller.ts:82-93 demonstrably understood the v6 named-tier merge (correctly overrides existing `short`/`long`), and payments.controller.ts:35 uses { long: {...} } correctly — confirming { default } is the wrong key here.

(4) No mitigation elsewhere: the custom MachineThrottlerGuard (common/guards/machine-throttler.guard.ts, registered as APP_GUARD) only overrides getTracker (IP-keying); it does NOT change tier-name resolution. nginx configs (ops/nginx/*.conf, frontend/nginx.conf) contain NO limit_req/limit_conn zones, so there is no edge rate limit compensating for this.

(5) SCOPE IS UNDERSTATED, not overstated: the SAME root cause defeats the security-critical auth throttles — auth.controller.ts defines LOGIN_THROTTLE (5/60s), REGISTER_THROTTLE (3/hr), FORGOT_THROTTLE (5/60s), SOCIAL_THROTTLE (3/hr), CHANGE_PASSWORD, REFRESH all as { default: {...} } (lines 40-58) and superadmin-auth.controller.ts likewise. These are all inert too, so login/password-reset brute-force protection silently falls back to ~100/min global instead of 5/min. This brute-force amplification on credential endpoints supports a high rating.

Real-world nuance (why not critical): the paths are NOT entirely unthrottled — the global tiers still cap a single IP at roughly short=10/1s and long=100/60s. So the delta for order-create is ~3/min intended vs ~100/min actual (≈33x looser), and IP-keyed buckets are shared across a NAT'd venue. That is a genuine, exploitable weakening of abuse and brute-force protection on anonymous write + auth paths, but not a total absence of throttling — so "high" is the right level (not critical). The finder's mechanism, files, lines, and "stricter caps never apply" conclusion are all accurate; this matches the previously-deferred infra finding and is still present on current main.
findings.evidence: ThrottlerModule.forRoot registers throttlers named only short/medium/long (app.module.ts:82-98) — there is no `default`. But every public hot endpoint uses @Throttle({ default: {...} }): qr-menu.controller.ts:29,66 (60/60s); customer-self-pay.controller.ts:30,41 (30/60s, 5/60s); customer-orders.controller.ts:56,89,109 (3/60s, 5/60s) for order-create / waiter-request / self-pay confirm. In @nestjs/throttler v6.4.0 a @Throttle map only overrides a throttler with that exact name, so `{default}` overrides nothing and these handlers fall back to the generic global buckets — the tuned stricter per-endpoint limits never apply.
findings.why_it_matters: The intended tight caps that protect the most abusable anonymous, write-heavy paths (create order 3/min, self-pay confirm 5/min, waiter call 3/min) are not enforced; only the loose global short/medium/long apply (e.g. long=100/60s). Under a malicious or buggy client this allows far more order/payment-intent churn than designed against the same single backend + Postgres, and pollutes KDS/POS with spam orders. (Note: this matches a previously-deferred infra finding; verified still present on current files.)
findings.recommendation: Either register a throttler literally named `default` in forRoot, or change every @Throttle on these controllers to target an existing name (e.g. @Throttle({ short: {...} }) / a new named bucket). Add an e2e assertion that the public order-create returns 429 past its intended limit.

findings.title: Production runs a single backend container with no resource limits and an unbounded Prisma connection pool against default Postgres max_connections
findings.evidence: docker-compose.prod.yml:62-146 defines ONE `backend` (container_name kds_backend_prod, no deploy.replicas, no cpus/mem_limit). DATABASE_URL (line 90) carries no `?connection_limit=` so Prisma defaults the pool to num_cpus*2+1 per process; postgres is the stock `postgres:15-alpine` (line 15) with default max_connections=100. No PM2/cluster/WEB_CONCURRENCY in Dockerfile (CMD `node dist/main`, single process) or compose.
findings.why_it_matters: Single replica = a deploy/restart or a crash is a full outage, and one CPU-bound request (e.g. the QR-code or PDF generation) blocks all concurrent users on that lone event loop. The moment a second replica is added for HA/scale, the uncapped pool means N replicas can each open ~17+ connections and exhaust Postgres's 100-conn ceiling, causing 'too many connections' errors under load. No mem/cpu limit means a runaway request can OOM/starve the host (which also runs Postgres+Redis).
findings.recommendation: Pin `?connection_limit=` and `pool_timeout=` on DATABASE_URL sized to (replicas × limit < postgres max_connections, leaving headroom), bump postgres max_connections or add PgBouncer before scaling out, and add cpus/mem_limit to the backend service. Plan a 2-replica HA setup (Redis socket adapter + leader-elected crons already support it).

findings.title: Menu/product images are served from the Node app process with no Cache-Control and no CDN
findings.evidence: main.ts:133-139 serves /uploads via express.static behind createUploadsAclMiddleware; uploads-acl.middleware.ts sets no Cache-Control/maxAge/immutable headers (grep found none). nginx hummytummy.com.conf:48-50 proxies /uploads/ to 127.0.0.1:3000 (the app) with no expires/caching and no CDN in front. Images live on a docker volume (compose prod line 131 uploads_storage).
findings.why_it_matters: Every diner downloading a menu pulls product images THROUGH the Node event loop and the single app process, competing with API requests for CPU/sockets. Without Cache-Control, browsers re-fetch images on each visit. This both inflates origin load on the hottest path and degrades diner page-load on mobile. Images are immutable (re-encoded by sharp to content-addressable-ish names) so they are ideal CDN/long-cache candidates.
findings.recommendation: Serve /uploads via nginx directly from the volume (alias) with `expires 30d; add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable"` instead of proxying to Node, and/or front them with a CDN. At minimum set long Cache-Control on the static handler.

findings.title: Synchronous QR-code/PDF/image generation runs inline in request handlers and can block the single event loop
findings.evidence: qr.service.ts:178-220 loops up to 500 tables calling `await QRCode.toDataURL(...)` per table inside the request (comment at line 12-15 states each PNG is ~500ms synchronous CPU → up to ~250s of CPU for a 500-table chain on one request). z-report-pdf.service.ts and invoice-pdf.service.ts use pdfkit, upload.service.ts uses sharp().resize().toBuffer() (lines 107-113, 215) — all in-request. No BullMQ/queue exists for these (no queue lib in package.json).
findings.why_it_matters: On the single-process backend, a large QR-sheet or report generation monopolizes the event loop and stalls EVERY concurrent request (KDS, POS, diner menu) for the duration. sharp/QRCode use libuv threadpool partially but the data-URL serialization and pdfkit work is on the main thread. As tenant table-counts and concurrent report use grow this becomes a latency cliff for unrelated users. QR generation is admin-triggered (lower frequency) but still impactful; image upload is more frequent.
findings.recommendation: Move heavy generation (large QR sheets, PDFs, image re-encode for big files) to a background queue (BullMQ on the existing Redis) returning a job id + downloadable artifact, or at least stream/yield between QR codes and cap concurrency; for uploads, ensure UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE is tuned and consider offloading. Prioritize once a second replica isn't masking the single-process risk.

findings.title: Reports accept an unbounded date range — wide-window queries load every PAID order in the range into JS
findings.evidence: reports DTOs (reports/dto/*.dto.ts) validate startDate/endDate only as @IsDateString @IsOptional with no max-window constraint; reports.service.ts:168-194 findMany's all PAID orders in [start,end] and buckets daily in JS (getCustomerAnalytics line 429 similar). Endpoint is auth+role-gated (reports.controller.ts:28-33 ADMIN/MANAGER) so not anonymous, and only 2 fields are selected, but a large tenant requesting 'all time' pulls one row per order into memory.
findings.why_it_matters: A big established tenant pulling a multi-year report streams hundreds of thousands of rows into the Node process on the shared single backend, spiking memory and event-loop time and degrading concurrent users. Bounded by date and by auth, so lower severity, but it grows with tenant tenure and is a foreseeable slow-report/OOM source.
findings.recommendation: Cap the max report window (e.g. reject >1 year, or require pagination/streaming for large ranges) and/or push daily bucketing into SQL via groupBy on a date_trunc expression so the rows never leave Postgres.

summary: The project pins major frameworks at reasonable-but-aging majors (NestJS 10, Prisma 6, React 18, Next/Vite stacks), commits npm lockfiles for every JS package, and uses `npm ci` in the CI quality gates and the backend production Docker stage — good foundations. However, the supply chain is materially behind on security patches and has no automated guardrails: `npm audit --omit=dev` reports 3 critical + 55 high in the backend production tree and 1 critical + 6 high in the frontend, including CVEs squarely on hot, attacker-reachable paths (axios SSRF/proxy-credential-leak used for all server-side webhook/payment/delivery/e-Fatura calls; jws<3.2.3 HMAC-verify weakness in the JWT auth tree; multer DoS on the upload path; ws DoS on the realtime socket). There is NO SCA scanning, NO Dependabot/Renovate, and the desktop POS + on-prem bridge-agent Rust crates ship with `Cargo.lock` gitignored (uncommitted), so those security-sensitive binaries are built non-reproducibly with un-audited transitive crates. Additionally the backend image's `dist/` is compiled in a stage that runs `npm install` (not `npm ci`), so the shipped JS can drift from the committed lockfile. Before onboarding paying tenants at scale, bump the auth/payment/upload/socket-path packages, commit the Rust lockfiles, and add a blocking SCA gate.
strengths: JS lockfiles are committed and tracked for every package (backend/package-lock.json, frontend/package-lock.json, root, apps, desktop, developer, landing, help — confirmed via `git ls-files`), giving a reproducible base for the npm side.
CI quality gates install with `npm ci --no-audit --no-fund` (.github/workflows/quality-gates.yml:40,108,141) — integrity-checked, lockfile-faithful installs in the test/lint pipeline.

Backend production Docker stage uses `npm ci --only=production` (backend/Dockerfile:55) and drops to a non-root `USER node` (Dockerfile:74) — runtime deps are lockfile-pinned and the container is unprivileged.
Core framework majors are current-enough and consistent: @nestjs/* 10.3+, @prisma/client & prisma 6.19, class-validator 0.14, helmet 8 — no dangerously ancient framework majors.
Datastore base images are sensibly major-pinned with documented rationale (postgres:15-alpine, redis:7-alpine in docker-compose.prod.yml:15,44) rather than floating :latest.

Handlebars (a critical-CVE package) is used only to compile static .hbs files from disk (src/common/services/email.service.ts, src/modules/contact/mailer.service.ts compile from process.cwd()/templates, not tenant-supplied bodies), and default {{ }} escaping applies — sharply limiting real exposure of that particular advisory.
No risky/unexpected postinstall scripts in the app package.json files; native builds (prisma generate, sharp) are explicit and conventional.

findings.title: axios on the server-side outbound path is below the patched version (SSRF, proxy-credential leak, ReDoS)
findings.verify_reasoning:
The factual core is TRUE and accurately reported. Backend declares "axios": "^1.13.2" and node_modules/axios is exactly 1.13.2; frontend declares ^1.15.1 / resolves 1.15.1. `npm audit --omit=dev` does flag axios (range 1.0.0-1.15.2) as HIGH and the cited advisories are present verbatim: Proxy-Authorization Credential Leak across HTTP-to-HTTPS Redirect (GHSA-p92q-9vqr-4j8v), NO_PROXY/IPv4-mapped-IPv6 SSRF bypass (GHSA-pjwm-pj3p-43mv, GHSA-3p68-rc4w-qgx5), and ReDoS via Cookie Name Injection (GHSA-hfxv-24rg-xrqf). axios is genuinely the server-side outbound client for the listed modules (confirmed grep of `from 'axios'` across delivery-platforms/base.adapter, payments/paytr.adapter, accounting/foriba-efatura+logo+parasut adapters, outbox/marketing-event-relay, public-stats/geolocation, provisioning/http-referral-directory). A bump is warranted (latest patched is 1.18.1; npm reports fixAvailable: true / non-breaking, NOT >=1.16.0 specifically — minor correction to the recommendation).

WHERE THE FINDING IS OVERSTATED (why "partly", not "confirmed" at high): the WHY-IT-MATTERS exploit narrative does not hold against this codebase's actual usage, so real-world exploitability is low-to-moderate, not high:
1) Proxy-credential-leak CVEs require a configured proxy. There is ZERO proxy configuration anywhere — grep for proxy/HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/httpsAgent/httpAgent/Proxy-Auth across src returned nothing, and no adapter sets `proxy:` in axios config. With no proxy in play, there are no Proxy-Authorization credentials to leak. This is the headline CVE in the title and it is effectively inapplicable.
2) The SSRF/NO_PROXY-bypass CVEs are about NO_PROXY being bypassed to reach internal hosts — again predicated on a proxy + NO_PROXY setup that does not exist here. More importantly, all outbound calls target FIXED or env/config-driven provider hosts (PayTR, Foriba, Logo, Parasut, delivery platforms via baseURL from config, geolocation via the hardcoded `https://ip-api.com/json/${ip}` host). The only externally-influenced input (client IP) is interpolated into a PATH segment of a hardcoded host, not a host-controllable position, so it cannot redirect the request to an attacker host. No code sets custom maxRedirects to enable a redirect-to-internal pivot. The "crafted target reaches internal services" scenario has no entry point.
3) ReDoS via cookie-name injection requires attacker-controlled Set-Cookie names on responses; these are trusted payment/e-Fatura/provider endpoints, not attacker-run servers.

So: the dependency IS stale and audit-flagged HIGH (a legitimate hygiene/supply-chain finding worth fixing — and trivial since fixAvailable is non-breaking), but the specific high-impact exploitation theory (credential leak / SSRF to internal services) is not reachable given no proxy config and fixed/config-driven destination hosts. Corrected severity: medium (stale vulnerable dependency on a payment/secret-bearing outbound path = real, should be patched promptly, but not a demonstrable high-severity exploit in this deployment).
findings.evidence: backend/package.json declares "axios": "^1.13.2" and node_modules/axios is 1.13.2; frontend resolves 1.15.1. `npm audit --omit=dev` flags axios range 1.0.0-1.15.2 [high] for, among others, 'Proxy-Authorization Credential Leak ... across HTTP-to-HTTPS Redirect', NO_PROXY/IPv4-mapped-IPv6 SSRF bypass, and ReDoS via cookie-name injection. axios is the outbound HTTP client for 12 server modules incl. delivery-platforms/adapters/base.adapter.ts, payments/adapters/paytr.adapter.ts, accounting/adapters/foriba-efatura.adapter.ts & logo.adapter.ts, outbox/marketing-event-relay.service.ts, public-stats/geolocation.service.ts, provisioning/http-referral-directory.client.ts.
findings.why_it_matters: These are exactly the requests that carry payment/e-Fatura/webhook secrets to third parties and that may follow redirects to attacker-influenced hosts; the proxy-credential-leak and SSRF-bypass CVEs can leak provider credentials or let a crafted target reach internal services in a multi-tenant cloud deployment.
findings.recommendation: Bump axios to >=1.16.0 (latest patched) in both backend and frontend, re-run `npm ci`, and re-audit. Verify the delivery/payment adapters still pass their specs.

findings.title: No SCA scanning and no Dependabot/Renovate — CVEs are invisible to the pipeline
findings.verify_reasoning:
All three checkable claims are TRUE on current main, and no mitigation exists anywhere.

1) No SCA in CI: `grep -rlnE 'trivy|snyk|grype|npm audit|osv-scanner|dependency-review' .github/workflows/` returns nothing (exit 1). A broadened grep across the entire repo for trivy/snyk/grype/osv/dependency-review/audit-ci/--audit-level/aquasec found nothing either. The only "audit" hits are unrelated: a Prisma migration "audit dump" in db-baseline.yml and the literal `--no-audit` flag. quality-gates.yml is the sole quality gate and is workflow_call'ed by test-deploy.yml + release-deploy.yml, so it is the single chokepoint — and it has no SCA step. CI installs confirmed at quality-gates.yml:40, :108, :141 all `npm ci --no-audit --no-fund`, i.e. audit is explicitly suppressed.

2) No Dependabot/Renovate: no .github/dependabot.yml, renovate.json, .renovaterc, or .renovaterc.json at repo root (only matches are inside vendored node_modules of third-party packages — not project config).

3) The vulnerability numbers are essentially exact. I ran `npm audit --omit=dev` myself: backend prod tree = 3 critical + 55 high (+41 moderate, total 100); frontend = 1 critical + 6 high (+7 moderate, total 14). Matches the finder's "backend 3 critical + 55 high, frontend 1 critical + 6 high" precisely.

Severity assessment: HIGH is justified. This is a structural pipeline gap (no automated CVE visibility) in front of a known-vulnerable prod tree that includes genuinely prod-reachable, security-relevant advisories — not just dev noise: axios SSRF (NO_PROXY bypass), jws improperly verifies HMAC signature (auth/token path), express-rate-limit IPv4-mapped-IPv6 bypass, socket.io-parser unbounded attachments, ws uninitialized-memory disclosure, multer DoS, path-to-regexp ReDoS, validator isURL bypass, form-data CRLF injection, nodemailer unintended-domain. Real direct deps of a payment/auth/realtime SaaS.

One nuance that keeps it at HIGH rather than higher: the raw "55 high" is inflated by fan-out — ~40 of the highs are the mjml family rooted in one/two mjml/html-minifier advisories pulled via the mail/templating dependency, so the headline count overstates distinct issues. But even discounting mjml, the residual direct-dependency high/critical set (axios, jws, express-rate-limit, ws, socket.io-parser, multer, path-to-regexp, lodash, validator, fast-xml-parser, handlebars, liquidjs) is substantial and prod-reachable. The finder's core thesis — zero automation means new CVEs in auth/payment paths ship unflagged — is sound. This compounds the repo's already-flagged public/leaked-secrets exposure in MEMORY. HIGH (not critical) because it is a missing-control/visibility gap rather than a confirmed live exploit, and remediation is a straightforward CI audit job + Dependabot config.
findings.evidence: `grep -rlnE 'trivy|snyk|grype|npm audit|osv-scanner|dependency-review' .github/workflows/` returns nothing; CI installs use `npm ci --no-audit` (quality-gates.yml:40,108,141). No .github/dependabot.yml, renovate.json, or .renovaterc exists. Yet `npm audit --omit=dev` currently reports backend 3 critical + 55 high and frontend 1 critical + 6 high in the PRODUCTION tree.
findings.why_it_matters: With zero automation, the 100+ advisories accumulate silently and new CVEs in the auth/payment path will never be flagged before they ship to paying tenants. This is the single biggest structural gap — every other finding here is a symptom of it.
findings.recommendation: Add a blocking CI job running `npm audit --omit=dev --audit-level=high` (or Trivy/osv-scanner with a vetted allowlist) on backend+frontend, and enable Dependabot/Renovate with grouped weekly PRs. Triage-allowlist transitive-only/dev-only advisories so the gate stays meaningful.

findings.title: Rust Cargo.lock is gitignored — desktop POS & on-prem bridge-agent build non-reproducibly with un-audited crates
findings.verify_reasoning:
Every technical claim is true on current main. (1) .gitignore line 69 contains a bare `Cargo.lock` under the "# Rust/Tauri" section; `git check-ignore -v` confirms it ignores all four binary crates' lockfiles (e.g. apps/local-bridge-agent/Cargo.lock, desktop/src-tauri/Cargo.lock). (2) `git ls-files | grep Cargo.lock` returns nothing — zero lockfiles tracked. (3) Lockfiles exist locally/untracked for 3 of 4 crates (kds-kiosk has none on disk at all, so it has never even been resolved into a committed lock). (4) CI builds non-reproducibly: bridge-agent.yml:73 is bare `cargo build --release` (also fmt/clippy/test at lines 58/64/67) with NO `--locked`; the cargo cache key (line 55) hashes Cargo.toml, not Cargo.lock, further confirming the lock is irrelevant to the build. desktop-release.yml:235 runs `npm run tauri build` with no `--locked` either. (5) No cargo-audit, cargo-deny, deny.toml, or audit.toml exists anywhere in the repo (verified via grep + find). So there is genuinely zero supply-chain scanning on the Rust side and no lock pinning.

No mitigation lives elsewhere — I checked the gitignore context, both Rust workflows, and searched for any deny/audit config. None of the crates pin via git/path either (only the normal lib/bin path entries), so resolution is fully semver-floating against crates.io.

Severity is justified at high, with honest nuance keeping it from blocker/critical: these are security-sensitive distributables — the bridge-agent authenticates to the on-prem device mesh and the desktop binary handles POS/printing on tenant premises, and they ship as signed updater bundles (Tauri auto-update). A yanked or semver-compatible-malicious transitive crate would enter silently on the next CI build with no scan and no reproducibility. The dependency surface is real (~18-33 direct deps per crate plus transitive). It is NOT a currently-exploitable runtime vuln (no known-bad crate is present today; this is a latent supply-chain/reproducibility exposure), which is why it sits at high rather than critical. The recommendation (commit lockfiles, build with --locked, add cargo-audit/cargo-deny) is correct and standard. One small refinement: kds-kiosk has no lockfile on disk to commit yet — a `cargo generate-lockfile` is needed there first.
findings.evidence: .gitignore:69 ignores `Cargo.lock`; `git ls-files | grep Cargo.lock` returns nothing, yet 4 crates exist (apps/local-bridge-agent/Cargo.toml, desktop/src-tauri/Cargo.toml, apps/kds-kiosk/src-tauri/Cargo.toml, frontend/src-tauri/Cargo.toml) and Cargo.lock files exist locally (e.g. apps/local-bridge-agent/Cargo.lock on disk, untracked). CI builds with `cargo build --release` (bridge-agent.yml:73) with no `--locked` and no cargo-audit/cargo-deny step (none found in desktop-release.yml or bridge-agent.yml).
findings.why_it_matters: The bridge-agent authenticates to the device mesh and the desktop binary handles POS/printing on tenant premises — these are security-sensitive distributables. Without a committed lockfile, every CI build resolves transitive crates fresh (a yanked/compromised semver-compatible crate enters silently), builds are not reproducible, and there is no vulnerability scan on the Rust side at all.
findings.recommendation: Remove `Cargo.lock` from .gitignore for the binary crates and commit the lockfiles; build with `cargo build --locked`; add a `cargo audit`/`cargo-deny` CI step to bridge-agent.yml and desktop-release.yml.

findings.title: Backend production artifact (dist/) is compiled with `npm install`, not `npm ci` — can drift from the committed lockfile
findings.evidence: backend/Dockerfile builds the shipped JS in the `development` stage: line 20 `RUN npm install` then line 28 `RUN npm run build`; the production stage then COPYs `--from=development /app/dist` (Dockerfile:57). Only the production stage's runtime node_modules uses `npm ci --only=production` (line 55). `npm install` resolves `^` ranges fresh and may mutate the lockfile in-image, so the compiled/bundled code can embed dependency versions different from package-lock.json.
findings.why_it_matters: Reproducibility and integrity of the actual shipped artifact are not guaranteed: two builds of the same tag can compile against different transitive code, and a poisoned compatible release could be baked into dist without the lockfile gate catching it. Undermines the value of committing the lockfile.
findings.recommendation: Change the build stage to `npm ci` (full install incl. devDeps) so the compiled artifact is lockfile-faithful and reproducible.

findings.title: jws<3.2.3 (HMAC signature verification) sits in the JWT authentication dependency tree
findings.evidence: `npm ls jws --omit=dev` shows jws@3.2.2 pulled by @nestjs/jwt@10.2.0 -> jsonwebtoken@9.0.2; jsonwebtoken/package.json declares "jws": "^3.2.2". `npm audit` flags jws <3.2.3 [high] GHSA-869p-cjfg-cm3x 'Improperly Verifies HMAC Signature'. @nestjs/jwt is the core auth token signer/verifier; passport-jwt and apple-signin-auth dedupe onto the same jsonwebtoken@9.0.2.
findings.why_it_matters: Anything in the JWT verification path warrants a higher bar for a multi-tenant SaaS where a forged/confused token = cross-tenant account takeover. jsonwebtoken@9 added its own algorithm hardening so practical exploitability is reduced, but running a known-flagged jws under the auth library is an avoidable risk that an auditor/pen-test will (rightly) raise.
findings.recommendation: Upgrade @nestjs/jwt/jsonwebtoken to a release that resolves jws>=3.2.3 (or add an npm override pinning jws>=4.0.0 under jsonwebtoken) and re-run the auth specs.

findings.title: multer and ws on the upload/realtime paths carry DoS CVEs
findings.evidence: `npm audit --omit=dev`: multer <=2.1.1 [high] (multiple 'Denial of Service' advisories) — backend declares "multer":"^2.0.2" directly and via @nestjs/platform-express@10.4.20, on the file-upload path. ws 8.0.0-8.20.1 [high] ('Memory exhaustion DoS from tiny fragments') resolves to ws@8.17.1 under socket.io@4.8.1/engine.io and @socket.io/redis-adapter — the realtime KDS/POS socket layer. express@4.21.2 (via @nestjs/platform-express) is within the flagged <=4.22.1 range.
findings.why_it_matters: Both are remotely reachable by unauthenticated or low-privilege clients (file uploads, websocket connections) and the CVEs are resource-exhaustion DoS — a single malicious tenant or scanner could degrade the shared multi-tenant backend. The realtime socket DoS is especially impactful since KDS/POS depend on it.
findings.recommendation: Upgrade @nestjs/platform-express (pulls patched multer + express) and bump socket.io/engine.io to releases resolving ws>=8.21; verify upload and socket integration tests.

findings.title: @nestjs-modules/mailer pulls a large chain of high/critical-CVE packages (mjml/liquidjs/handlebars/lodash/html-minifier) into the prod tree
findings.evidence: `npm ls` traces the bulk of backend advisories to @nestjs-modules/mailer@2.0.2: mjml@4.16.1 (+ ~35 mjml-* subpackages all flagged), liquidjs@10.22.0 [critical: RCE/path-traversal/DoS], handlebars@4.7.8 [critical], lodash@4.17.21 [high], js-cookie/picomatch/html-minifier [high], plus preview-email/mailparser. The app itself compiles templates with the standalone `handlebars` package from static .hbs files (email.service.ts, contact/mailer.service.ts), and renders no tenant-controlled mjml/liquid templates.
findings.why_it_matters: These are real critical CVEs in the production node_modules, but the dangerous codepaths (liquidjs RCE, mjml directory traversal, handlebars AST injection) are not reached with attacker-controlled input in this codebase, so practical exposure is low. The risk is inventory noise that buries genuinely-reachable advisories and the maintenance drag of a heavy unused-feature dependency.
findings.recommendation: Upgrade @nestjs-modules/mailer to its latest (newer mjml/liquidjs fix most of these) or, since templates are rendered via the standalone handlebars + nodemailer directly, evaluate dropping @nestjs-modules/mailer entirely to shed the mjml/liquidjs/preview-email subtree.

findings.title: Base container images are tag-pinned but not digest-pinned; mixed node majors (18 vs 20); no engines field
findings.evidence: backend/Dockerfile uses `FROM node:20` (no -alpine, no @sha256 digest); frontend/Dockerfile uses `FROM node:18`/`node:18-alpine`/`nginx:alpine` while developer/landing/help use node:20-alpine — `grep '@sha256' Dockerfile` finds none. Neither backend nor frontend package.json declares an `engines` field. node:18 is approaching/at EOL.
findings.why_it_matters: Floating `node:20`/`node:18`/`nginx:alpine` tags mean OS-package CVEs and even the node runtime can change under the same image tag between builds (non-reproducible base, possible regressions), and the EOL-track node:18 frontend builder stops receiving security patches. Lack of digest pinning also weakens supply-chain integrity of the base layer.
findings.recommendation: Pin base images by digest (or at minimum align all builders on node:20-alpine and drop node:18), add an `engines.node` constraint, and let the SCA/Trivy job scan the base layers for OS CVEs.
